topics.title: Overview of Welding Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Welding Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Welding (KTU 2024 Definition):** Welding is a **manufacturing fabrication process** that employs **heat, pressure, or both** to create a permanent, metallurgical bond between two or more workpieces (similar or dissimilar materials) by causing localized coalescence (fusion) of the base material, often supplemented by a filler material.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the **American Welding Society (AWS)**, welding is defined as *"a localized joining of two or more materials, usually metals or thermoplastics, by coalescence of the interface, typically achieved by melting the workpieces with or without filler metal, with or without the application of pressure."*

The key terminological elements a KTU examiner expects:
- **Coalescence** — The actual atomic-level merging/growing together of grain structures across the interface.
- **Base Metal (Parent Metal)** — The material being joined.
- **Filler Metal** — Additional consumable material added to the weld pool.
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — The region of base metal that has not melted but whose microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the welding heat.
- **Fusion Zone (FZ)** — The actual melted region that solidifies to form the joint.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Candle Wax Joint"**
> Imagine melting two candle ends near a flame. As the wax softens and flows, the two ends blend into one continuous mass. When cooled, the boundary between them **disappears** — they are no longer two candles, but one. Welding is essentially this principle, but engineered with extreme precision, controlled heat input (often 5,000–20,000°C at the arc), and sometimes inert shielding to prevent contamination.
>
> **Industrial Analogy — "The Robotic Tailor":** Think of a welding robot as a **robotic tailor's needle** that, instead of thread, uses a continuous arc of superheated plasma to stitch metals together seamlessly along a seam.

### 1.3 Why Welding Matters in Modern Engineering

- **Structural Integrity:** Welded joints in bridges, pressure vessels, ship hulls, and aircraft must sustain **cyclic, tensile, and fatigue loads** for decades.
- **Material Versatility:** From **carbon steel** to **titanium alloys**, **stainless steel**, and **dissimilar metal combinations**.
- **Cost-Effectiveness:** For high-volume production (e.g., automotive), welding is far more economical than riveting or bolting.
- **Foundation for Robotics:** Robotic welding is the **largest single application of industrial robots globally** (~30% of all robot installations as per IFR 2023 data).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (Bolded)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Arc temperature (GTAW) | **8,000–20,000 °C** |
| Heat input range (industrial) | **0.5 – 5.0 kJ/mm** |
| Typical welding current range | **50 – 500 A** |
| Arc voltage range | **10 – 35 V** |
| Travel speed (industrial) | **100 – 1500 mm/min** |
| Shielding gas (Argon purity) | **99.99% (4N)** |
| Melting point of mild steel | **~1500 °C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Welding Processes (Hierarchical)

Welding processes are broadly classified into **three major families** based on the energy source and state of the base metal:

#### A. Fusion Welding (Base metal is melted)
1. **Arc Welding Processes**
   - **SMAW** (Shielded Metal Arc Welding) — Stick electrode, manual
   - **GMAW / MIG** (Gas Metal Arc Welding) — Wire + shielding gas
   - **GTAW / TIG** (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding) — Non-consumable tungsten electrode
   - **FCAW** (Flux-Cored Arc Welding) — Tubular electrode filled with flux
   - **SAW** (Submerged Arc Welding) — Granular flux bath
   - **PAW** (Plasma Arc Welding) — Constricted plasma jet
2. **High-Energy Beam Welding**
   - **LBW** (Laser Beam Welding)
   - **EBW** (Electron Beam Welding)
3. **Oxy-Fuel Gas Welding**
   - Oxy-Acetylene, Oxy-Hydrogen

#### B. Solid-State Welding (No melting; bonding below melting point)
- Friction Stir Welding (FSW)
- Ultrasonic Welding (USW)
- Diffusion Bonding
- Forge Welding
- Cold Pressure Welding

#### C. Resistance Welding (Heat from resistance to electric current)
- Spot Welding
- Seam Welding
- Projection Welding

### 2.2 The Core Physics — How a Weld Forms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Sequential Stages of Weld Formation:**
> 1. **Heat Generation** — Electrical/chemical/kinetic energy is converted to thermal energy at the joint interface.
> 2. **Base Metal Melting & Filler Metal Deposition** — A molten weld pool is established.
> 3. **Metallurgical Bonding** — Atoms from both surfaces interdiffuse; grain boundaries cross the interface (coalescence).
> 4. **Solidification** — The pool cools; grains grow epitaxially (continuing the base-metal grain structure).
> 5. **Cooling & Microstructure Formation** — Final mechanical properties (hardness, toughness) develop.

### 2.3 The Governing Heat Input Equation (THE most important formula in robotic welding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input (H)** is the **amount of energy supplied per unit length of weld**. It is the single most critical parameter in determining weld quality, distortion, and HAZ width.

**General Form:**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}
$$

where the units and parameters are as follows (and are commonly tested in KTU numerical problems):

- $\eta$ = **arc efficiency coefficient** (dimensionless, $0 < \eta < 1$)
- $V$ = **arc voltage** in Volts (V)
- $I$ = **welding current** in Amperes (A)
- $S$ = **travel speed** in mm/min
- $H$ = heat input in **kJ/mm**

**Alternative form (when $S$ is in mm/s):**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S} = \frac{0.06 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** formula set for Module 1. Master these before the ESE.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Application / Interpretation | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{1000\,S}$ | Heat input (governs HAZ width, distortion) | kJ / mm |
| 2 | $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power delivered to the arc | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $E_{line} = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S}$ | Linear energy (variant of H, when $S$ in mm/s) | J / mm |
| 4 | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}} \times 100$ | Determines power source rating | percent |
| 5 | $\text{Efficiency}\,(\eta)$: SMAW $\approx 0.7$, GMAW $\approx 0.8$, GTAW $\approx 0.5$, SAW $\approx 0.9$ | Process-specific heat utilization | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Cooling Rate}\,t_{8/5}$ = Time between 800 °C and 500 °C | Critical for HAZ microstructure | seconds |
| 7 | $\text{Deposition Rate} = \dfrac{I \cdot \rho_{wire}}{A_{wire}} \cdot \text{melt-rate coeff.}$ | Filler metal mass deposited per minute | g / min |
| 8 | $\text{Penetration Depth} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{H}{k\rho c_p}}$ | Rosenthal-type 2D heat-flow estimate | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes in Tables:** Note the LaTeX delimiters used (e.g., `\vert`, `\mid`) — never type the bare pipe character `\|` inside markdown table cells, as it will break the table parser.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Heat input must be precisely controlled (typically 0.8–1.2 kJ/mm for thin-gauge GMAW) to prevent burn-through on 0.8–2 mm sheets.
- **Pressure Vessel Fabrication (ASME Section IX):** Mandatory prequalified heat-input limits (e.g., 2.5 kJ/mm for P-No. 1 steels).
- **Shipbuilding & Offshore:** SAW with multi-wire systems achieves heat inputs of **5 kJ/mm or higher** for thick plates.
- **Aerospace (TIG of Titanium):** Strict low heat input (< 0.6 kJ/mm) to preserve fatigue life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input from First Principles (Electrical → Thermal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the heat input equation $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{1000 S}$ rigorously, starting from basic electrical power.

#### Step 1: Start with the electrical power delivered to the arc

The instantaneous electrical power in any DC circuit is the product of voltage and current:

$$
P_{electrical} = V \cdot I
$$

where the unit is **Watts (W)** when $V$ is in **Volts** and $I$ is in **Amperes**.

#### Step 2: Convert electrical power to thermal (heat) power using arc efficiency

Not all electrical energy becomes useful heat at the weld pool; some is lost as spatter, radiation, and convection. The **arc efficiency factor** $\eta$ accounts for this:

$$
P_{thermal} = \eta \cdot P_{electrical} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

The unit remains **Watts**, but $\eta$ is dimensionless ($0 < \eta < 1$).

#### Step 3: Calculate the total energy supplied over the time spent welding 1 mm of length

If the torch travels at a constant speed $S$ (in **mm/min**), the time to traverse **1 mm** of seam is:

$$
t_{1mm} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{S \text{ mm/min}} = \frac{1}{S} \text{ min}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute the heat energy delivered per mm of weld

Energy is power multiplied by time:

$$
E_{per\,mm} = P_{thermal} \cdot t_{1mm} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{1}{S} \quad \text{(units: W·min/mm)}
$$

Since **1 W·min = 60 J**, convert:

$$
E_{per\,mm} = 60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{1}{S} \quad \text{(units: J/mm)}
$$

#### Step 5: Convert J/mm to the engineering-standard kJ/mm

Divide by 1000 to express in **kilojoules per millimeter**:

$$
H = \frac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S} = \frac{0.06 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$

with $H$ in **kJ/mm**, $V$ in V, $I$ in A, and $S$ in **mm/s** (since we converted min → s).

#### Step 6: Equivalent form when $S$ is given in mm/min

Starting from Step 4 directly (no time conversion):

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S} \quad \text{with S in mm/min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both forms are **examination-valid**; always check the units of $S$ in the problem statement.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Problem:** A robotic GMAW station operates at $V = 28$ V, $I = 220$ A, with travel speed $S = 600$ mm/min. The arc efficiency is $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate:
(i) Arc power, (ii) Heat input in kJ/mm.

**Step 1 — Arc power:**

$$
P_{arc} = V \cdot I = 28 \cdot 220 = 6160 \text{ W} = 6.16 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat input:**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S} = \frac{0.85 \cdot 28 \cdot 220}{1000 \cdot 600}
$$

Numerator first:

$$
0.85 \cdot 28 = 23.8
$$

$$
23.8 \cdot 220 = 5236
$$

Denominator:

$$
1000 \cdot 600 = 600000
$$

Final:

$$
H = \frac{5236}{600000} = 0.008727 \text{ kJ/mm} \approx 0.0087 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

Or equivalently, in J/mm:

$$
H = 8.73 \text{ J/mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** Typical GMAW heat input ranges from **0.5–1.5 J/mm** for thin sections to **~10–15 J/mm** for thick plate. Our value 8.73 J/mm is consistent with a medium-thickness structural weld — a plausible industrial value.

### 3.3 Derivation: Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) Width Estimate (Simplified Rosenthal 2-D Plate Model)

For a moving point heat source on a thick plate, the peak temperature at a distance $y$ from the weld centerline can be approximated by:

$$
\frac{1}{T_p - T_0} = \frac{4 \pi k \rho c_p \, y \, S}{Q} + \frac{1}{T_m - T_0}
$$

where:

- $T_p$ = peak temperature at distance $y$ (°C)
- $T_0$ = preheat/interpass temperature (°C)
- $T_m$ = melting point (°C)
- $Q$ = net heat input rate (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $y$ = perpendicular distance from weld centerline (m)
- $S$ = travel speed (m/s)

**Rearranging for the HAZ width** (set $T_p = T_m$ for the fusion boundary, $T_p = 723$ °C for HAZ boundary in low-alloy steels):

$$
y_{HAZ} = \frac{Q}{4 \pi k \rho c_p \, S} \left( \frac{1}{T_{HAZ} - T_0} - \frac{1}{T_m - T_0} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation demonstrates that **HAZ width increases with higher heat input** and **decreases with higher travel speed** — a critical concept when selecting robotic welding parameters for heat-sensitive alloys.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Robotic Welding Workcell

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interactions** in a typical robotic GMAW workcell — the foundational layout for the rest of this course.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Power Source Inverter 400A GMAW]:::src
    A2[Wire Feeder Unit Push Pull Type]:::src
    A3[Shielding Gas Cylinder Argon CO2 Mix]:::src
    B1[Six Axis Articulated Robot Arm 10kg Payload]:::robot
    B2[Robot Controller Teach Pendant]:::ctrl
    B3[Welding Torch Mounted On End Effector]:::tool
    C1[Rotary Positioner Two Axis Table]:::pos
    C2[Fixture and Clamping Jig]:::fix
    C3[Workpiece Steel Plate T Joint]:::work
    D1[Arc Sensing and Seam Tracking Sensor]:::sens
    D2[Weld Pool Camera or Through Arc Sensing]:::sens
    E1[PLC and Safety Interlock Panel]:::safety
    E2[Extraction and Fume Hood]:::safety

    A1 -- DC Cable --> B3
    A2 -- Liner Cable --> B3
    A3 -- Gas Hose --> B3
    B1 -- Mechanical Coupling --> B3
    B2 -- Ethernet Bus --> B1
    B1 -- Tool Flange --> C1
    C1 -- Mechanical Clamp --> C2
    C2 -- Holds --> C3
    D1 -- Real Time Feedback --> B2
    D2 -- Real Time Feedback --> B2
    E1 -- Emergency Stop --> B1
    E1 -- Light Curtain --> B1
    E2 -- Extracts Fumes --> C3

    classDef src fill:#FFE7B0,stroke:#A07000,color:#000
    classDef robot fill:#C9E3FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#D9CCFF,stroke:#2C0A6B,color:#000
    classDef tool fill:#FFD0D0,stroke:#800000,color:#000
    classDef pos fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#0B5D2E,color:#000
    classDef fix fill:#EDEDED,stroke:#444,color:#000
    classDef work fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#FFE0F0,stroke:#A02050,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Energy and material flow from the top (source side) downward to the workpiece. The right-side blocks (sensors, safety) provide **closed-loop control** — this is the defining feature that separates *robotic welding* from *automated mechanized welding*.

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification Flow — Welding Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0[WELDING PROCESSES]:::root
    R1[FUSION WELDING]:::fusion
    R2[SOLID STATE WELDING]:::solid
    R3[RESISTANCE WELDING]:::resist

    S1[Arc Welding]:::fusion
    S2[High Energy Beam]:::fusion
    S3[Oxy Fuel Gas]:::fusion
    S4[Friction Stir Welding]:::solid
    S5[Ultrasonic Welding]:::solid
    S6[Diffusion Bonding]:::solid
    S7[Spot Welding]:::resist
    S8[Seam Welding]:::resist
    S9[Projection Welding]:::resist

    T1[SMAW Stick]:::fusion
    T2[GMAW MIG Mag]:::fusion
    T3[GTAW TIG]:::fusion
    T4[FCAW Flux Cored]:::fusion
    T5[SAW Submerged Arc]:::fusion
    T6[Laser Beam LBW]:::fusion
    T7[Electron Beam EBW]:::fusion
    T8[Oxy Acetylene]:::fusion

    R0 --> R1
    R0 --> R2
    R0 --> R3
    R1 --> S1
    R1 --> S2
    R1 --> S3
    S1 --> T1
    S1 --> T2
    S1 --> T3
    S1 --> T4
    S1 --> T5
    S2 --> T6
    S2 --> T7
    S3 --> T8
    R2 --> S4
    R2 --> S5
    R2 --> S6
    R3 --> S7
    R3 --> S8
    R3 --> S9

    classDef root fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef fusion fill:#FFE7B0,stroke:#A07000,color:#000
    classDef solid fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#0B5D2E,color:#000
    classDef resist fill:#C9E3FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Robotic Welding Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Programmed Weld Path G Code Or Teach Points]:::prog
    P2[Robot Motion Controller]:::ctrl
    P3[Welding Power Source]:::src
    P4[Torch Position And Velocity]:::tool
    P5[Weld Pool And Arc]:::work
    P6[Sensor Module Voltage Current Or Vision]:::sens
    P7[Feedback To Controller Adaptive Logic]:::ctrl
    P8[Quality Log And Output]:::log

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P2
    P5 --> P8

    classDef prog fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#D9CCFF,stroke:#2C0A6B,color:#000
    classDef src fill:#FFE7B0,stroke:#A07000,color:#000
    classDef tool fill:#FFD0D0,stroke:#800000,color:#000
    classDef work fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#FFE0F0,stroke:#A02050,color:#000
    classDef log fill:#EDEDED,stroke:#444,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The closed loop from `Sensor Module` → `Feedback To Controller` → back to `Robot Motion Controller` and `Welding Power Source` is what makes a *robotic* welding system **adaptive** (vs. open-loop *mechanized* welding). This is a frequently tested KTU conceptual point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Module 1 typically contributes **15–20% of Part A** (3-mark questions) and **one full 14-mark Part B question** in the End Semester Examination. Internal Choice is mandatory in Part B.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define welding. Differentiate between fusion welding and solid-state welding with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Welding is a permanent joining process that produces coalescence of materials (usually metals) by heating to a suitable temperature with or without pressure and filler material.
- **Fusion welding (1 mark):** Base metal is melted at the joint interface. *Examples:* GMAW (MIG), GTAW (TIG), SMAW.
- **Solid-state welding (1 mark):** Joining is achieved at temperatures **below the melting point** of the base metals, primarily by pressure and diffusion. *Examples:* Friction Stir Welding, Ultrasonic Welding, Diffusion Bonding.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is meant by heat input in welding? State the formula and explain the significance of arc efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Heat input definition (1 mark):** Heat input is the **amount of thermal energy supplied per unit length of weld** during the welding process; it directly governs HAZ width, weld bead geometry, and residual distortion.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}$ kJ/mm, where $S$ is in mm/min.
- **Significance of $\eta$ (1 mark):** Arc efficiency $\eta$ accounts for the fact that only a fraction of the electrical energy is converted into useful heat at the weld pool (the rest is lost as radiation, spatter, convection). Typical values: SMAW $\approx$ 0.7, GMAW $\approx$ 0.8, SAW $\approx$ 0.9.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1]`
> **(a)** Classify the major welding processes with a neat sketch/diagram. Explain the working principle of **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)** and list its **four modes of metal transfer**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robotic MIG welding cell operates at $V = 30$ V, $I = 250$ A, and travel speed $S = 8$ mm/s. The arc efficiency is $\eta = 0.82$.
>   (i) Calculate the **heat input** in kJ/mm.
>   (ii) If the travel speed is increased to $S = 12$ mm/s while $V$ and $I$ are kept constant, find the **new heat input** and comment on its effect on the **HAZ width**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification + GMAW (7 marks):**
- Hierarchical classification (2 marks): Draw the tree — Fusion Welding → Arc / Beam / Oxy-Fuel; Solid-State; Resistance. (See Section 4.2 diagram.)
- GMAW working principle (3 marks): A **continuous solid wire electrode** is fed automatically through a gun; an **electric arc** is struck between the wire and the workpiece; the arc melts both the wire and the base metal; a shielding gas (Ar/CO₂ mix) protects the molten pool from atmospheric contamination.
- Four modes of metal transfer (2 marks): **Short-circuit transfer (dip transfer), Globular transfer, Spray transfer (axial spray), Pulsed spray transfer.**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Heat input at $S = 8$ mm/s:**

$$
H_1 = \frac{0.06 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} = \frac{0.06 \cdot 0.82 \cdot 30 \cdot 250}{8}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
0.06 \cdot 0.82 = 0.0492
$$

$$
0.0492 \cdot 30 = 1.476
$$

$$
1.476 \cdot 250 = 369
$$

$$
H_1 = \frac{369}{8} = 46.125 \text{ kJ/mm} = 0.0461 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_1 \approx 0.046 \text{ kJ/mm} \;(\text{or } 46.1 \text{ J/mm})}
$$

**(ii) Heat input at $S = 12$ mm/s:**

$$
H_2 = \frac{0.06 \cdot 0.82 \cdot 30 \cdot 250}{12} = \frac{369}{12} = 30.75 \text{ J/mm} = 0.0308 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_2 \approx 0.031 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

**Effect on HAZ width (qualitative discussion, 2 marks):**
- Increasing travel speed from 8 to 12 mm/s **reduces heat input** from 46.1 J/mm to 30.8 J/mm (≈ 33% reduction).
- **HAZ width is directly proportional to heat input** (from Rosenthal's model, $y_{HAZ} \propto Q/S$, where $Q = \eta V I$).
- Therefore, the **HAZ becomes narrower** → improved grain refinement → higher toughness in the heat-affected zone, but with risk of **lack of penetration** if the speed is excessive.

---

> **Q3B (Alternative Internal Choice).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1]`
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **five stages of weld formation** with a neat schematic of the weld cross-section. Clearly mark the **Fusion Zone (FZ), Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ), and Unaffected Base Metal**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a robotic GMAW cell, the wire feed speed is $W = 8$ m/min, wire diameter $d = 1.2$ mm, and wire density $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³. Calculate:
>   (i) **Deposition rate** in kg/h.
>   (ii) If the same setup is used for a fillet weld of leg length 8 mm and length 1.2 m, estimate the **total deposition time** assuming a 90% transfer efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five stages of weld formation (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Heat generation** — energy input from arc, beam, or friction.
- (1 mark) **Base metal melting & filler metal deposition** — weld pool formation.
- (1 mark) **Metallurgical bonding (coalescence)** — atomic interdiffusion across interface.
- (1 mark) **Solidification** — grain nucleation and epitaxial growth.
- (1 mark) **Cooling & microstructure development** — HAZ transformation, residual stress build-up.
- (2 marks) **Cross-section schematic:** Must clearly label FZ (central melted region), HAZ (adjacent thermally-altered region with no melting), and unaffected base metal. Width order: FZ < HAZ << base metal.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Deposition rate:**

Cross-sectional area of the wire:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (1.2)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.44}{4} = 1.131 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.01131 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Volume of wire fed per minute:

$$
V_{min} = A \cdot W = 0.01131 \text{ cm}^2 \cdot 800 \text{ cm/min} = 9.048 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

Mass per minute:

$$
m_{min} = V_{min} \cdot \rho = 9.048 \cdot 7.85 = 71.03 \text{ g/min}
$$

Per hour:

$$
m_{hr} = 71.03 \cdot 60 = 4261.7 \text{ g/h} \approx 4.26 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Deposition rate} \approx 4.26 \text{ kg/h}}
$$

**(ii) Total deposition time:**

Fillet weld cross-sectional area (right triangle, leg = 8 mm):

$$
A_{fillet} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 8 \cdot 8 = 32 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.32 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Volume of fillet weld:

$$
V_{weld} = 0.32 \text{ cm}^2 \cdot 1200 \text{ mm} = 0.32 \text{ cm}^2 \cdot 120 \text{ cm} = 38.4 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Required filler mass (accounting for 90% transfer efficiency $\eta_t$):

$$
m_{required} = \frac{V_{weld} \cdot \rho}{\eta_t} = \frac{38.4 \cdot 7.85}{0.90} = \frac{301.44}{0.90} = 334.93 \text{ g}
$$

Time required:

$$
t = \frac{m_{required}}{m_{min}} = \frac{334.93}{71.03} = 4.716 \text{ min} \approx 4 \text{ min } 43 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 4.72 \text{ minutes}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost 2–3 Marks in Module 1 ESE Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in the heat input formula:** The most common error is using $S$ in mm/s with the $H = \eta V I / (1000 S)$ form (which expects mm/min), giving an answer that is off by a factor of 60. **Always check the unit of $S$ first.**
> 2. **Forgetting the arc efficiency $\eta$:** Many students correctly write the formula but plug in $\eta = 1$ (i.e., 100% efficiency), which is physically impossible. SMAW and GMAW are explicitly assigned different $\eta$ values — memorize the table.
> 3. **Confusing HAZ with FZ:** The HAZ is **not melted**; only its microstructure is altered. Writing "HAZ is the melted region" will cost full marks on a 7-mark question.
> 4. **In GMAW classification:** The four modes of metal transfer (Short-circuit, Globular, Spray, Pulsed) are **not optional** — listing only two is a typical 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Cross-section diagram missing labels:** In a 7-mark question asking for a "neat sketch," an unlabeled diagram fetches **0 marks** for the diagram portion. Always annotate FZ, HAZ, weld crown, weld root, and base metal.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Overview of Welding Technology: 1-Page Recap**

- ✅ **Welding** = permanent coalescence of materials, with or without filler/pressure.
- ✅ **Three families:** Fusion Welding, Solid-State Welding, Resistance Welding.
- ✅ **Arc welding variants:** SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, FCAW, SAW, PAW.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{1000\,S}$ (kJ/mm, with $S$ in mm/min) — the **single most important** equation of the module.
- ✅ **Arc efficiency $\eta$:** SMAW ≈ 0.7, GMAW ≈ 0.8, GTAW ≈ 0.5, SAW ≈ 0.9 (memorize).
- ✅ **Heat input $H$ ↑ ⇒ HAZ width ↑, distortion ↑, but risk of lack of fusion ↓.**
- ✅ **Cooling time $t_{8/5}$** is the critical metallurgical parameter between 800 °C and 500 °C.
- ✅ **Five stages of weld formation:** Heat generation → Melting → Coalescence → Solidification → Microstructure formation.
- ✅ **Weld regions:** Fusion Zone (FZ, melted) ⊂ HAZ-altered base metal ⊂ Unaffected base metal.
- ✅ **Industrial share:** Robotic welding accounts for ~30% of all industrial robot installations globally.
- ✅ **Deposition rate formula:** $R_{dep} = A_{wire} \cdot S_{wire} \cdot \rho_{wire} \cdot 60$ kg/h.
- ✅ **For ESE:** Always check $S$ units; always state $\eta$; always label diagrams; always present final answers in **kJ/mm** or **J/mm** explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Welding Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Welding Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotic Welding Technology (OERUT723)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, *welding* is defined as a localized permanent joining process in which two or more pieces of material (typically metals or thermoplastics) are coalesced through the application of **heat**, **pressure**, or both, often with the addition of a filler material, to produce a metallurgical bond across the joint interface. The **American Welding Society (AWS)** classifies welding as "a localized coalescence of metals wherein coalescence is produced by heating to suitable temperatures, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of filler metal."

A **welding process** is therefore the standardized, controllable method by which this coalescence is achieved, involving a defined heat source, shielding mechanism, joint preparation, and consumable/filler management system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to **classify, compare, and select** appropriate welding processes for robotic automation, with emphasis on the **arc-based processes** most commonly deployed in industrial robots (GMAW/MIG, GTAW/TIG, PAW, and SAW).

## 1.2 Classification of Welding Processes (AWS Framework)

The **American Welding Society** broadly classifies welding into three super-families based on the primary joining mechanism:

| S.No | Super-Family | Primary Mechanism | Example Processes |
|------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Fusion Welding** | Melting of base metal (and filler) | SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, SAW, PAW, Oxy-fuel |
| 2 | **Solid-State Welding** | Bonding below melting point using pressure & diffusion | Friction Stir, Ultrasonic, Forge, Roll Bonding |
| 3 | **Brazing & Soldering** | Melting only the filler (< 450 °C for solder) | Torch brazing, Induction brazing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotic Welding Context:** Over **75%** of all industrial robots deployed for welding use **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG)** or its pulsed variants, followed by **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)** for high-precision aerospace and stainless-steel joints. Spot welding (resistance) dominates the automotive body shop with thousands of robots worldwide.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine two ice cubes pressed together. At room temperature, they don't stick. But if you apply a little heat *and* pressure (just below the melting point), the surface molecules begin to interlock — they **fuse without becoming liquid**. That is **solid-state welding**. Now, instead of pressure, melt both cubes completely together so the liquids intermix and solidify as one solid — that is **fusion welding**. Robotic welding is essentially a *machine-driven, sensor-monitored, highly repeatable version* of this fusion, with the robot acting as the steady "hand" guiding the heat source along the joint path.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Arc Temperature (GMAW/GTAW):** Approximately **6,000 °C – 20,000 °C** at the arc core.
- **Melting Point of Steel:** ~**1,500 °C**.
- **Electrode Positive Polarity (DCEP):** Most common for GMAW; gives deep penetration.
- **Heat Input Formula (KTU High-Yield):** 
$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$
 where **V** = Voltage (V), **I** = Current (A), **S** = Travel speed (mm/min). This is **bold** required knowledge for robotic weld parameter programming.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Major Welding Family

### 2.1.1 Arc Welding Processes (Most Deployed in Robotics)

#### A. Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW / Stick Welding)
- **Heat Source:** Electric arc between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the workpiece.
- **Shielding:** Decomposition of electrode coating generates a protective **gas shield + slag layer**.
- **Mechanism:** Manual or mechanized; **flux provides deoxidizers and arc stabilizers**.
- **Robotic Use:** Limited — only with adaptive seam-tracking systems due to **electrode consumption** (electrode length decreases, requires constant tracking).
- **Why it matters for KTU:** Foundational process; exam questions frequently test SMAW electrode classification (E6010, E7018).

#### B. Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG / MAG)
- **Heat Source:** Continuous arc between a **continuously-fed solid wire electrode** and the workpiece.
- **Shielding:** Externally supplied **inert gas (Argon → MIG)** or **active gas (CO₂/Ar-CO₂ mix → MAG)**.
- **Mechanism:** Wire feed speed (WFS) and arc voltage are independently controlled — ideal for **robotic parameterization**.
- **Transfer Modes (KTU High-Yield):**
  1. **Short-Circuit Transfer** — Low voltage (18–22 V), low current; "stitching" droplets.
  2. **Globular Transfer** — High current, large irregular droplets; rarely used robotically.
  3. **Spray Arc Transfer** — High current (> 200 A), axial droplet stream; smooth, spatter-free.
  4. **Pulsed Spray Transfer** — Background current + peak pulses; **best for robotic thin-gauge and out-of-position welding**.
- **Robotic Use:** **#1 process** in robotic welding cells.

#### C. Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)
- **Heat Source:** Arc between a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** and the workpiece.
- **Shielding:** Inert gas (**Argon or Helium**) — never reactive gas, to prevent tungsten erosion.
- **Mechanism:** Filler rod fed manually or with cold-wire/hot-wire feeder; **arc length controlled by HF or lift-arc starting**.
- **Current Polarity Options:**
  - **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative):** Deep penetration; standard for steel.
  - **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive):** Cleaning action on aluminum; ~⅓ penetration.
  - **AC (Alternating Current):** Combines both — **standard for aluminum robotic TIG**.
- **Robotic Use:** Aerospace, stainless steel, nuclear, food-grade welds — high quality, low deposition rate.

#### D. Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)
- **Variant of GMAW** where the electrode is a **tubular wire filled with flux**.
- **Shielding:** Either gasless (self-shielded — *FCAW-S*) or gas-shielded (*FCAW-G*).
- **Robotic Use:** Heavy structural fabrication, shipbuilding; high deposition rates.

#### E. Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)
- **Heat Source:** Arc is **completely buried under a granular flux blanket** — invisible, no spatter, no UV.
- **Shielding:** Flux + molten slag pool.
- **Mechanism:** Used in mechanized/robotic **1G (flat) and 2F (horizontal fillet)** positions only.
- **Robotic Use:** Pressure vessels, ship hulls, wind tower longitudinal seams.

#### F. Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)
- **Heat Source:** Constricted arc (nozzle orifice compresses the arc → *plasma jet*).
- **Two Variants:**
  1. **Non-Transferred Arc:** Plasma gas heats externally (welding of powders).
  2. **Transferred Arc:** Arc strikes between electrode and workpiece (welding itself).
- **Robotic Use:** Tube mills, micro-TIG for thin foil, aerospace.

### 2.1.2 Resistance Welding Processes

| Process | Description | Robotic Deployment |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Spot Welding (RSW)** | Two copper electrodes clamp sheets, current (5–20 kA) for ~0.1–0.5 s fuses a nugget. | **Dominant in automotive body-in-white (BIW)**; 4,000+ spot welds per car. |
| **Projection Welding** | Embossed projections concentrate current; multiple nuggets per stroke. | Nut and bolt projection welding on chassis. |
| **Seam Welding** | Roller electrodes produce continuous overlapping spots. | Fuel tanks, exhausts. |
| **Flash / Butt Welding** | Arc flash + upset pressure joins end-to-end. | Rail, chain links. |

### 2.1.3 Gas (Oxy-Fuel) Welding

- **Oxy-Acetylene (OAW):** Highest flame temperature (~**3,500 °C**); flame chemistry — *neutral*, *carburizing*, *oxidizing*.
- **Oxy-Hydrogen, Oxy-Propane:** Lower temperature brazing and soft soldering.
- **Robotic Use:** Rare; limited to repair and brazing tasks.

### 2.1.4 Solid-State Welding

- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW):** Non-consumable rotating tool stirs plasticized metal — used in **aluminum aerospace fuselage (e.g., Boeing 787, SpaceX)**.
- **Ultrasonic Welding (USW):** High-frequency vibration (~20 kHz) under pressure; thin foils and battery tab welding.
- **Explosion Welding:** Cladding dissimilar metals (titanium-steel transition joints).

### 2.1.5 High-Energy Beam Welding

- **Laser Beam Welding (LBW):** Focused laser (fiber, CO₂, Nd:YAG, disk); keyhole mode; high speed, low distortion.
- **Electron Beam Welding (EBW):** Vacuum chamber required; deepest penetration possible; aerospace turbine blades.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|-------------------|----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Heat Input** | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | V = voltage, I = current, S = travel speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | **Arc Energy (Robotic)** | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | J (or kJ) | t = arc time |
| 3 | **Deposition Rate (GMAW)** | $D_r = \dfrac{WFS \cdot A_e \cdot \rho}{1000}$ | kg/h | WFS in m/min, A_e = wire cross-section |
| 4 | **Power in Arc** | $P = V \cdot I$ | W | Instantaneous arc power |
| 5 | **Wire Feed Speed to Current Relation** | $I \approx k \cdot WFS$ | A vs m/min | Empirical, varies with wire diameter |
| 6 | **Spot Weld Heat (Houltermann Eq.) | $Q = I^2 R t$ | J | I = current, R = contact resistance, t = time |
| 7 | **Penetration Depth (PAW / LBW keyhole)** | $d \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k T_m}{v \rho c \pi}}$ | mm | k=thermal conductivity, v=travel speed |
| 8 | **Filler Wire Diameter Selection** | $d \approx \dfrac{t}{50}$ to $\dfrac{t}{30}$ | mm | t = plate thickness |
| 9 | **Shielding Gas Flow Rule** | $Q_{Ar} \approx 10$–$20$ L/min | L/min | For GMAW/GTAW |
| 10 | **Weld Metal / Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Controlled by $H$ (heat input) | — | Higher H → coarser grain, lower toughness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No absolute-value vertical bars** inside table cells. Note the use of $\sqrt{\dfrac{}{}}$ and $\dfrac{}{}$ notations to keep all math within LaTeX delimiters without breaking table parsing.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Spot + MIG robotic cells for body-in-white; multi-robot coordinated lines.
- **Shipbuilding:** SAW + FCAW for thick plate (20–50 mm) double-sided butt joints.
- **Aerospace:** GTAW + LBW + FSW for lightweight aluminum-lithium alloys.
- **Nuclear:** GTAW with austenitic stainless steel (e.g., 304L) for primary loop piping.
- **Electronics / EV battery:** Ultrasonic + Laser spot welding for cell interconnects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Equation (Foundation of All Robotic Weld Programming)

The **heat input** is the single most important parameter in robotic arc welding — it directly governs penetration, HAZ width, distortion, and mechanical properties.

### Step 1 — Define Arc Power

The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the arc is the product of arc voltage and arc current.

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

where **P** is in watts (W), **V** in volts (V), and **I** in amperes (A).

> **Explanation:** This is simply Ohm's law generalized for a non-linear arc load. In robotic controllers, V and I are sampled at ≥ 1 kHz by the power source interface.

### Step 2 — Multiply by Arc-on Time to Get Total Energy

If the robot dwells over a joint for time $t$ seconds, the total energy deposited is:

$$
E = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Energy per Unit Length

To get heat input **per millimeter** of weld length (the KTU-mandated unit), we divide by the **weld length** traversed during the arc-on time.

The weld length $L$ traversed in time $t$ at travel speed $S$ (mm/min) is:

$$
L = \frac{S}{60} \cdot t \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine and Simplify

Dividing the energy by the length:

$$
H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{L} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{\dfrac{S \cdot t}{60}} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S}
$$

The above expression has units of **J/mm**. To convert to the standard **kJ/mm** used in KTU/WPS:

$$
H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** We divide by **60** to convert minutes → seconds, and by **1000** to convert Joules → kilojoules. This equation is built into every **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** and every robotic **Weld Coordinate Table**.

### Step 5 — Sample Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** A robot is welding at **V = 28 V**, **I = 250 A**, **S = 400 mm/min**.

$$
H = \frac{28 \cdot 250 \cdot 60}{400 \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
H = \frac{420{,}000}{400{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 1.05 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For structural steel, the AWS-recommended upper limit is ~**1.5 kJ/mm** to avoid excessive HAZ softening in quenched & tempered grades. So 1.05 kJ/mm is **safe** for the procedure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spot Weld Heat (Houltermann Equation)

In **resistance spot welding**, the heat generated at the faying interface is governed by Joule's law applied to the contact resistance.

$$
Q = I^{2} R t
$$

where **I** = current (A), **R** = dynamic contact resistance (µΩ), **t** = weld time (cycles or s).

> **Note for KTU:** Modern automotive cells use **medium-frequency DC (MFDC)** inverters delivering **6–12 kA** for ~**200–400 ms**, giving $Q \approx 7.2 \times 10^{6}$ J at peak — enough to melt a ~6–8 mm nugget diameter in 1 mm low-carbon steel sheet.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Decision Chart

| Selection Criterion | GMAW | GTAW | FCAW | SAW | PAW | LBW | RSW |
|---------------------|------|------|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| **Thickness Range (mm)** | 1–30 | 0.5–6 | 2–>50 | 6–>100 | 0.1–10 | 0.5–25 | 0.5–3 |
| **Position Capability** | All | All | All | 1G/2F only | All | All | Lap only |
| **Deposition Rate** | **High** | Low | **Very High** | **Highest** | Low | Low | N/A |
| **Distortion Control** | Medium | **High** | Medium | Low | **High** | **Very High** | **High** |
| **Robotic Adaptability** | **Excellent** | Good | Good | Fair | Good | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Equipment Cost (relative)** | $$ | $$$ | $$ | $$$ | $$$$ | $$$$$ | $$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical takeaway:** GMAW dominates robotic cells because it offers a **balanced compromise** across deposition rate, position flexibility, and ease of automation (no manual filler feeding). LBW and GTAW dominate when **distortion or quality** outweigh throughput.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Selecting Process for Robotic Cell

**Scenario:** A Tier-1 automotive supplier must robotically weld 1.2 mm galvannealed steel door panels. Production rate = 120 panels/h.

**Analysis:**

1. **Thickness** → thin sheet → RSW (spot) or GMAW short-circuit.
2. **Throughput** → 120/h implies cycle < 30 s → **RSW chosen** (single stroke < 1 s).
3. **Galvannealed coating** → zinc vapor risks porosity → **GMAW with pulsed-spray + Ar-CO₂** better.
4. **Decision:** **Pulsed GMAW robot cell** with seam-tracking arc sensor, OR **RSW robot** with servo guns. KTU expectation: justify with **heat input vs distortion** trade-off.

$$
H_{GMAW} = \frac{19 \cdot 180 \cdot 60}{600 \cdot 1000} = 0.342 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

This low heat input keeps distortion < **0.3 mm** on 1.2 mm sheet — within automotive Class-A tolerance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Master Classification of Welding Processes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robotic Welding Process Selection] --> B{Fusion Process?}
    A --> C{Solid-State Process?}
    A --> D{Brazing or Soldering?}

    B -- Yes --> E{Heat Source?}
    E -- Electric Arc --> F1[SMAW Stick]
    E -- Electric Arc --> F2[GMAW MIG MAG]
    E -- Electric Arc --> F3[GTAW TIG]
    E -- Electric Arc --> F4[FCAW]
    E -- Electric Arc --> F5[SAW Submerged Arc]
    E -- Electric Arc --> F6[PAW Plasma Arc]

    E -- High Energy Beam --> G1[Laser Beam Welding]
    E -- High Energy Beam --> G2[Electron Beam Welding]

    E -- Oxy-Fuel Gas --> H1[Oxy-Acetylene]
    E -- Oxy-Fuel Gas --> H2[Oxy-Hydrogen]

    C -- Yes --> I1[Friction Stir FSW]
    C -- Yes --> I2[Ultrasonic USW]
    C -- Yes --> I3[Diffusion Bonding]
    C -- Yes --> I4[Explosion Welding]
    C -- Yes --> I5[Forge and Roll Bonding]

    D -- Yes --> J1[Torch Brazing]
    D -- Yes --> J2[Induction Brazing]
    D -- Yes --> J3[Resistance Brazing]

    F2 --> K[Arc Sensor + Robot TCP]
    F3 --> K
    F6 --> K
    G1 --> K
    K --> L[Adaptive Weld Controller]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Robotic GMAW Cell Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    PC[Programming Pendant HMI] --> CTRL[Robot Controller]
    CTRL --> MAN[Manipulator Arm 6-Axis]
    MAN --> TCP[End Effector Torch Mount]
    TCP --> TCH[Welding Torch]
    CTRL --> PS[Wire Feeder and Power Source]
    PS --> WF[Wire Spool]
    PS --> GAS[Shielding Gas Cylinder Ar or CO2]
    TCH --> ARC[Electric Arc]
    ARC --> WP[Workpiece Fillet Joint]
    WP --> GND[Grounding Clamp]
    GND --> PS
    CTRL --> SENS[Arc Sensor and Seam Tracker]
    SENS --> CTRL
    WP --> POS[Positioner Turntable]
    POS --> CTRL
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — GTAW Arc Polarity Decision Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Select GTAW Current Polarity] --> Q1{Base Material?}
    Q1 -- Mild Steel --> DCEN[DC Electrode Negative Deep Penetration]
    Q1 -- Stainless Steel --> DCEN
    Q1 -- Aluminum Alloy --> AC[AC Polarity Cleaning plus Penetration]
    Q1 -- Magnesium --> AC
    Q1 -- Copper --> DCEN
    Q1 -- Thin Sheet less than 1 mm --> DCEN
    DCEN --> OUT1[Arc Energy 60 percent on workpiece]
    AC --> OUT2[Arc Energy 50 percent cleaning plus 50 percent penetration]
    DCEN --> REC[Recommended for Robotic Path Stability]
    AC --> REC
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Robotic Weld Process Throughput vs Quality

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Axis X Throughput kg per hour] --> Q
    Y[Axis Y Weld Quality 1 to 10] --> Q
    Q --> P1[RSW - High throughput 8 - Quality 6]
    Q --> P2[SMAW - Low throughput 2 - Quality 5]
    Q --> P3[GMAW Spray - High throughput 9 - Quality 8]
    Q --> P4[GTAW - Low throughput 3 - Quality 10]
    Q --> P5[SAW - Highest throughput 10 - Quality 7]
    Q --> P6[Laser LBW - High throughput 8 - Quality 9]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** GMAW (spray) sits in the *upper-right*, confirming it as the dominant robotic choice. GTAW wins on quality but loses on deposition. SAW wins on throughput but is limited to flat position only.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between MIG and TIG welding processes. List any **two** advantages of MIG for robotic applications.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | GMAW (MIG/MAG) | GTAW (TIG) |
|--------|-----------------|-------------|
| Electrode | Continuous consumable wire | Non-consumable tungsten |
| Filler | Wire itself | Separate filler rod |
| Shielding Gas | Inert (Ar) or Active (CO₂/Ar mix) | Purely inert (Ar/He) |
| Deposition Rate | **High** | Low |
| Robotic Suitability | **Excellent** (continuous wire) | Good (separate filler feed needed) |

**Two advantages for robotics:**
1. **Continuous wire feed** eliminates electrode length tracking — enables uninterrupted multi-meter welds.
2. **Higher deposition rate** → faster cycle times → higher ROI.

*(1 Mark for table, 1 Mark for each advantage = 3 Marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define **Heat Input** in arc welding. State its **SI unit** and the formula.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** Heat input is the total energy delivered per unit length of weld, controlling HAZ size, grain growth, and distortion.
- **Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

- **SI Unit (1 Mark):** **kilojoules per millimeter (kJ/mm)**. *(Engineers may also express in J/mm.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Heat Input Numericals:**
> - Students *frequently forget* to convert **mm/min → mm/s** (hence the factor 60). Always check units before substituting.
> - Always convert final energy to **kJ/mm**, not J/mm, to match the AWS standard used in WPS.
> - Do not confuse **travel speed (S)** with **wire feed speed (WFS)** — they are independent parameters.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Common Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** 

**(a)** Classify the major **arc welding processes** used in robotic welding cells. Explain any **three** of them in detail with neat sketches of their equipment setup, electrode type, and shielding mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robotic GMAW cell is programmed to weld 6 mm thick structural steel with the following parameters: Arc Voltage = 26 V, Current = 220 A, Travel Speed = 350 mm/min. Calculate:
  (i) Heat input in kJ/mm.
  (ii) If the cycle time must reduce by 20% (i.e., travel speed increases to 420 mm/min) without changing heat input, what must the new current be (voltage held constant)?
  **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification (3 Marks) + any three detailed (4 Marks = 1⅓ each):**

The major arc welding processes used in robotic cells are:

1. **GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding)** — continuous consumable wire electrode; shielding via externally supplied inert/active gas; most widely robotized process.
2. **GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding)** — non-consumable tungsten; inert gas shielding; high-precision robotic TIG.
3. **FCAW (Flux-Cored Arc Welding)** — tubular electrode with flux core; self-shielded or gas-shielded.
4. **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** — arc buried under granular flux; mechanized flat-position welding.
5. **PAW (Plasma Arc Welding)** — constricted arc from nozzle; transferred and non-transferred variants.
6. **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding)** — flux-coated stick electrode; limited robotic use.

**Detailed Explanation (any three):**

- **GMAW:** Wire fed by a servo-controlled wire feeder; arc struck between wire tip and workpiece; shielding gas (Ar for MIG; Ar/CO₂ for MAG) protects the weld pool. Transfer modes: short-circuit, globular, spray, pulsed-spray. Ideal for robotic automation due to continuous wire and stable arc.
- **GTAW:** Tungsten electrode held in a collet; arc struck using high-frequency (HF) start or lift arc; filler rod manually dipped or fed by cold/hot wire feeder. Polarity: DCEN for steel, AC for aluminum. Robotics uses **AVC (Arc Voltage Control)** for height regulation.
- **SAW:** Arc and weld pool are completely submerged under a layer of granular flux fed from a hopper. No visible arc, no spatter, no UV hazard. Mechanized for long seams; robotics used for longitudinal pressure-vessel welds.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Heat Input (3 Marks):**

Given: $V = 26$ V, $I = 220$ A, $S = 350$ mm/min.

$$
H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
H = \frac{26 \cdot 220 \cdot 60}{350 \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
H = \frac{343{,}200}{350{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 0.981 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

**(ii) New Current at 420 mm/min, Same Heat Input (4 Marks):**

Set $H_{\text{new}} = H_{\text{old}} = 0.981$ kJ/mm:

$$
0.981 = \frac{26 \cdot I_{\text{new}} \cdot 60}{420 \cdot 1000}
$$

Rearrange to solve for $I_{\text{new}}$:

$$
I_{\text{new}} = \frac{0.981 \cdot 420 \cdot 1000}{26 \cdot 60}
$$

$$
I_{\text{new}} = \frac{412{,}020}{1560}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{\text{new}} \approx 264.1 \text{ A}}
$$

**Verification (1 Mark):**

$$
H = \frac{26 \cdot 264.1 \cdot 60}{420 \cdot 1000} = \frac{412{,}000}{420{,}000} \approx 0.981 \text{ kJ/mm} \;\; \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare **resistance spot welding (RSW)** and **gas metal arc welding (GMAW)** with respect to: (i) heat source, (ii) typical application, (iii) suitability for robotic automation. Mention **two specific industries** where RSW dominates and **two** where GMAW dominates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** with a labeled schematic. A robotic SAW station operates at 32 V, 600 A, with a travel speed of 1.2 m/min. Compute the heat input in kJ/mm and state whether it falls within the typical range for thick-plate structural steel. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | RSW | GMAW |
|-----------|-----|------|
| **Heat Source** | Joule heating from high current (5–20 kA) | Electric arc (~6,000–20,000 °C) |
| **Joint Type** | Lap joints (overlap only) | Butt, lap, fillet, T-joints |
| **Cycle Time** | 0.2–0.5 s per spot | Continuous, several seconds per meter |
| **Filler** | None (autogenous) | Continuous consumable wire |
| **Robotic Suitability** | Excellent (servo guns) | Excellent (continuous wire + arc sensor) |
| **Industries** | **Automotive BIW, white goods** | **Shipbuilding, structural steel, pipelines** |

*(1 Mark for table completion, 1 Mark for industry mapping)*

**(b) SAW Principle + Numerical:**

**Principle (3 Marks):**
In SAW, the arc is established between a **continuously fed bare wire electrode** and the workpiece, but is **completely buried under a blanket of granular flux** fed from a hopper. The flux melts to form a protective slag over the weld pool, shielding it from atmospheric contamination. No spatter, no UV, no visible arc — ideal for **flat-position mechanized welding** of thick plates.

**Schematic (1 Mark):** *(Refer to standard SAW diagram — flux hopper above, wire feed nozzle, submerged arc, slag crust, workpiece.)*

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given: $V = 32$ V, $I = 600$ A, $S = 1.2$ m/min $= 1200$ mm/min.

$$
H = \frac{32 \cdot 600 \cdot 60}{1200 \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
H = \frac{1{,}152{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 0.96 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** For thick-plate structural steel (> 12 mm), the typical SAW heat input ranges from **0.8 to 3.5 kJ/mm** depending on grade. Hence, **0.96 kJ/mm is well within the acceptable range**, indicating controlled deposition with moderate HAZ.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Types of Welding Processes**

- ✅ Welding = localized coalescence; classified by AWS into **fusion, solid-state, and brazing**.
- ✅ **Arc welding family:** SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, FCAW, SAW, PAW — GMAW is the **#1 robotic process**.
- ✅ **GMAW shielding:** MIG = Ar (inert); MAG = Ar/CO₂ (active). Pulse spray transfer = best robotic mode.
- ✅ **GTAW:** Tungsten (non-consumable), DCEN for steel, **AC for aluminum**, separate filler rod.
- ✅ **SAW:** Submerged under flux — flat position only, highest deposition rate, no visible arc.
- ✅ **Resistance family:** RSW dominates automotive BIW; heat from $Q = I^2 R t$.
- ✅ **Solid-state:** FSW (aluminum aerospace), USW (battery tabs), explosion (cladding).
- ✅ **Beam processes:** LBW (keyhole, high speed), EBW (vacuum, deepest penetration).
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ in **kJ/mm** — *memorize this verbatim*.
- ✅ Typical robotic heat input: **0.3–1.5 kJ/mm** for steel; above 1.5 kJ/mm risks HAZ softening.
- ✅ Robotic process selection is governed by: **thickness, position, deposition rate, distortion tolerance, and quality requirement**.
- ✅ AWS polarities: **DCEP** = deep penetration for GMAW; **DCEN** = deep penetration for GTAW steel; **AC** = aluminum cleaning.
- ✅ Shielding gas flow standard: **10–20 L/min** for Ar in GMAW/GTAW; trim upward for stainless / titanium.
- ✅ Filler diameter rule: $d_{\text{wire}} \approx t_{\text{plate}}/30$ to $t_{\text{plate}}/50$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution of Welding Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evolution of Welding Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding Technology Evolution** refers to the chronological progression of metallurgical joining techniques — from primitive forge-welding (smelting and hammer joining of iron) to the present-day **intelligent, sensor-integrated, robotized arc welding** cells driven by **Industry 4.0** digital control loops.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OERUT723 – Robotic Welding Technology)**, the study of welding evolution is foundational because every modern parameter in robotic MIG/MAG, TIG (GTAW), Plasma, and Laser-Arc hybrid welding is a direct descendant of a 19th–20th century invention. Understanding this lineage is essential to comprehend *why* today's robotic welding power sources use **constant-voltage (CV) output**, *why* **Inverter-based IGBT power sources** replaced transformer-rectifiers, and *why* **seam tracking** via laser vision exists.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Smartphone Lineage of Joining"**
> Think of welding evolution like the lineage of mobile phones:
> - **1880s Forge Welding** = the bulky "brick phone" — works, but crude, manual, and skill-dependent.
> - **1940s TIG / MIG** = the first "feature phone" — reliable, repeatable, semi-automatic.
> - **1980s Robotic Arc Welding** = the "smartphone" — programmable, multi-axis, sensor-aware.
> - **2020s AI-driven Adaptive Welding** = the "AI smartphone" — self-learning, closed-loop controlled.
>
> A robotic welding engineer in 2025 cannot troubleshoot a **fuzzy-logic arc-length controller** without knowing that arc welding itself began with **carbon arc heating (Bernardos, 1881)** and **resistance heating (Elihu Thomson, 1886)**.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme examiners frequently use Module 1 questions to test a student's **chronological reasoning** and **process-classification aptitude**. Marks are often lost when students confuse *invention dates* with *commercialization dates*, or when they fail to map inventors to the **specific process** they pioneered.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics recur across the entire evolution:

- **Arc Temperature Range:** $\mathbf{6000^{\circ}C \text{ to } 20{,}000^{\circ}C}$
- **Standard Industrial Voltage (MIG/MAG CV):** $\mathbf{15\text{ V to } 40\text{ V (DC)}}$
- **Standard Wire Feed Speed (WFS):** $\mathbf{2\text{ m/min to } 20\text{ m/min}}$
- **Shielding Gas Purity (Argon/CO₂ grade):** $\mathbf{\geq 99.99\%}$
- **Robot Repeatability (modern 6-axis):** $\mathbf{\pm 0.03\text{ mm to } \pm 0.08\text{ mm}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Epochs of Welding Evolution

The evolution of welding technology can be cleanly partitioned into **six major epochs**. Each epoch introduced a *new energy source* or a *new control paradigm* that redefined what "joining two metals" meant.

#### Epoch 1 — Forge & Solid-State Welding Era (Pre-1800 to ~1880)

- **Principle:** Joining achieved by *plastic deformation* of metal in a heated, hammered state. No melting occurs.
- **Why it matters historically:** Demonstrated that metallurgical bonding is possible *below the melting point* — a principle that survives today in **friction stir welding (FSW)** and **ultrasonic welding**.
- **Key Limitation:** Highly **operator-skill-dependent**; impossible to mechanize in a robotic cell.

#### Epoch 2 — Carbon Arc & Resistance Welding Era (1881 – 1920)

- **1881 — Nikolai Bernardos (Russia):** Patented the **carbon arc welding** process using a carbon electrode and an electric arc.
- **1885 – 1886 — Elihu Thomson (USA):** Invented **resistance welding** (spot and projection welding), enabling mass production of sheet-metal assemblies.
- **Why it matters:** Introduced **electricity as a controllable heat source** — the foundational leap toward automation.

#### Epoch 3 — Coated Electrode & Oxy-Acetylene Era (1900 – 1940)

- **1900 — Strohmenger (UK):** Introduced the **flux-coated (covered) electrode**, stabilizing the arc and adding deoxidizers.
- **1903 — Edmond Fouché & Picard (France):** Developed the **oxy-acetylene torch** using oxygen + acetylene (flame temperature ~**$3,480^{\circ}\text{C}$**).
- **Why it matters:** Coated electrodes introduced **arc chemistry control** — a direct ancestor of modern **flux-cored arc welding (FCAW)**.

#### Epoch 4 — Inert Gas Shielded Arc Welding Era (1940 – 1970)

- **1940 — Meredith (USA):** Patented the **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)** process, using a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** shielded by inert gas.
- **1948 — Robinoff / Airco (USA):** Invented **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG)**, using a **continuously-fed consumable wire** and shielding gas.
- **Why it matters:** This is the **birth of true mechanization** — MIG is *the* dominant process in modern robotic welding cells.

#### Epoch 5 — Robotic Arc Welding Era (1970 – 2000)

- **1961 — Unimate robot (Devol & Engelberger):** First industrial robot; deployed in GM die-casting in **1961**.
- **1974 — Carl Clausing (Cincinnati Milacron):** Introduced the **T³ (The Tomorrow Tool) robot** — one of the first commercially used for **arc welding**.
- **Why it matters:** Established the **6-axis articulated robot** as the workhorse of welding automation; introduced the **teach pendant** and **playback programming** paradigm.

#### Epoch 6 — Intelligent / Sensor-Integrated Welding Era (2000 – Present)

- **2000s onward:** **Through-the-arc sensing (TAC)**, **laser seam tracking**, **arc-length fuzzy-logic control**.
- **2010s onward:** **Offline programming (OLP)**, **digital twin simulation**, **vision-based weld pool monitoring**.
- **2020s:** **AI-driven adaptive welding**, **cloud-based weld data analytics**, **collaborative robots (cobots)** in welding.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **key classification metrics** frequently tested in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| Parameter | Forge Welding (Solid-State) | Resistance Welding | SMAW (Stick) | GTAW (TIG) | GMAW (MIG/MAG) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Source** | Hammer + Furnace | Resistive (I²R) heating | Electric Arc | Electric Arc (Tungsten) | Electric Arc (Wire) |
| **Melting Occurs?** | No | No / Slight | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Shielding** | None | None | Flux coating | Inert gas (Ar) | Inert / Active gas |
| **Typical Temp (°C)** | $900 - 1200$ | $1500 - 2000$ | $6000 - 10000$ | $6000 - 20000$ | $6000 - 20000$ |
| **Automation Potential** | Very Low | High (Spot) | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Robot Compatibility** | Not feasible | Limited | Rare | Medium | Excellent |
| **Invention Year (approx.)** | Pre-3000 BC | 1886 | 1900 | 1940 | 1948 |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **6-axis robotic MIG/MAG cell** used today in **automotive BIW (Body-in-White)** assembly lines is a direct technological descendant of all six epochs. A modern automotive weld cell (e.g., for a Tesla Model Y or Maruti Suzuki frame) integrates:
> - **Arc control** (Epoch 2/3),
> - **Solid-state inverter power source** (Epoch 4 refinement),
> - **6-axis articulated arm** (Epoch 5),
> - **Laser seam tracking + adaptive fill** (Epoch 6).
>
> Failure to understand any one epoch causes cascading errors in **weld procedure specification (WPS)** development and **parameter optimization**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is **historical-analytical** rather than mathematical. Therefore, instead of algebraic derivations, this section provides an **exhaustive analytical walkthrough** of the **technological cause-and-effect chain** linking each epoch to the next, and a **classification matrix** derivable directly from first principles.

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the Evolution Chain

The transition between epochs is governed by a consistent **4-step logic** that can be reconstructed as follows:

**Step 1 — Identify the Limitation of the Predecessor**
At the end of each epoch, a critical industrial bottleneck emerges. For instance, in Epoch 3, the **manual stick electrode** limited production rates to approximately **30 – 60 welds/hour per welder**.

**Step 2 — Formulate the Engineering Need**
A new control variable is demanded. In Epoch 4, the need became: *"Can we shield the molten pool from atmospheric contamination without using a flux coating?"* — leading to **inert gas shielding**.

**Step 3 — Apply a New Physical Principle**
A previously unexploited physical phenomenon is harnessed. The **Tungsten Inert Gas** process exploited the **extremely high melting point of tungsten ($\approx 3422^{\circ}\text{C}$)** to allow the electrode to remain solid while the workpiece melted.

**Step 4 — Verify Commercial Feasibility**
The new process must achieve **deposition rate**, **bead quality**, and **repeatability** thresholds that justify industrial adoption. MIG (1948) achieved this through **continuous wire feed**, eliminating the stop-start cycle of stick electrodes.

### 3.2 The Classification Derivation (Heat Source Family Tree)

Starting from the **root concept of joining two metals**, the process tree can be analytically derived as:

$$
\text{Welding} = \underbrace{\text{Solid-State Welding}}_{\text{No Melting}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\text{Fusion Welding}}_{\text{Melting Occurs}}
$$

Further decomposition:

$$
\text{Fusion Welding} = \underbrace{\text{Air-Exposed}}_{\text{SMAW / FCAW}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\text{Gas-Shielded}}_{\text{GTAW, GMAW, PAW}}
$$

And finally:

$$
\text{Gas-Shielded Fusion} = \underbrace{\text{Non-Consumable Electrode}}_{\text{GTAW, Plasma}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\text{Consumable Electrode}}_{\text{GMAW, FCAW-Gas}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **Venn-style decomposition** is a high-yield KTU 2024 answer template — examiners award full marks when students draw the *parent-child relationship* explicitly.

### 3.3 Analytical Timeline Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below should be memorized verbatim for the 14-mark questions. It is a **direct extraction from KTU Module 1 syllabus outcomes**.

| Decade | Dominant Innovation | Process / System | Industrial Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-1880 | Forge Welding | Blacksmith hammer-join | Limited to small blacksmiths |
| 1881 | Carbon Arc | Bernardos Patent | First *electrical* joining |
| 1886 | Resistance Spot | Elihu Thomson | Birth of mass-production sheet metal |
| 1900 | Coated Electrode | Strohmenger | Stabilized arc chemistry |
| 1903 | Oxy-Acetylene | Fouché & Picard | Portable, field-usable |
| 1940 | GTAW (TIG) | Meredith | Aerospace-grade precision |
| 1948 | GMAW (MIG) | Robinoff/Airco | Birth of mechanization |
| 1961 | Unimate Robot | Devol & Engelberger | First programmable manipulator |
| 1974 | T³ Arc-Welding Robot | Cincinnati Milacron | Robotic arc welding in factories |
| 1990s | Inverter Power Source | Fronius / Lincoln | Compact, high-frequency control |
| 2000s | Seam Tracking | Servo-Robot / Scansonic | Closed-loop adaptive control |
| 2010s | Offline Programming | ABB RobotStudio / DELMIA | Digital-twin-based path planning |
| 2020s | AI/Cloud Welding | OTC-Daihen / Yaskawa | Self-tuning, IoT data logging |

### 3.4 Derivation of the "Why Robotics Needs MIG" Statement

A core KTU 2024 Module 1 expected answer is the **justification of MIG as the default robotic process**. The logical chain is:

$$
\text{Robot Trajectory} \rightarrow \text{Continuous Motion} \rightarrow \text{Cannot Stop to Change Electrode} \rightarrow \text{Need Continuous Wire Feed} \rightarrow \text{Therefore: GMAW (MIG/MAG) is the natural robotic welding process.}
$$

This is mathematically equivalent to stating: **a discontinuous process (SMAW) has a duty cycle that violates the continuous-trajectory constraint of a 6-axis robot.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Timeline Flow (Evolution Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Epoch 1: Forge Welding Pre-1880] --> B[Epoch 2: Carbon Arc and Resistance 1881 to 1920]
    B --> C[Epoch 3: Coated Electrode and Oxy-Acetylene 1900 to 1940]
    C --> D[Epoch 4: Inert Gas Shielded Arc 1940 to 1970]
    D --> E[Epoch 5: Robotic Arc Welding 1970 to 2000]
    E --> F[Epoch 6: Intelligent Sensor Integrated 2000 to Present]

    A1[Manual Hammer Join] --- A
    B1[Carbon Arc 1881] --- B
    B2[Resistance Spot 1886] --- B
    C1[Coated Stick 1900] --- C
    C2[Oxy Acetylene 1903] --- C
    D1[GTAW TIG 1940] --- D
    D2[GMAW MIG 1948] --- D
    E1[Unimate 1961] --- E
    E2[T3 Robot 1974] --- E
    F1[Laser Seam Tracking 2000s] --- F
    F2[AI Adaptive Welding 2020s] --- F
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Modern Robotic Welding Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        P1[CAD Model of Weld Path]
        P2[WPS Welding Procedure Spec]
    end

    subgraph Control[Control Layer]
        C1[Offline Programming Software]
        C2[Robot Controller]
        C3[Power Source Inverter]
    end

    subgraph Sensing[Sensing Layer]
        S1[Through the Arc Sensor]
        S2[Laser Seam Tracker]
        S3[Arc Length Fuzzy Controller]
    end

    subgraph Robot[Mechanical Layer]
        R1[6 Axis Articulated Robot]
        R2[Torch and Wire Feeder]
        R3[Shielding Gas Unit]
    end

    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R2
    S1 --> C3
    S2 --> C2
    S3 --> C3
    R1 --> S1
    S2 --> R1
    R3 --> R2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Welding Technology]

    root --> ss[Solid State Welding]
    root --> fs[Fusion Welding]

    ss --> s1[Forge Welding]
    ss --> s2[Friction Stir Welding]
    ss --> s3[Ultrasonic Welding]

    fs --> fe[Arc Based]
    fs --> fb[Non Arc Based]

    fe --> fe1[SMAW Manual Stick]
    fe --> fe2[GTAW TIG]
    fe --> fe3[GMAW MIG MAG]
    fe --> fe4[Plasma Arc PAW]
    fe --> fe5[Flux Cored FCAW]

    fb --> fb1[Oxy Acetylene]
    fb --> fb2[Laser Beam LBW]
    fb --> fb3[Electron Beam EBW]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for Students:** The classification tree above is a **guaranteed KTU exam visual**. KTU 2024 examiners reward answers that include a *hand-drawn or computer-rendered* process classification tree, similar to the Mermaid block above.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List and briefly explain any two major milestones in the evolution of welding technology that enabled the transition from manual to mechanized welding.**

**Model Answer:**

The two major milestones are:

1. **Invention of Coated Electrode (Strohmenger, 1900):** Introduced flux coating on stick electrodes, which stabilized the arc, added deoxidizing elements, and protected the weld pool from atmospheric contamination. This laid the foundation for **SMAW** as a reliable industrial process.

2. **Invention of Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG) by Robinoff, 1948:** Introduced **continuous wire feed** combined with **inert gas shielding**, eliminating the stop-start cycle of stick electrodes. This continuous-feed architecture is the **direct enabler of robotic arc welding**, as a 6-axis robot requires a continuous, uninterrupted weld path.

> **Marks Distribution:** *2 marks for listing correctly with year + inventor; 1 mark for linking to mechanization.*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Why is GMAW (MIG/MAG) considered the most robot-friendly welding process? Justify with reference to the evolution of welding technology.**

**Model Answer:**

GMAW is considered the most robot-friendly process because of three evolutionary factors:

1. **Continuous Wire Feed:** Allows uninterrupted deposition along a programmed robot trajectory, matching the continuous motion profile of a 6-axis articulated robot.

2. **Adaptable to Constant-Voltage (CV) Power Sources:** Modern inverter-based power sources (1990s evolution) provide stable arc characteristics, easily interfaced with robot controllers.

3. **Compatible with Through-the-Arc Sensing (TAC):** This sensor feedback (Epoch 6) enables real-time adaptive control, which is essential for robotic seam tracking.

> **Marks Distribution:** *1 mark per point with explicit evolution linkage; 3 × 1 = 3 marks.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, classify the various welding processes based on the **heat source and state of the metal** during joining. Briefly describe the **solid-state** and **fusion welding** categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **six major epochs** in the evolution of welding technology from forge welding to modern intelligent robotic welding. Highlight the contribution of any **three key inventors** and their patented processes. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Classification of Welding Processes:**

| Basis | Category 1 | Category 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **State of Metal** | Solid-State Welding (no melting) | Fusion Welding (melting occurs) |
| **Heat Source** | Mechanical (forge, friction, ultrasonic) | Thermal (arc, gas flame, beam) |

**Solid-State Welding:** Joining is achieved by **plastic deformation** of metals at temperatures below their melting point. Example: Forge welding, Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Ultrasonic welding. **Advantage:** No metallurgical defects from solidification; **Disadvantage:** Requires high force, limited to specific joint geometries.

**Fusion Welding:** Joining is achieved by **melting the base metal** (with or without filler) followed by solidification. Example: SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, Plasma, Laser. **Advantage:** Versatile joint types, high strength; **Disadvantage:** Susceptible to porosity, solidification cracking.

*Refer to the Mermaid classification tree in Section 4.3 for the diagrammatic answer.*

> **Mark Split (a):** *3 marks for block diagram; 2 marks for solid-state description; 2 marks for fusion description.*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

The **six epochs of welding evolution** are:

**Epoch 1 — Forge Welding (Pre-1880):**
Manual hammer-joining of heated iron. Pure mechanical force + heat. Limited to small blacksmith operations. **Limitation:** Operator-skill dependent, non-mechanizable.

**Epoch 2 — Carbon Arc & Resistance Welding (1881 – 1920):**
- **Nikolai Bernardos (1881):** Patented carbon arc welding — first use of electricity as a heat source.
- **Elihu Thomson (1886):** Invented resistance spot welding — first mass-production joining method.

**Epoch 3 — Coated Electrode & Oxy-Acetylene (1900 – 1940):**
- **Strohmenger (1900):** Coated stick electrode with flux — stabilized the arc and added deoxidizers.
- **Fouché & Picard (1903):** Oxy-acetylene torch — enabled portable, field-usable cutting/welding.

**Epoch 4 — Inert Gas Shielded Arc (1940 – 1970):**
- **Meredith (1940):** Invented GTAW (TIG) using non-consumable tungsten + inert gas.
- **Robinoff / Airco (1948):** Invented GMAW (MIG) — continuous wire + gas shielding.

**Epoch 5 — Robotic Arc Welding (1970 – 2000):**
- **Devol & Engelberger (1961):** Unimate — first industrial robot.
- **Carl Clausing / Cincinnati Milacron (1974):** T³ robot — first commercial **arc-welding robot**.

**Epoch 6 — Intelligent / Sensor-Integrated (2000 – Present):**
Integration of **laser seam tracking**, **through-the-arc sensing**, **fuzzy-logic arc control**, **offline programming**, and **AI-driven adaptive welding** (Industry 4.0).

> **Mark Split (b):** *1 mark per epoch brief; 1 bonus mark each for naming three inventors correctly with year and process — 3 × 1 = 3 marks reserved for inventors; remaining 4 marks for descriptive content.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **transition from manual stick electrode welding (SMAW) to mechanized MIG/MAG robotic welding**, emphasizing the technological limitations overcome at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **role of power source evolution** — from transformer-rectifier units to modern **IGBT-based inverter power sources** — in enabling robotic arc welding. List **two key parameters** a modern robotic welding power source must control. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The transition from SMAW to robotic MIG/MAG involved overcoming **four critical limitations**:

1. **Stop-Start Cycle of SMAW:** Stick electrodes (~**$350\text{ mm}$** length) must be replaced every 2 – 4 minutes. A robot on a continuous trajectory cannot wait. **Solution:** Continuous wire feed (MIG wire spool).

2. **Manual Slag Removal:** SMAW leaves a slag layer requiring chipping — a discrete post-process step. **Solution:** MIG uses gas shielding, **no slag** is generated.

3. **Inconsistent Arc Length:** Manual welders naturally compensate; robots require **constant-voltage (CV) regulation**. **Solution:** CV power source with arc-length feedback (TAC).

4. **Operator-Dependent Quality:** Human welder skill varies; robots require **repeatable parameter logging**. **Solution:** Digital welding controllers with **closed-loop current/voltage feedback**.

> **Mark Split (a):** *1.5 marks per limitation; 0.5 mark per solution; 1 mark for overall conclusion.*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Power Source Evolution:**

- **1960s – 1980s:** **Transformer-Rectifier (Silicon Diode) Power Sources** — bulky ($\sim 200\text{ kg}$), single-knob voltage control, low power factor.
- **1990s onward:** **Inverter Power Sources (IGBT/MOSFET)** — compact, high switching frequency ($\sim 20\text{ kHz}$), precise waveform control, digital interface to robot controller.

**Two Key Parameters Controlled by a Modern Robotic Power Source:**

1. **Constant Voltage (CV) Output:** Typically **$15 - 40\text{ V}$** DC, regulated to within $\pm 0.5\text{ V}$ for stable MIG arc.

2. **Wire Feed Speed (WFS):** Typically **$2 - 20\text{ m/min}$**, dynamically adjusted by the robot controller based on torch position and travel speed.

> **Mark Split (b):** *3 marks for power source evolution narrative; 2 marks each for the two parameters with units.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating inventors with processes:** Many students write *"Edison invented MIG"* — this is **incorrect** (Edison had no role in MIG). Robinoff/Airco invented MIG in **1948**.
> 2. **Confusing years:** GTAW is **1940**, not 1948; MIG is **1948**, not 1940. Examiners deduct **0.5 – 1 mark** per swapped year.
> 3. **Omitting units:** Writing "voltage of 20" instead of **"20 V"** costs marks in numerical questions.
> 4. **Skipping the linkage:** Simply listing epochs without explaining *how each solved the limitation of the previous* is penalized. Always write: *"Epoch X arose because Epoch X-1 suffered from limitation Y."*
> 5. **Wrong robot association:** The **Unimate (1961)** is a *general-purpose* industrial robot; the **T³ (1974)** is specifically the *first arc-welding robot*. Mixing these loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Evolution of Welding Technology (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

- **Six Epochs:** Forge (Pre-1880) → Carbon Arc/Resistance (1881-1920) → Coated/Oxy-Acetylene (1900-1940) → Inert Gas Shielded (1940-1970) → Robotic Arc (1970-2000) → Intelligent Sensor (2000+).

- **Three Must-Know Inventors:** Bernardos (1881, Carbon Arc), Thomson (1886, Resistance), Meredith (1940, TIG), Robinoff (1948, MIG), Devol (1961, Unimate).

- **MIG is the default robotic process** because of *continuous wire feed + CV power source + no slag + sensor compatibility*.
