5. Continuous operation with high arc-on time.

**Limitations:**
1. Spatter generation (especially in CO₂).
2. Sensitive to draft and contamination.
3. Higher heat input can cause distortion in thin sheets.
4. Requires precise wire-feed calibration.

---

#### Part (b) — Robotic GTAW and Comparison [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of GTAW:** A non-consumable tungsten electrode sustains the arc. The weld pool is shielded by an inert gas (Ar or He). Filler metal may be added manually or via a cold-wire feeder.

**Equipment:**
1. Robot with **arc-length control** (AVC) for constant CTWD.
2. **TIG power source** with HF start or lift-arc.
3. **Tungsten electrode** (2% lanthanated or ceriated).
4. **Gas lens** for laminar shielding flow.
5. **Water-cooled torch** for high-current operation.

**Parameters:**
- DCEN: most heat in workpiece (≈70%).
- Pulse parameters: peak current, background, pulse width, pulse frequency.
- AC for aluminum: balances cleaning and penetration.

**Advantages:**
1. Highest quality welds, no spatter.
2. Excellent for thin materials, stainless steel, titanium.
3. Precise heat input control.
4. Superior cosmetic appearance.

**Limitations:**
1. Low deposition rate (≈1 kg/h).
2. Slower travel speeds.
3. Requires highly skilled setup and torch maintenance.
4. Sensitive to operator errors if not automated.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | GMAW | GTAW |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode | Consumable | Non-consumable |
| Filler | The wire itself | Separate |
| Deposition Rate | High | Low |
| Heat Input | Higher | Lower |
| Spatter | Yes (especially CO₂) | None |
| Robotics Suitability | High | High (with AVC) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Material Range | Wide | Reactive metals best |
| Typical Industry | Automotive BIW | Aerospace, Nuclear |

**Conclusion:** GMAW dominates **high-volume structural applications** like automotive and shipbuilding. GTAW is preferred for **precision-critical, thin-section, or reactive-metal welds** in aerospace and pharmaceutical industries. Many robotic cells integrate **both processes** in hybrid production lines.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** Explain with neat sketches the **principle, equipment, parameters, advantages, and applications** of **(a)** Laser Beam Welding (LBW) **and (b)** Friction Stir Welding (FSW) as used in robotic cells.

#### Part (a) — Robotic Laser Beam Welding [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A high-power laser beam (typically **fiber laser at 1070 nm wavelength**, power 1–10 kW) is focused through a lens to a small spot (0.1–0.6 mm diameter). The intense electromagnetic radiation heats and vaporizes the metal, creating either a **conduction-mode** weld (shallow, wide) or a **keyhole-mode** weld (deep, narrow).

**Power density equation:**

$$P_d = \dfrac{P_{laser}}{\pi r^2}$$

For a 4 kW laser focused to 0.3 mm radius:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_d &= \dfrac{4000}{\pi (0.3)^2} \\
&= \dfrac{4000}{0.2827} \\
&\approx 14{,}148 \text{ W/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Modes:**
- **Conduction Mode:** $P_d \sim 10^5$ W/mm² → cosmetic welds, lap joints.
- **Keyhole Mode:** $P_d \sim 10^6$–$10^7$ W/mm² → deep penetration.

**Equipment:**
1. **Laser source** (fiber, disc, or CO₂).
2. **Fiber-optic beam delivery** to robot head.
3. **Robotic scanner head or focusing optics**.
4. **Shielding gas nozzle** (He or Ar to prevent plasma plume).
5. **Robot with high path accuracy**.
6. **Cooling system** for optics and laser source.

**Parameters:**
- Laser power (kW)
- Travel speed (m/min)
- Focal position (mm above/below surface)
- Beam quality (BPP — beam parameter product, mm·mrad)
- Shielding gas flow rate

**Advantages:**
1. Very high travel speeds (up to 10 m/min in thin sheet).
2. Low heat input → minimal distortion.
3. Non-contact process (no tool wear).
4. Excellent for dissimilar metals (e.g., copper-aluminum in EV batteries).
5. Easily automated on robots.

**Limitations:**
1. High capital cost.
2. Strict joint-fit-up requirements.
3. Plasma plume can shield beam at high power.
4. Safety hazards (Class 4 laser).

**Applications:** Battery tab welding, tailored blanks, hermetic sealing, aerospace, medical device manufacturing.

---

#### Part (b) — Robotic Friction Stir Welding [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A non-consumable rotating tool (with a **shoulder** and a **pin/probe**) is plunged into the joint between two workpieces. Frictional heat between the tool and workpiece softens (but **does not melt**) the material into a plasticized state. The rotating pin **stirs** the plasticized material, and the shoulder constrains it. As the tool traverses the joint, the stirred material is forged into a solid-state weld.

**Tool Geometry:**
- **Pin diameter:** typically 3–10 mm.
- **Pin length:** slightly less than plate thickness.
- **Shoulder diameter:** 2–3× pin diameter.
- **Tilt angle:** 1–3° to consolidate material.

**Process Parameters:**
- **Rotational speed (RPM):** 100–3000 (lower for aluminum: 400–1200 RPM).
- **Traverse speed:** 50–500 mm/min.
- **Axial force:** 5–50 kN.
- **Tilt angle:** 1–3°.

**Temperature generated:** $T \approx 0.7$ to $0.9 \, T_{melt}$ (°K). For aluminum $T_{melt} = 660$°C, so working temperature ≈ 460–600°C.

**Equipment:**
1. **Heavy-duty robotic or gantry system** capable of high axial force.
2. **Spindle** with precise RPM control.
3. **FSW tool holder** with force feedback.
4. **Backing plate / anvil**.
5. **Cooling system** for tool.

**Advantages:**
1. No melting → no solidification defects (porosity, cracks).
2. Low distortion.
3. Excellent mechanical properties (often superior to parent metal).
4. Environmentally friendly (no fumes, no shielding gas).
5. Suitable for "non-weldable" alloys (e.g., 2xxx and 7xxx Al series).

**Limitations:**
1. Requires rigid clamping and high force.
2. Leaves an exit-hole at the end of the weld.
3. Lower travel speeds than arc welding.
4. Tool wear on hard materials.

**Applications:**
- **Marine:** Aluminum decks of high-speed ferries.
- **Aerospace:** Lower wing panels of Boeing, fuselage panels.
- **Rail:** Train car body panels.
- **Automotive:** EV battery trays, suspension links.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | LBW | FSW |
|---|---|---|
| State of Metal | Molten (with vapor) | Solid (plasticized) |
| Heat Source | Laser photons | Friction |
| Filler | Optional | None |
| Distortion | Very Low | Low |
| Joint Type | Lap, butt | Butt primarily |
| Robotic Suitability | High | Moderate (force req.) |
| Defects | Porosity, underfill | Tunnel defects, flash |

**Conclusion:** Both LBW and FSW are **advanced, high-value robotic processes**. LBW excels in **thin-section, high-speed, dissimilar-metal joining**, while FSW dominates **thick-section, solid-state, defect-free welds** in aluminum alloys.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Heat input formula units:** Many students write $H = VI/S$ without the **60/1000 conversion**. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing GMAW with GTAW:** Saying "TIG uses consumable electrode" — this is the **#1 conceptual error** in KTU papers.
> 3. **Forgetting arc efficiency η:** Heat input is "gross" — actual heat absorbed by the workpiece = $\eta \times H$. Examiners often penalize for omitting η.
> 4. **Spot welding equation:** Writing $Q = IRt$ instead of $Q = I^2Rt$ — a *very* common mistake costing **1 mark**.
> 5. **FSW misconception:** Many students incorrectly write that FSW melts the metal. **It does NOT.** Mark lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Pulsed vs. Spray GMAW:** If asked about "robotics-friendly GMAW," always specify **pulsed-spray transfer**. Generic answers get partial credit only.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Types of Robotic Welding**

- ⭐ Robotic welding is classified by **heat source**: Arc, Resistance, Beam, Friction, Specialty.
- ⭐ **GMAW (MIG):** Consumable wire, external shielding gas, **pulsed mode** preferred in robotics.
- ⭐ **GTAW (TIG):** Non-consumable tungsten, **DCEN for steel, AC for aluminum**.
- ⭐ **PAW:** Constricted arc, **keyhole mode** for deep penetration.
- ⭐ **FCAW:** Tubular wire with internal flux, good for outdoor/structural work.
- ⭐ **SAW:** Arc buried in flux, **thick plates**, shipbuilding, pressure vessels.
- ⭐ **RSW:** Joule heating $Q = I^2 R t$, dominant in **automotive BIW** (4,000–6,000 welds/car).
- ⭐ **LBW:** Power density $10^6$–$10^7$ W/mm²; **conduction vs. keyhole modes**.
- ⭐ **FSW:** Solid-state, **no melting**, no shielding gas, ideal for aluminum.
- ⭐ **Stud Welding:** Stud-on-plate fastening, used in shipbuilding.
- ⭐ **HLAW:** Combines laser + GMAW, **single-pass thick plates up to 20 mm**.
- ⭐ **Master Formula:** $H = (V \cdot I \cdot 60) / (S \cdot 1000)$ in **kJ/mm**.
- ⭐ **Arc Efficiency (η):** GMAW ≈ 0.66–0.85, GTAW ≈ 0.40–0.60, SAW ≈ 0.85–0.95.
- ⭐ **Power Density** increases in order: Arc < RSW < PAW < LBW < EBW.
- ⭐ **Robot Suitability Ranking (most to least):** RSW > GMAW > LBW > GTAW > HLAW > FSW (force-limited) > SAW (gantry).
- ⭐ **Industrial sectors:** Automotive → RSW/GMAW; Aerospace → GTAW/LBW/FSW; Shipbuilding → SAW/GMAW/FSW; Energy → SAW/PAW/GTAW.
- ⭐ **Standard parameters to memorize:** WFS, TS, CTWD, RPM, tilt angle, focal position.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arc Welding (MIG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arc Welding (MIG)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MIG (Metal Inert Gas) Welding**, formally designated in engineering standards as **GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding)** by the **American Welding Society (AWS)**, is a fusion welding process in which a **continuous, consumable solid wire electrode** is automatically fed at a controlled rate through a welding gun. An **electric arc** is established between the constantly advancing wire (which acts as both electrode and filler metal) and the workpiece, melting both the wire and the base metal to form a weld pool. The molten weld pool and the arc zone are simultaneously shielded from atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂) by an externally supplied **shielding gas** delivered through a nozzle concentric to the wire guide.

In robotic welding cells, MIG/GMAW is the **dominant process** because of its high deposition rate, suitability for automation, and compatibility with **6-axis articulated robot manipulators**. The standard classification symbol used in **ISO 4063** is **process number 131, 135, 136** (depending on transfer mode).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automatic Fountain Pen" of Welding

Imagine writing with a **fountain pen that continuously refills itself** while you write — the ink (molten wire) flows automatically, the tip never runs dry, and a gentle breeze of protective air (shielding gas) keeps the ink from drying or oxidizing before it touches the page (workpiece). 

That is essentially how MIG welding works:
- **Wire = "Refilling ink cartridge"** — continuously fed from a spool.
- **Arc = "Tip of the pen touching paper"** — heat melts wire + base metal.
- **Shielding gas = "Protective breeze"** — keeps contamination away.
- **Welding gun = "Pen body"** — held by the robot end-effector.

The student should think of MIG as the **"spray-paint gun" of the welding world**: pull the trigger, and a continuous, controllable stream of molten metal is deposited where the robot aims it.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range (Robotic MIG) |
|---|---|
| Shielding Gas | **Argon (Ar)**, **CO₂**, or **Ar/CO₂ mix (75–95% Ar)** |
| Wire Diameter | **0.8 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.2 mm, 1.6 mm** |
| Arc Voltage | **15 V – 32 V** (typical **18–26 V**) |
| Welding Current | **50 A – 500 A** |
| Wire Feed Speed (WFS) | **2 – 20 m/min** |
| Shielding Gas Flow | **10 – 20 L/min** |
| Typical Deposition Rate | **1 – 8 kg/hour** |
| Electrode Polarity | **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** — Standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **MIG ≠ TIG.** A common student error: MIG uses a **consumable wire** (melts to become filler), whereas **TIG (GTAW)** uses a **non-consumable tungsten** electrode with a **separate filler rod** dipped manually or by a wire feeder. The shielding gas chemistry also differs (TIG uses pure Ar; MIG typically uses Ar/CO₂ or Ar/O₂ active mixes).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Sub-Systems of a Robotic MIG Cell

A MIG welding installation is **NOT just a power source and a gun**. In robotic cells (per **KTU Module 2 syllabus**), it is a tightly integrated mechatronic system with **four sub-systems**:

### A. The Power Source (Welding Rectifier / Inverter)
- Modern units are **inverter-based IGBT power sources** (e.g., Lincoln Power Wave, Fronius TPS, Miller Dimension).
- Operates on **Constant Voltage (CV) characteristic** — voltage is held constant; current varies automatically with wire feed speed.
- Provides **DCEP** polarity (~70% of heat on workpiece → deeper penetration).

### B. The Wire Feeder Assembly
- Two main types: **Push-type**, **Pull-type**, and **Push-Pull** (for long distances up to 10 m in robotic cells).
- Drive rolls: **Knurled (V-groove) for steel**, **Smooth U-groove for aluminum**.
- Uses a **4-roll drive** system for stable feeding.

### C. The Welding Gun (Robotic-Mounted)
- **Air-cooled** (up to ~250 A) or **Water-cooled** (above ~300 A, preferred in robotics to reduce cable weight).
- Integrated with **robot end-effector** via a mounting bracket; cables routed through **cable management (dress pack)** to avoid snagging.

### D. Shielding Gas Delivery System
- Gas cylinder → **regulator/flowmeter (L/min)** → **gas hose** → gun nozzle.
- For robotic MIG, **mass flow controllers (MFC)** are preferred for precision.

## 2.2 The Four Modes of Metal Transfer (Most Critical KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal transfer mode** = the manner in which molten metal droplets detach from the electrode tip and travel across the arc to the weld pool. The mode is **controlled by current and voltage settings** and directly determines weld quality, position capability, and heat input.

| Transfer Mode | Current Range | Voltage | Droplet Size | Best For | Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT)** | 50–150 A | 14–20 V | Bridge during short | Thin sheets, low heat | **All positions** |
| **Globular Transfer** | 150–200 A | 22–25 V | Large gravity drops | Non-critical, **flat only** | **Flat / Horizontal** |
| **Spray Transfer (Axial)** | 200–500 A | 25–32 V | Fine stream <wire dia | Thick plates, high productivity | **Flat / Horizontal** |
| **Pulsed Spray Transfer** | 50–300 A (peak) | Pulsed | One droplet per pulse | **Robotic, positional, low-spatter** | **All positions** |

### Why Pulsed Spray Dominates Robotic MIG
- **Pulsed** current alternates between a **high peak current** (detaches droplet) and a **low background current** (maintains arc).
- One electrical pulse → one precisely metered droplet.
- Allows **spray-type transfer** at **low average current** → suitable for **out-of-position welding**, which is critical for complex 3D robotic weld seams.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these **four formulas** — they appear in nearly every Part B question on robotic MIG welding.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **$HI = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$** | V=Voltage, I=Current, S=Travel speed | **kJ/mm** | Heat Input (HAZ control) |
| 2 | **$DR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot WFS \cdot \rho \cdot 60$** | d=wire dia, WFS=wire feed speed, ρ=density | **kg/hr** | Deposition Rate |
| 3 | **$P_{arc} = V \times I$** | Arc Power | **Watts (W)** or **kW** | Power at weld |
| 4 | **$V_{melt} = \dfrac{60 \cdot WFS}{I \times 1000}$** | Melting rate per ampere | **mm/A·s** | Process efficiency |

**Heat Input** determines **HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) width, grain size, and distortion**.
- **Rule of thumb**: For carbon steel, $HI < 1.5$ kJ/mm to avoid sensitization in stainless; typical robotic MIG uses $HI = 0.8 - 2.0$ kJ/mm.
- **Duty cycle** of power source matters: at 60% duty cycle, robot can weld at rated current for 6 of every 10 minutes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive industry**: Body-in-white (BIW) robotic MIG — 4,000+ weld spots per car.
- **Shipbuilding**: Long seam welds on plate panels.
- **Aerospace**: Thin-gauge aluminum welding with pulsed spray.
- **Heavy fabrication**: Thick-plate spray transfer for pressure vessels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input (HI) Formula from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important derivation in Module 2.** Examiners regularly award 4–5 marks just for the correct derivation of Heat Input.

**Starting Concept:** Heat input is the **energy delivered per unit length of weld**. It is computed from the arc power and the travel speed.

**Step 1 — Identify the electrical power supplied to the arc.**

The instantaneous electrical power consumed at the arc is the product of arc voltage $V$ and welding current $I$:

$$P_{arc} = V \times I \quad [\text{in Watts}]$$

where $V$ is measured across the arc (in Volts) and $I$ is the current through the wire (in Amperes).

**Step 2 — Convert to energy per unit time.**

Power in Watts is energy per second. To express energy in **Joules per minute** (the standard time unit in welding because WFS and travel speed are per minute), we multiply by 60 seconds:

$$E_{\text{per min}} = V \times I \times 60 \quad [\text{in J/min}]$$

**Step 3 — Determine the length of weld produced per minute.**

If the robot TCP (Tool Center Point) travels at speed $S$ in **mm/min**, then in 1 minute, the arc traverses a length $S$ mm. So the **energy per millimetre of weld** is:

$$HI_{\text{raw}} = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S} \quad [\text{in J/mm}]$$

**Step 4 — Convert J/mm to kJ/mm for KTU/industry standard.**

Divide by 1000 to switch from Joules to kilojoules:

$$HI = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad [\text{in kJ/mm}]$$

**Step 5 — Apply a thermal efficiency factor $\eta$ for practical use.**

Not all arc energy reaches the workpiece. Approximately **60–85%** is transferred (rest lost as spatter, radiation, fume). AWS standard uses $\eta = 0.8$ for steel:

$$HI_{\text{effective}} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state $\eta = 0.8$ explicitly for carbon steel MIG, or $\eta = 0.6$ for aluminum. Omitting $\eta$ costs 1 mark.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deposition Rate from Wire Geometry

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the consumable wire.**

The wire is a cylinder of diameter $d$. Its cross-sectional area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \quad [\text{m}^2 \text{ or mm}^2]$$

**Step 2 — Volume of wire consumed per minute.**

If the wire feed speed is $WFS$ in **m/min**, the length consumed in 1 minute is $WFS$ metres. Volume per minute:

$$V_{\text{per min}} = A \times WFS \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{min}]$$

**Step 3 — Mass per minute using density $\rho$.**

The density of steel wire $\rho \approx 7.85 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$, aluminum $\rho \approx 2.7 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$m_{\text{per min}} = \rho \times A \times WFS \quad [\text{kg/min}]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/hour for industry reporting.**

Multiply by 60:

$$DR = 60 \times \rho \times A \times WFS \quad [\text{kg/hr}]$$

In expanded form:

$$\boxed{DR = 60 \times \rho \times \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \times WFS}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A robotic MIG cell uses 1.2 mm diameter steel wire, fed at 8 m/min, with arc voltage 24 V, current 220 A, and robot travel speed 0.5 m/min. Calculate the **deposition rate (kg/hr)** and **heat input (kJ/mm)**. (Use $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\eta = 0.8$.)

### Solution — Part (a): Deposition Rate

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.44 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$DR = 60 \times 7850 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times 8$$

$$DR = 60 \times 7850 \times 9.048 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{DR \approx 4.26 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

### Solution — Part (b): Heat Input

$$HI = 0.8 \times \dfrac{24 \times 220 \times 60}{0.5 \times 10^3 \times 1000}$$

$$HI = 0.8 \times \dfrac{317{,}200}{500{,}000}$$

$$HI = 0.8 \times 0.6344$$

$$\boxed{HI \approx 0.508 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** $0.508$ kJ/mm is a **low heat input** — suitable for thin-gauge welding (≤ 3 mm) and low-distortion robotic applications.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Effects on Weld Quality

| If We INCREASE ↓ | Weld Bead Width | Penetration | Spatter | Distortion | Spatter Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage (V)** | Increases ↑ | Slightly ↓ | Slightly ↑ | Increases ↑ | Higher |
| **Current (I)** | Slightly ↑ | Increases ↑↑ | Increases ↑ | Increases ↑ | Higher |
| **Wire Feed Speed (WFS)** | Increases ↑ | Increases ↑ | Increases ↑ | Increases ↑ | Higher |
| **Travel Speed (S)** | Decreases ↓ | Decreases ↓ | Variable | Decreases ↓ | Risk of undercut |
| **Gas Flow Rate** | Marginal | Marginal | **Too low → porosity; too high → turbulence** | Negligible | Critical for shielding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid for KTU viva:** "**V-I-WFS go up together**" — in CV MIG, voltage, current, and WFS are **coupled**. Touch the WFS knob and current follows automatically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Robotic MIG Welding Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Robot Controller Cabinet] -->|Robot Motion Commands| A2[6-Axis Articulated Robot Arm]
    A2 -->|TCP Trajectory| A3[Welding Torch / Gun Mounted on End Effector]
    
    B1[Inverter Power Source CV Mode] -->|Welding Cable DCEP| A3
    B2[Wire Spool ER70S-6] -->|Push Pull Feeder| B3[4-Roll Drive Unit]
    B3 -->|Conduit Liner| A3
    
    C1[Shielding Gas Cylinder Argon CO2 Mix] -->|Regulator| C2[Mass Flow Controller L per min]
    C2 -->|Gas Hose| A3
    
    D1[Workpiece Clamp Table] -->|Earth Return Cable| B1
    
    A3 -->|Establishes Arc| D1
    A3 -->|Shielding Gas Shield| D1
    
    A1 <-->|Fieldbus Ethernet IP DeviceNet| B1
    A1 <-->|Fieldbus| C2
    A1 <-->|Encoder Feedback| B3

    classDef controller fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef power fill:#7d1f1f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef wire fill:#5f4b1f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef gas fill:#1f5f3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef weld fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    class A1,A2 controller
    class B1 power
    class B2,B3 wire
    class C1,C2 gas
    class A3,D1 weld
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — MIG Process Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Operator Programs Robot Path Offline] --> P2[Upload Program via USB or Network]
    P2 --> P3[Robot Controller Executes Motion]
    P3 --> P4[Trigger Signal Sent to Power Source]
    P4 --> P5[Arc Ignition Wire Starts Feeding]
    P5 --> P6[Arc Established Melting Wire Plus Base Metal]
    P6 --> P7[Shielding Gas Flow Initiated]
    P7 --> P8[Weld Pool Formed Under Gas Shield]
    P8 --> P9{Real Time Arc Monitoring Sensors}
    P9 -->|Voltage Current Feedback| P10[Adaptive Control Loop Adjusts WFS and V]
    P10 --> P6
    P9 -->|Anomaly Detected| P11[Stop Signal to Robot and Power Source]
    P11 --> P12[Alarm Logged in Controller]

    classDef process fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef monitor fill:#7d4b1f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef alarm fill:#7d1f1f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8 process
    class P9,P10 monitor
    class P11,P12 alarm
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct Metal Transfer Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{What is the Plate Thickness} -->|Below 3 mm| S2{What is the Weld Position}
    S1 -->|3 to 6 mm| S3{What is the Position}
    S1 -->|Above 6 mm| S4{What is the Position}

    S2 -->|All Position including Vertical and Overhead| S5[Use Short Circuit Transfer]
    S2 -->|Flat or Horizontal| S6[Use Short Circuit or Pulsed Spray]

    S3 -->|All Position| S7[Use Pulsed Spray Transfer]
    S3 -->|Flat or Horizontal| S8[Use Globular or Spray Transfer]

    S4 -->|All Position| S9[Use Pulsed Spray at High Peak Current]
    S4 -->|Flat or Horizontal| S10[Use Conventional Spray Transfer]

    classDef decision fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#1f5f3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    class S1,S2,S3,S4 decision
    class S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10 process
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between MIG (GMAW) and TIG (GTAW) welding processes. Mention at least three points.**

**Model Answer (Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1):**

| # | MIG / GMAW | TIG / GTAW |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Consumable** continuous wire electrode acts as filler | **Non-consumable** tungsten electrode; separate filler rod fed manually or automatically |
| 2 | Shielding gas: **Ar / CO₂ / Ar + CO₂ active mix** | Shielding gas: **Pure Argon** (inert, no reactive component) |
| 3 | Operates on **Constant Voltage (CV)** power source, DCEP | Operates on **Constant Current (CC)** power source, DCEN |
| 4 | Higher deposition rate (1–8 kg/hr), **suitable for automation & robotics** | Lower deposition rate, **superior weld quality**, more operator skill |
| 5 | No flux; spatter is common | No flux, **virtually spatter-free** |

*(Any three points in tabular form: **3 marks**.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**List any three modes of metal transfer in MIG welding and state the current/voltage range for each.**

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

1. **Short-Circuit Transfer:** Current **50–150 A**, Voltage **14–20 V**. Used for thin sheets and all-position welding.
2. **Spray (Axial) Transfer:** Current **>200 A**, Voltage **25–32 V**. Used for thick plates, flat position only.
3. **Pulsed Spray Transfer:** Peak current **200–300 A**, background current **50–80 A**, pulsing frequency **50–500 Hz**. Used for robotic positional welding.

*(Any one missing parameter loses 1 mark each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> **[ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern: Pick ONE of A or B]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **equipment and process setup of a robotic MIG welding cell**. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic MIG welding cell uses **1.0 mm diameter steel wire**, fed at **10 m/min**, with **arc voltage 26 V**, welding current **250 A**, and robot travel speed **0.6 m/min**. Calculate:
  (i) The **deposition rate** in kg/hr. Take density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(4 marks)**
  (ii) The **effective heat input** in kJ/mm. Take thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. **(3 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### (a) Block Diagram & Description (7 marks)

*(Expected answer: a labelled block diagram of the robotic MIG cell — same as Section 4.1 — followed by a brief description of each sub-system.)*

**Description (3 marks for components + 4 marks for description):**

A robotic MIG welding cell consists of the following **four major sub-systems** working in synchronization:

1. **Robot Manipulator (1 mark):** A 6-axis articulated robot (e.g., FANUC ARC Mate, ABB IRB, KUKA KR) carries the welding torch. The Tool Center Point (TCP) follows a programmed path on the workpiece surface. Repeatability is typically **±0.05 mm**.

2. **Welding Power Source (1 mark):** An **inverter-based constant-voltage (CV) source** with IGBT technology, operating in **DCEP** mode. Typical rating: 350–500 A at 60% duty cycle.

3. **Wire Feeder & Consumable (1 mark):** A **push-pull type wire feeder** with 4 drive rolls delivers the consumable electrode (e.g., **ER70S-6** for mild steel, **ER4043 / ER5356** for aluminum) through a conduit liner to the gun.

4. **Shielding Gas System (1 mark):** Argon/CO₂ (80/20) or pure CO₂ for steel; pure Ar for aluminum. Gas is metered by a **mass flow controller (15–20 L/min)** and delivered through the gun nozzle.

5. **Process Integration (1 mark):** The robot controller communicates with the power source and wire feeder over **fieldbus protocols** (EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet, Profinet). **Arc-on/arc-off**, gas pre-flow/post-flow, and crater-fill parameters are coordinated.

##### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Wire diameter $d = 1.0 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Wire feed speed $WFS = 10 \text{ m/min}$
- Arc voltage $V = 26 \text{ V}$
- Welding current $I = 250 \text{ A}$
- Travel speed $S = 0.6 \text{ m/min} = 600 \text{ mm/min}$
- $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\eta = 0.8$

---

**(i) Deposition Rate (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of wire:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (1 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2 — Apply deposition rate formula:

$$DR = 60 \times \rho \times A \times WFS$$

$$DR = 60 \times 7850 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \times 10$$

$$DR = 60 \times 7850 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{DR \approx 3.70 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

---

**(ii) Effective Heat Input (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Apply heat input formula:

$$HI = \eta \times \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

$$HI = 0.8 \times \dfrac{26 \times 250 \times 60}{600 \times 1000}$$

$$HI = 0.8 \times \dfrac{390{,}000}{600{,}000}$$

$$HI = 0.8 \times 0.65$$

$$\boxed{HI \approx 0.52 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **four modes of metal transfer** in MIG welding. For each, state the typical current range and at least one application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **shielding gas composition** in robotic MIG welding. Compare **pure CO₂, pure Argon, and Ar/CO₂ (80/20)** mixes with respect to penetration, spatter, and bead profile. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### (a) Four Modes of Metal Transfer (7 marks)

1. **Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT) — 2 marks:**
   - Current: **50–150 A**; Voltage: **14–20 V**.
   - Wire touches the weld pool (short circuits) → current spike → pinch-off → droplet deposited.
   - **Application:** Thin sheet welding (0.6–3 mm), all positions including vertical and overhead, automotive sheet-metal robotic cells.

2. **Globular Transfer — 1.5 marks:**
   - Current: **150–200 A**; Voltage: **22–25 V**.
   - Large droplets (>wire diameter) form and fall by gravity.
   - **Application:** Limited to **flat/horizontal** position; used in non-critical heavy fabrication, not preferred in robotics.

3. **Spray (Axial) Transfer — 2 marks:**
   - Current: **>200 A** (transition current); Voltage: **25–32 V**.
   - Fine droplets detach axially at a rate of hundreds per second.
   - **Application:** Thick plates (>6 mm), high productivity robotic welding, flat/horizontal position only.

4. **Pulsed Spray Transfer — 1.5 marks:**
   - Background current **50–80 A**, peak current **200–300 A**; pulse frequency **50–500 Hz**.
   - One droplet per pulse — controlled metal transfer.
   - **Application:** **Positional robotic welding**, thin-to-medium plates, low-spatter aerospace and automotive applications.

##### (b) Shielding Gas Comparison (7 marks)

| Property | **Pure CO₂** | **Pure Argon** | **Ar/CO₂ (80/20)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Penetration** | Deep (reactive gas) | Shallow, wide | Medium-deep |
| **Spatter** | **High** | Low | Low-Medium |
| **Bead Profile** | Rough, convex | Smooth, flat | Smooth, slight crown |
| **Wetting Action** | Poor | Excellent | Very Good |
| **Cost** | Cheapest | Most expensive | Moderate |
| **Best Suited For** | Non-critical structural steel | **Aluminum, stainless, non-ferrous** | **General robotic MIG on mild steel** |

- **Pure CO₂** (1.5 marks): Produces deepest penetration because CO₂ dissociates in the arc, releasing oxygen, increasing arc energy. Drawback: high spatter, poor bead cosmetics. Used in heavy structural fabrication.
- **Pure Argon** (1.5 marks): Inert, gives smooth arc, no spatter. Required for MIG welding of **aluminum and stainless steel** because reactive gases would oxidize the weld pool. Cannot be used on steel — produces unstable arc and lack of fusion.
- **Ar/CO₂ (80/20) — Most common in robotics (2 marks):** The de-facto industry standard for robotic MIG of mild steel. Combines the arc stability of Argon with the wetting and penetration benefits of CO₂. Produces excellent bead with minimal spatter, ideal for automated seam tracking and arc-sensing robotic systems.
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** The selection of shielding gas is a **strategic process parameter**, not a commodity. For a typical robotic MIG cell welding mild steel BIW components, **Ar/CO₂ 80/20** is the universal default.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Lossing Mistakes in MIG Exam Answers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency factor $\eta$** in heat input — loses **1 mark** every time it is omitted.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in deposition rate** — writing the answer in kg/min instead of kg/hr. Always multiply by 60.
> 3. **Conflating MIG with TIG** — using "tungsten electrode" for MIG descriptions. **MIG is ALWAYS consumable wire.**
> 4. **Wrong polarity** — MIG standard is **DCEP** (Electrode Positive). Writing DCEN costs 1 mark.
> 5. **In transfer mode answers** — students often miss the **transition current** concept (>200 A needed for spray transfer). Mention this explicitly.
> 6. **Spelling "Metal Inert Gas" as MIG** is technically inaccurate for active mixes (Ar/CO₂ is NOT inert — CO₂ is reactive). The technically correct term is **GMAW**. Use both.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **MIG = GMAW** (Process 131/135/136 in ISO 4063).
- ✅ **Polarity:** DCEP (Electrode Positive, Workpiece Negative).
- ✅ **Power source:** Constant Voltage (CV), inverter-based.
- ✅ **Four transfer modes:** Short-Circuit, Globular, Spray, Pulsed Spray.
- ✅ **Pulsed Spray = Best choice for robotic positional welding.**
- ✅ **Heat Input formula:** $HI = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ kJ/mm.
- ✅ **Deposition Rate formula:** $DR = 60 \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot WFS$ kg/hr.
- ✅ **Shielding gas standard for steel:** Ar/CO₂ (80/20); **For aluminum:** Pure Argon.
- ✅ **Wire diameter** = 0.8 / 1.0 / 1.2 / 1.6 mm; **Common WFS** = 2–20 m/min.
- ✅ **Efficiency factor:** $\eta = 0.8$ for steel, $\eta = 0.6$ for aluminum.
- ✅ **Robotic MIG cell** has 4 sub-systems: Robot, Power Source, Wire Feeder, Gas Delivery.
- ✅ **Fieldbus protocols:** EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet, ProfiNet for robot–source communication.
- ✅ **Wire classification:** ER70S-6 (mild steel), ER4043/ER5356 (aluminum), ER308L (stainless).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Resistance Welding

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Resistance Welding (RW)** is a fusion welding process in which coalescence of metallic surfaces is produced by the heat generated from the resistance offered by the workpiece to the flow of an electric current, with the simultaneous application of pressure (with or without filler metal). The heat liberated follows **Joule's Law of Heating**, and the process is fundamentally governed by the equation:

$$H = I^2 R t$$

where **H** is the heat generated (in Joules), **I** is the welding current (in Amperes), **R** is the contact resistance of the joint (in Ohms), and **t** is the duration of current flow (in seconds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Resistance Welding is a non-arc, non-filler, pressure-dependent welding process classified under *Solid-State / Pressure Welding* in the AWS (American Welding Society) taxonomy. The robotic integration of resistance welding (Spot Welding) forms the backbone of modern automotive assembly lines.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to join two thin sheets of paper using a flat iron (clothes iron). When the heated iron is pressed against the two sheets, the heat softens the cellulose fibers and the mechanical pressure fuses them together. While resistance welding is more sophisticated, the principle is identical:

- **The Flat Iron** = The **Electrodes** (conduct electricity and apply force).
- **The Heat of the Iron** = The **I²R Heat** (generated internally by current flow, not from an external flame or arc).
- **The Sheets of Paper** = **Metal Workpieces** (typically low-carbon steel, stainless steel, or aluminum alloys).
- **The Pressure applied** = **Electrode Force** (essential to forge the softened metal into a solid-state bond).

Unlike arc welding where an external spark melts the metal, in resistance welding the metal heats itself from the inside-out. The current is forced to squeeze through the high-resistance contact area between two pieces, generating intense localized heat that softens (plasticizes) the metal, after which pressure completes the metallurgical bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Arc welding is a *surface heating* process, whereas Resistance welding is a *through-heating / internal heating* process. This makes RW extremely fast, repeatable, and ideal for high-volume robotic automation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Electrical Resistivity of Low-Carbon Steel:** $\rho \approx 10 \text{ to } 15 \, \mu\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$
- **Electrical Resistivity of Aluminum:** $\rho \approx 2.8 \, \mu\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$
- **Electrical Resistivity of Copper (electrodes):** $\rho \approx 1.7 \, \mu\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ — *intentionally low to avoid electrode heating*.
- **Typical Industrial Current Range:** **5,000 A to 20,000 A**
- **Typical Electrode Force Range:** **2 kN to 8 kN**
- **Weld Time (Spot):** **0.1 s to 1.5 s** (in robotic high-speed lines, often < 0.3 s)

## 1.4 Classification of Resistance Welding Processes

| Sub-Process | Joint Geometry | Application Domain |
|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** | Lap joint, localized nugget | Automotive body-in-white (BIW) |
| **Resistance Seam Welding (RSEW)** | Lap joint, continuous leak-tight seam | Fuel tanks, radiators |
| **Resistance Projection Welding (RPW)** | Lap joint, pre-formed embossments | Cross-wire welding, nut-to-plate |
| **Resistance Butt Welding (RBW)** | Butt joint, uniform cross-section | Wire, rod, bar stock, rail welding |
| **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)** | Butt joint, with arcing flash-off | Heavy sections, automotive axles, pipelines |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heat Generation Mechanism (Joule's First Law)

The fundamental physics of resistance welding is rooted in **Joule's Law of Electrical Heating**. When an electric current *I* flows through a conductor of resistance *R* for a time *t*, the heat generated is:

$$H = I^2 R t \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

For a practical resistance welding system, however, the resistance *R* is not a single fixed value. It is a **dynamic, time-varying combination** of three distinct resistive zones. This is captured in the **Heat Balance Equation**:

$$H_{total} = I^2 R_{total} \, t = H_{weld} + H_{loss}$$

where $R_{total}$ is the sum of:

$$R_{total} = 2 R_{electrode} + 2 R_{electrode-work} + R_{bulk} + 2 R_{work-work}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why:** The "useful heat" that creates the weld nugget is concentrated at the **work-to-work interface** ($R_{work-work}$). The other resistances (electrode bulk, electrode-work contact) are parasitic heat sinks that must be minimized through proper electrode material selection (Class 1, Class 2 RWMA copper alloys) and surface conditioning.

## 2.2 Stages of a Spot Weld (Temporal Evolution)

1. **Squeeze Time ($t_s$):** Electrodes descend and apply force *before* current flows. Ensures intimate mechanical contact. Typical: **0.1 – 0.4 s**.
2. **Weld Time ($t_w$):** Current flows; heat accumulates; metal plasticizes and forms a **nugget**.
3. **Hold Time ($t_h$):** Current is OFF, but pressure is maintained. Allows the nugget to **cool under pressure**, preventing porosity and expulsion of molten material.
4. **Off Time ($t_o$):** Electrodes retract; the next welding cycle begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle Time (Robotic Productivity Metric):** $T_{cycle} = t_s + t_w + t_h + t_o$. In automotive lines, a typical robotic spot weld cycle is **0.6 to 1.2 seconds**, enabling a throughput of 60+ welds/minute per robot.

## 2.3 The Concept of Weldability Lobe

The **Weldability Lobe** (also called the **Current-Time Lobe**) is a graphical representation of the relationship between welding current and welding time that produces acceptable welds. It is bounded by two critical curves:

- **Minimum Current Curve (Lower Bound):** Below this, insufficient heat is generated; the nugget is too small (cold weld).
- **Expulsion Curve (Upper Bound):** Beyond this, excessive heat causes molten metal to be ejected from the joint, leaving voids (porosity, weak weld).

The optimal operating point lies in the **center of the lobe**, providing maximum process tolerance.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Joule's Heat Equation** | $H = I^2 R t$ | J, A, $\Omega$, s | Foundation of all RW processes |
| 2 | **Heat Balance Equation** | $H_{total} = I^2 R_{total} t$ | J | Accounts for heat losses |
| 3 | **Pupinated Heat Balance** | $H = I^2 R t = H_{weld} + H_{electrode} + H_{cooling} + H_{radiation}$ | J | Distribution of energy |
| 4 | **Resistance of a Cylinder** | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | $\Omega$ | Predicts $R_{bulk}$ |
| 5 | **Power Input** | $P = I^2 R$ | W (Watts) | Instantaneous heat rate |
| 6 | **Nugget Diameter (Empirical)** | $d_n = 4 \sqrt{\dfrac{F}{\pi H_{material}}}$ | mm | Used in projection welding |
| 7 | **Nugget Diameter (RSW empirical)** | $d_n \approx 0.5 d_{electrode}$ | mm | Quick design rule |
| 8 | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) width** | $W_{HAZ} = K \sqrt{\alpha t_w}$ | m | Diffusion-based growth |
| 9 | **Sheet-Thickness Rule (Min Current)** | $I_{min} \approx 8000 \cdot t^{0.5}$ | A | For low-carbon steel (t in inches) |
| 10 | **Electrode Force (Rule of thumb)** | $F \approx 2000 \cdot d_{nugget}$ | N | Empirical thumb rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions frequently test the **Heat Balance Equation** and the ability to compute heat distribution. Memorize the relative magnitude: **70–80%** of $H_{total}$ goes to the workpieces, **5–15%** to electrodes, and the remainder is lost to cooling water, radiation, and conduction into fixtures.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** A modern passenger car contains **4,000 – 5,000 resistance spot welds**. Robotic resistance welding cells with servo-gun technology achieve dimensional accuracy of **$\pm 0.1$ mm**.
- **Battery Manufacturing:** Pouch cells and cylindrical cell tabs (lithium-ion batteries) are joined using **ultrasonic-assisted resistance welding** to avoid thermal damage to electrolytes.
- **Aerospace & Rail:** Flash butt welding is used for continuous rail strings; **Thermit welding** (a related process) is used as backup.
- **White Goods:** Refrigerator cabinets, washing machine drums — all use robotic RSW.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Distribution in a Spot Weld

**Problem Statement (Canonical KTU Problem):**
> *A single-phase AC resistance spot welding machine delivers a current of 8,000 A (RMS) for 0.2 s through a stack-up of two 1.2 mm low-carbon steel sheets. The contact resistance at the interface is 200 $\mu\Omega$, the bulk resistance of the two sheets combined is 100 $\mu\Omega$, and the electrode-to-work contact resistance is 50 $\mu\Omega$ per side. Calculate the total heat generated, and the fraction of useful heat that appears at the work-to-work interface.*

### Step 1: Identify the Resistive Network

The total resistance of the welding circuit is the series sum:

$$R_{total} = 2 R_{electrode-work} + R_{bulk} + R_{work-work}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$R_{total} = 2(50 \, \mu\Omega) + 100 \, \mu\Omega + 200 \, \mu\Omega$$

$$R_{total} = 100 + 100 + 200 = 400 \, \mu\Omega = 400 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega$$

### Step 2: Apply Joule's Law to Compute Total Heat

$$H_{total} = I^2 R_{total} t$$

$$H_{total} = (8000)^2 \times (400 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.2$$

$$H_{total} = 64 \times 10^6 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2$$

$$H_{total} = 64 \times 10^6 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$H_{total} = 5{,}120 \, \text{J}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Useful Heat (Work-to-Work Interface)

The useful heat is the heat generated at $R_{work-work}$ only:

$$H_{useful} = I^2 R_{work-work} t = (8000)^2 \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.2$$

$$H_{useful} = 64 \times 10^6 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2$$

$$H_{useful} = 2{,}560 \, \text{J}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Heat Efficiency

$$\eta_{heat} = \dfrac{H_{useful}}{H_{total}} = \dfrac{2560}{5120} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

### Step 3.1: Verification of the Heat Balance

The non-useful heat is:

$$H_{loss} = H_{total} - H_{useful} = 5120 - 2560 = 2560 \, \text{J}$$

This 50% loss occurs in the electrode bulk, electrode-work interfaces, and cooling water. KTU 2024 scheme expects students to discuss this efficiency. Note that the bulk of the "useful" heat ultimately is also conducted away into the surrounding metal — so the actual fraction of heat that forms the nugget is closer to **20–30%** of the total input. This is the concept of the **"practical heat efficiency"** of the RW process.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Parameters on Weld Quality

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Heat | Effect on Weld Quality | Effect on Electrode Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Welding Current (I)** | Quadratic increase ($I^2$) | Larger nugget, risk of expulsion | Severe reduction (pitting, mushrooming) |
| **Weld Time (t)** | Linear increase | Deeper fusion, wider HAZ | Mild reduction |
| **Electrode Force (F)** | Reduces $R_{contact}$, decreases heat | Smaller, denser nugget | Strongly increases |
| **Hold Time ($t_h$)** | No heat (current off) | Reduces porosity, shrinkage cracks | Mild increase |
| **Squeeze Time ($t_s$)** | No heat | Ensures intimate contact, fewer spatters | Mild increase |
| **Electrode Tip Radius** | Increases contact area, lowers $R$ | Larger, shallower nugget | Significant increase |
| **Sheet Surface (clean/oily)** | Alters $R_{contact}$ unpredictably | Inconsistent nuggets | Variable |
| **Material Resistivity (high)** | Higher heat | Better weldability (e.g., SS vs Al) | Mild effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The relationship is **NOT linear**. A 10% increase in current causes a **~21% increase in heat** (since $H \propto I^2$). This is why current control is the *most sensitive* lever in RW.

## 3.3 Derivation of Nugget Diameter (Empirical Model)

Based on heat balance and thermal diffusion theory, the nugget diameter for a spot weld on a single sheet of thickness *t* is approximately:

$$d_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \eta_{heat} I^2 R_{interface} t_{weld}}{\pi \rho_s c_s (T_{melt} - T_{ambient}) t}}$$

where $\rho_s$ is density, $c_s$ is specific heat, and $T_{melt}$ is the melting point. Although this expression is rarely used in numerical KTU problems, it illustrates that **nugget diameter scales with $\sqrt{I^2 t}$** — i.e., the **heat input** parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hook:** KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks: *"Why is current control preferred over time control in robotic resistance welding?"* — Answer: The square-law dependence ($H \propto I^2$) gives finer process control and faster response than the linear time dependence, especially with modern **closed-loop constant-current (CCC) feedback** systems using **primary or secondary current sensors**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Resistance Spot Welding Cell

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the architecture of an industrial robotic RSW station, mapping the functional interactions between components (since physical drafting is replaced by block-level architecture as per protocol).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robot Controller - PLC/CPU] --> B[Servo Gun Controller]
    A --> C[Weld Timer - MFDC or AC]
    A --> D[Safety Interlock PLC]
    B --> E[Servo Motor and Gearbox]
    E --> F[Servo Gun Weld Head]
    F --> G[Upper Electrode - Class 2 Cu]
    F --> H[Lower Electrode - Class 2 Cu]
    G --> I[Workpiece - Lap Joint]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Fixture and Clamp Table]
    C --> K[Thyristor / IGBT Inverter Bridge]
    K --> L[Welder Transformer - 50 Hz or 1 kHz MFDC]
    L --> F
    D --> M[Light Curtain and E-Stop]
    D --> N[Pressure Transducer Feedback]
    F --> N
    C --> O[Current Sensor - Rogowski Coil]
    O --> C
    J --> P[Part Presence Sensor - Limit Switch]
    P --> A
```

### Description of the Functional Flow

1. **Robot Controller (A):** The master PLC sequences the welding cycle. It receives part-present signals from the fixture (P) and triggers the weld timer (C) when the gun is in position.
2. **Weld Timer (C):** Modern systems use **Medium-Frequency DC (MFDC)** technology — a 1 kHz inverter chops the 50 Hz mains, rectifies it, and feeds the welding transformer. This produces a smoother, more controllable current waveform than legacy AC.
3. **Welder Transformer (L):** Steps down voltage (typically 380 V → 8 V) while stepping up current to 5,000–20,000 A.
4. **Servo Gun (B, E, F):** A **motorized** weld gun where a servo motor (instead of a pneumatic cylinder) applies electrode force. This enables precise force control and real-time adaptive welding.
5. **Closed-Loop Feedback (N, O):** Pressure and current are continuously monitored, allowing adaptive control algorithms to compensate for sheet thickness variation, electrode wear, and stack-up tolerances.

## 4.2 Schematic: Heat Flow Zones During a Spot Weld

```mermaid
graph TD
    Z1[Zone 1: Electrode Bulk Heat Loss] --> Z2[Zone 2: Electrode-Work Contact]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3: Sheet Bulk Conduction]
    Z3 --> Z4[Zone 4: Work-Work Interface - NUGGET FORMS HERE]
    Z4 --> Z5[Zone 5: Heat Affected Zone - HAZ]
    Z5 --> Z6[Zone 6: Unaffected Base Metal]
    Z4 --> Z7[Nugget Growth - Solidification Front]
    Z7 --> Z5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to *draw or describe* a spot weld cross-section, students must correctly label: **(1) Nugget**, **(2) HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone)**, **(3) Parent Metal (Unaffected Zone)**, and **(4) Indentation** at the electrode contact surface. The interface between the two sheets shows the characteristic **dendritic columnar grain structure** of the cast nugget.

## 4.3 Process Flow: Robotic Spot Welding Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Robot Traverse to Weld Point] --> S2[Step 2: Verify Part Presence in Fixture]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Lower Gun - Squeeze Phase]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Build Up Electrode Force]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Initiate Current - Weld Phase]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Monitor Current and Force in Real Time]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Cut Off Current - Hold Phase]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Retract Gun - Off Phase]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Increment Weld Counter and Log Data]
    S9 --> S10{More Welds Required?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S1
    S10 -- No --> S11[Step 11: Robot Traverse to Next Station]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*Define Resistance Welding. State Joule's law of heating and explain the significance of each variable in the context of robotic resistance spot welding.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Resistance welding is a pressure welding process in which the heat required for coalescence is generated by the resistance of the workpiece to the flow of an electric current, with the simultaneous application of pressure. The heat generated is given by:

$$H = I^2 R t$$

- **I** (current, A): Controlled by the welding transformer. Typical industrial values are 5,000 – 20,000 A. Current has a **quadratic** effect on heat.
- **R** (resistance, $\Omega$): The dynamic total resistance comprising electrode bulk, electrode-work contact, sheet bulk, and work-work contact. The work-work contact resistance is the **useful resistance** that creates the nugget.
- **t** (time, s): Weld time, typically 0.1 – 1.5 s in robotic applications.

In robotic RSW, all three parameters are monitored by closed-loop sensors for adaptive process control.

### Question 2: Process Comparison

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Differentiate between Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) and Resistance Seam Welding (RSEW). Mention two industrial applications of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | RSW | RSEW |
|---|---|---|
| Joint Type | Discrete spot welds | Continuous overlap weld |
| Electrodes | Point electrodes (truncated cone / dome) | Wheel electrodes (roller) |
| Current Application | Pulsed (single shot) | Continuous or intermittent (roller rotation) |
| Application | Car body panels, sheet metal cabinets | Fuel tanks, radiators, oil drums |
| Pressure | Static | Dynamic (roller contact) |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Or Choice 1`

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the working principle of **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)**. Explain the role of electrodes and the concept of squeeze, weld, and hold time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robotic spot welding machine operates at a current of 10,000 A for a weld time of 0.15 s. The interface resistance is 250 $\mu\Omega$ and the total resistance is 600 $\mu\Omega$. Calculate:
- (i) The total heat generated
- (ii) The useful heat at the work interface
- (iii) The heat efficiency

Discuss why this efficiency is the *theoretical maximum* and how it is affected in real welding conditions.

### **Model Answer A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Working Principle of RSW (4 Marks):**

In resistance spot welding, two overlapping sheets are clamped between two copper electrodes. A high current (5,000 – 20,000 A) at low voltage (typically 4 – 12 V) is passed through the stack-up. The highest resistance in the circuit occurs at the **work-to-work interface** because the surface oxides and micro-asperities create a constriction resistance. This localized high resistance generates intense heat per Joule's law, raising the temperature above the melting point of the base metal. Pressure applied through the electrodes forces the molten material to fuse, forming a **nugget**. After current cutoff, pressure is maintained during the hold time to allow the nugget to solidify without porosity.

**Cross-Section Sketch Description (1 Mark):**

The cross-section shows: (i) upper and lower electrodes, (ii) two sheets in lap configuration, (iii) the circular **nugget** at the interface, (iv) the surrounding **HAZ** with refined grains, (v) the **unaffected parent metal**, and (vi) a slight **indentation** at the electrode contact.

**Role of Electrodes and Timing (2 Marks):**

- **Electrodes:** Made of Class 2 RWMA copper-chromium or copper-zirconium alloys. They must have **low resistivity** to minimize heat loss, **high thermal conductivity** to extract heat quickly, and **high hardness** to resist deformation.
- **Squeeze Time:** Allows the electrodes to seat firmly and establish stable contact resistance.
- **Weld Time:** Duration of current flow. Determines nugget size.
- **Hold Time:** Current is OFF, but force is maintained. Allows solidification under pressure, preventing porosity and cracks.

### **Model Answer A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- $I = 10{,}000 \text{ A}$
- $t = 0.15 \text{ s}$
- $R_{interface} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega$
- $R_{total} = 600 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega$

**(i) Total Heat Generated (2 Marks):**

$$H_{total} = I^2 R_{total} t = (10{,}000)^2 \times (600 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.15$$

$$H_{total} = 10^8 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.15$$

$$H_{total} = 10^8 \times 9 \times 10^{-5} = 9{,}000 \, \text{J}$$

**(ii) Useful Heat at Interface (2 Marks):**

$$H_{useful} = I^2 R_{interface} t = (10{,}000)^2 \times (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.15$$

$$H_{useful} = 10^8 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.15$$

$$H_{useful} = 10^8 \times 3.75 \times 10^{-5} = 3{,}750 \, \text{J}$$

**(iii) Heat Efficiency (1 Mark):**

$$\eta = \dfrac{H_{useful}}{H_{total}} = \dfrac{3750}{9000} = 0.4167 = 41.67\%$$

**Discussion (2 Marks):**

The computed efficiency of **41.67%** is the *theoretical electrical efficiency* — the fraction of total heat appearing at the work interface. In practice, the *thermal efficiency* (heat actually raising the nugget above the melting point) is much lower, typically **20–30%**, because:

- A large fraction of the useful heat is **conducted away** by the surrounding bulk metal.
- **Cooling water** in the electrode holders removes additional heat.
- **Radiation and convection** losses occur from the hot surfaces.
- **Electrode mushrooming** and surface contamination alter the contact resistance.

Therefore, in real robotic RW, only 20–30% of the input electrical energy is effectively used to melt the metal at the interface. This is why MFDC technology, closed-loop control, and adaptive algorithms are critical in modern welding cells.

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `Or Choice 2`

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the **Weldability Lobe** in resistance spot welding. Draw a typical current–time diagram and identify the regions of under-welding, acceptable welding, and expulsion. Discuss how this lobe shifts with changes in electrode force and material thickness.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Resistance Butt Welding (RBW)** and **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)** with respect to:
- (i) Current and voltage requirements
- (ii) Heat source and melting mechanism
- (iii) Weld quality and applications
- (iv) Suitability for robotic automation

### **Model Answer B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Weldability Lobe Concept (3 Marks):**

The **Weldability Lobe** (also known as the *Current-Time Lobe* or *Lobe Curve*) is a process window that defines the combinations of welding current and welding time that produce a sound, defect-free spot weld of a given diameter.

The lobe is bounded by two curves:

- **Lower Bound — Minimum Current Curve:** Below this curve, the heat input is insufficient to form a nugget of the required minimum diameter. The result is a *cold weld* with low shear strength.
- **Upper Bound — Expulsion Curve:** Beyond this curve, the heat input is so high that molten metal is forcibly ejected from the joint interface. The resulting weld exhibits porosity, voids, and reduced strength.

The **optimal operating point** lies in the geometric center of the lobe, providing the **largest process tolerance** to disturbances such as sheet thickness variation or surface contamination.

**Effect of Electrode Force (2 Marks):**

Increasing electrode force **lowers the contact resistance** $R_{contact}$ at the work-work interface. This shifts both curves to the **right** — i.e., higher currents are required to achieve the same heat input, but the **width of the lobe increases**, providing greater process robustness.

**Effect of Material Thickness (2 Marks):**

For thicker sheets, the bulk resistance increases, and more heat is conducted away. Both curves shift **rightward and upward**, requiring higher currents and/or longer times. The lobe also becomes **narrower**, indicating reduced process window — making robotic adaptive control more critical.

### **Model Answer B(b) — 7 Marks**

| Criterion | RBW (Resistance Butt Welding) | FBW (Flash Butt Welding) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Current and Voltage** | Low voltage (4–10 V), moderate current; continuous current during upset | Very high current (50–200 kA for heavy sections), arcing voltage |
| **(ii) Heat Source** | Resistance heating at the abutting interface | Arc + resistance heating; flashing action vaporizes surface |
| **(iii) Melting Mechanism** | Both pieces heated simultaneously across the entire cross-section | Localized flashing melts the interface progressively; ~30–50% of metal is flashed off as sparks |
| **(iv) Weld Quality** | Good, but may have a planar grain boundary; susceptible to oxide inclusions | Excellent — flashing expels oxides and contaminants; forge-welded grain structure |
| **(v) Applications** | Wire, rod, small-diameter bars, band-saw blades | Rails, large-diameter pipes, automotive axle shafts, ring-rolling |
| **(vi) Robotic Suitability** | Limited — requires precise alignment and high upset force; mostly manual/semi-automatic | Excellent — modern flash butt welding is highly automated, used in continuous rail-welding trains |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing RBW with FBW:** Students often mix up "resistance" and "flash" butt welding. Remember: **FBW involves an arc (flash)**; RBW does not. FBW's superior weld quality arises because the **flashing action expels oxides** from the joint — this is a *key exam point*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Heat Balance:** When asked to compute heat, many students write $H = I^2 R t$ and forget that $R$ is a **sum of multiple resistive zones** — this costs 2–3 marks in KTU 2024 evaluations.
> 3. **Units Confusion:** Resistance is often in **micro-ohms ($\mu\Omega$)** in KTU problems. A slip of $10^6$ in unit conversion is the single most common numerical error.
> 4. **Weldability Lobe Sketch:** If asked to draw, ensure both curves are labeled and the **expulsion curve** is above the **minimum current curve**. The intersection region is the *acceptable welding zone*.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Resistance welding uses **internal I²R heat** + **electrode pressure** to create a fusion bond.
- **Master Formula:** $H = I^2 R t$ — and remember $R = R_{electrode} + R_{contact} + R_{bulk} + R_{interface}$.
- **Stages:** Squeeze → Weld → Hold → Off (cycle time = sum of all four).
- **Weldability Lobe:** Two-curve window bounded by **minimum current** (lower) and **expulsion** (upper).
- **Heat Efficiency:** Theoretical ~50%; practical **20–30%**.
- **Current Magnitude:** 5,000 – 20,000 A (single spot), up to **200,000 A** (flash butt).
- **Electrode Material:** Class 2 RWMA copper alloys (low resistivity, high thermal conductivity).
- **Modern Trend:** **MFDC (Medium-Frequency DC)** welders + **Servo Guns** + **Closed-Loop Current/Force Control** = Industry 4.0 standard.
- **Robotic Integration:** RSW is the **most automated** welding process globally; 4,000–5,000 welds per car body.
- **Comparison Table:** Know RSW vs RSEW vs RBW vs FBW — KTU loves to ask 14-mark comparison questions.
- **Key Industrial Constant:** $\rho_{Cu} = 1.7 \, \mu\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ (electrodes), $\rho_{Steel} = 10{-}15 \, \mu\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ (workpiece).
- **Squeeze Time Rule:** Never start current before force is fully applied — spatter and cracks result.
- **Hold Time Rule:** Always maintain force after current cutoff — prevents porosity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laser Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** is a high-precision, non-contact fusion welding process that uses the concentrated thermal energy of a **coherent, monochromatic, and collimated** photon beam to join metallic (or thermoplastic) workpieces by creating a localized melt pool, typically with the assistance of a vapor-filled cavity known as the *keyhole*. When integrated with an articulated robot, the technique is classified as **Robotic Laser Welding (RLW)** — a key high-speed joining method in modern Industry 4.0 manufacturing cells.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Laser Welding falls under the broader umbrella of **high-energy-density (HED) welding processes** and is differentiated from conventional arc welding by:
- Extremely high power density (**10⁶ – 10⁷ W/cm²**)
- Minimal Heat Affected Zone (**HAZ**)
- Low distortion and high depth-to-width ratio of the weld bead

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Laser Welding is treated as a non-contact, single-pass, deep-penetration welding method suitable for **robotic automation** because the laser beam (no electrode wear, no tool change) can be delivered via fiber optics to a robot-mounted scanner or focusing head.

## 1.2 Key Terminology for Robotic Laser Welding

| Term | Meaning | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Coherence** | Photons in phase (temporal + spatial) | Allows beam to stay focused over long distances |
| **Monochromaticity** | Single wavelength output | Predictable absorption by workpiece |
| **Collimation** | Low beam divergence | Maintains energy density at focal point |
| **Focal Point** | Smallest cross-section of the beam | Region of maximum power density |
| **Keyhole** | Vapor capillary formed by recoil pressure | Enables deep penetration welding |
| **Numerical Aperture (NA)** | Light-gathering ability of fiber | Critical for fiber-delivered systems |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Sunlight Through a Magnifying Glass"

Imagine holding a magnifying glass on a bright sunny day. When the glass is far from the paper, nothing happens. But when you lower it to the **focal point**, the light energy compresses into a tiny dot, igniting the paper. This is *exactly* how a laser welder works — but instead of sunlight, a **synthesized, coherent beam** is compressed through a special lens onto a metallic surface, vaporizing a micro-cavity (the *keyhole*) and melting the surrounding metal. A robot moves this "magnifying glass" along the joint with millimeter precision, joining parts at speeds unattainable by arc welding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering insight:** In an automotive BIW (Body-in-White) line, a single 6-axis robot equipped with a fiber laser (e.g., 4 kW IPG fiber laser) can weld a **galvanized steel roof-to-side panel** joint at 6–8 m/min with a penetration depth of 3 mm in a single pass — a job that would require 2–3 MIG passes.

## 1.4 Common Industrial Lasers Used in Robotic Welding

| Laser Type | Wavelength | Fiber-Deliverable? | Typical Robotic Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ Laser** | **10.6 μm** | ❌ No (mirror-based) | Heavy-section steel cutting, shipbuilding |
| **Nd:YAG Laser** | **1.064 μm** | ✅ Yes | Stainless steel, aerospace alloys |
| **Fiber Laser (Ytterbium-doped)** | **1.07 μm** | ✅ Yes (best) | Automotive sheet welding (most common) |
| **Disc Laser** | **1.03 μm** | ✅ Yes | Thick-section automotive & rail |
| **Diode Laser** | **0.8–0.98 μm** | ✅ Yes | Plastic welding, brazing, cladding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why fiber lasers dominate robotic cells:** Their short wavelength (≈ **1.07 μm**) is absorbed much better by metals than the 10.6 μm of CO₂ lasers (which are mostly reflected by aluminum/steel). They also have wall-plug efficiencies of 30–40% versus 10–15% for CO₂, drastically reducing operating cost.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (for KTU problems)

| Constant / Metric | Value / Range | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light, **c** | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Photon energy equation |
| Planck's constant, **h** | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Photon energy |
| Wavelength, **λ** | 0.8 – 10.6 μm | Determines metal absorption |
| Power density at focus | **10⁶ – 10⁷ W/cm²** | Keyhole formation threshold |
| Typical focal spot diameter | 0.2 – 0.6 mm | Penetration control |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

Laser welding operates on the principle of **photon-to-thermal energy conversion at a micro-focal zone**. The sequence is:

1. **Beam Generation:** A gain medium (e.g., Yb-doped fiber) is pumped (electrically or by diode) to produce **population inversion**. Photons of wavelength λ are amplified via *stimulated emission* and emerge as a coherent beam.

2. **Beam Delivery:** The laser is routed through a **fiber-optic cable** (or articulated mirror arm for CO₂) to a focusing head mounted on the robot's end-effector.

3. **Focusing:** A collimating lens and a focusing lens reduce the beam diameter at the workpiece surface to a *focal spot* of diameter **d_f** (typically 0.2–0.6 mm).

4. **Energy Absorption:** When photons strike the metal, their energy (E = hν = hc/λ) is absorbed by free electrons and converted to lattice vibrational energy → **heat**.

5. **Keyhole Formation:** Once power density exceeds ≈ **10⁶ W/cm²**, the metal vaporizes. The vapor's *recoil pressure* digs a deep, narrow cavity (the **keyhole**) into the workpiece.

6. **Multiple Reflections inside Keyhole:** The beam bounces *several times* off the keyhole walls, dramatically increasing the effective absorption (from ~30% at the surface to >90% deep inside the keyhole).

7. **Melt Pool Solidification:** As the robot moves the beam along the joint, the molten metal on the keyhole walls flows around and solidifies behind, forming a **narrow, deep, fish-bone shaped weld**.

## 2.2 Why Laser Welding is Special — The "Why" Behind the Process

- **High depth-to-width ratio:** A 4 kW fiber laser can penetrate 6–8 mm of steel in a single pass with a bead width of only 1–2 mm → less filler, less distortion.
- **Low HAZ:** Because the energy is concentrated, surrounding material barely heats up → preserves mechanical properties of advanced high-strength steels (AHSS) used in cars.
- **No contact:** No electrode, no wear, no spatter → ideal for **hygienic welding** (food-grade, medical).
- **Robotic compatibility:** The beam is "massless" → the robot can move at very high speeds (>10 m/min) with high acceleration without inertia penalties.

## 2.3 Two Operating Modes of Laser Welding

| Mode | Power Density | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conduction Mode** | < 10⁶ W/cm² | Surface melting only; shallow, wide bead | Thin sheets, electronics |
| **Keyhole (Penetration) Mode** | **> 10⁶ W/cm²** | Vapor capillary + multiple internal reflections | **Most robotic welding (>90% of RLW cells)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The transition from conduction to keyhole mode occurs when the power density exceeds the **evaporation threshold** of the material. For steel, this threshold is approximately **10⁶ W/cm²**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU ESE questions. Master these and the derivations that follow.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of a single photon | h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3×10⁸ m/s, λ in m |
| 2 | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d_f^2}$ | **Power density at focal spot** (W/m²) | P = laser power (W), d_f = focal spot diameter (m) |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot P}{v}$ | **Heat input per unit length** (J/m) | η = absorption efficiency, P = power (W), v = travel speed (m/s) |
| 4 | $BPP = \theta \cdot \omega_0$ | Beam Parameter Product (mm·mrad) | θ = half-angle divergence, ω₀ = beam waist radius |
| 5 | $d_f = \dfrac{4 f \lambda M^2}{\pi D}$ | **Focused spot diameter** for a TEM₀₀ beam | f = focal length, λ = wavelength, M² = beam quality, D = collimated beam diameter |
| 6 | $Z_R = \dfrac{\pi \omega_0^2}{M^2 \lambda}$ | **Rayleigh length** (depth of focus) | ω₀ = waist radius, M² = beam quality |
| 7 | $A_{abs} = 1 - R$ | Absorptivity of metal surface | R = reflectivity (0 to 1) |
| 8 | $\eta = 0.30 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda_{CO_2}}{\lambda}\right)$ (approx.) | Relative absorption efficiency with wavelength | For comparing CO₂ vs fiber lasers |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Tesla, BMW, and Volkswagen use **robotic fiber laser welding** for joining AHSS door rings and battery tray enclosures. The high speed eliminates multiple robot stations.
- **Aerospace:** Welding of titanium and Inconel turbine casings where HAZ must be tightly controlled.
- **Battery Manufacturing:** Tab welding and can-to-cap sealing for lithium-ion cells — laser welding gives hermetic seals without internal contamination.
- **Shipbuilding & Heavy Fabrication:** CO₂ laser + 6-axis robot for thick-plate joining, replacing submerged arc welding (SAW) for certain seams.

> [!NOTE]
> The penetration depth (P_d, depth) in keyhole mode can be approximated as proportional to **(P / v)^0.7** for a given material — a useful empirical rule of thumb used in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Focused Spot Diameter of a Gaussian Laser Beam

**Given:** A laser beam of wavelength λ, initial (collimated) diameter D, beam quality factor M², and focusing lens of focal length f.

**To Derive:** The diameter d_f of the focused spot on the workpiece.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Start with the far-field divergence of a Gaussian beam**

For an ideal (M² = 1) Gaussian beam, the half-angle divergence is:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi \omega_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the beam quality factor M²**

For a real beam, divergence is degraded by the factor M²:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{M^2 \lambda}{\pi \omega_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the focused spot radius in terms of focal length and divergence**

The focused spot radius is the product of the focal length and the half-angle divergence:

$$
\omega_f = f \cdot \theta = f \cdot \dfrac{M^2 \lambda}{\pi \omega_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate the initial beam radius (ω_initial = D/2) to the focused spot via the lens equation**

For a thin lens, the relationship between the input collimated beam radius and focused spot radius (when the beam waist is at the lens) is:

$$
\omega_f = \dfrac{M^2 \lambda f}{\pi \omega_{initial}} = \dfrac{M^2 \lambda f}{\pi (D/2)} = \dfrac{2 M^2 \lambda f}{\pi D}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to spot diameter (d_f = 2 ω_f)**

$$
d_f = 2 \omega_f = 2 \cdot \dfrac{2 M^2 \lambda f}{\pi D} = \dfrac{4 M^2 \lambda f}{\pi D}
$$

### Final Result:

$$
\boxed{\,d_f = \dfrac{4 f \lambda M^2}{\pi D}\,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** To obtain a small focal spot (high power density), you must (a) use a **short-wavelength laser** (fiber < CO₂), (b) use a **short focal length lens**, and (c) **expand the collimated beam diameter D** (use a beam expander).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Density at the Focal Spot

**Given:** Laser power P, focal spot diameter d_f (assumed circular).

**To Derive:** Power density (W/m²) at the focal point.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define power density**

Power density is power divided by the area over which it acts:

$$
P_d = \dfrac{P}{A_{focal}}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the focal spot area (circular)**

$$
A_{focal} = \pi \left(\dfrac{d_f}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the power density equation**

$$
P_d = \dfrac{P}{\pi d_f^2 / 4} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d_f^2}
$$

### Final Result:

$$
\boxed{\,P_d = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d_f^2}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU problems, you will often be asked to verify whether a given combination of laser power and spot size produces **keyhole mode** (P_d > 10⁶ W/cm²) or **conduction mode** (P_d < 10⁶ W/cm²).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Input Formula for Laser Welding

**Given:** Laser power P, absorption efficiency η, travel speed v.

**To Derive:** Heat input H (J/mm or J/m).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of heat input in welding**

Heat input is the energy delivered per unit length of the weld:

$$
H = \dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Length}}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the time spent per unit length**

The time taken to traverse a length L at speed v is:

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{v}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the energy delivered in that time**

The actual energy absorbed by the workpiece is the product of laser power, efficiency, and time:

$$
E = \eta \cdot P \cdot t = \eta \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by length to get heat input per unit length**

$$
H = \dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot P \cdot (L/v)}{L} = \dfrac{\eta P}{v}
$$

### Final Result:

$$
\boxed{\,H = \dfrac{\eta P}{v}\,}
$$

**Units:** If P is in Watts (J/s) and v is in mm/s, then H is in **J/mm** (preferred in welding). If P in W and v in m/s, H is in **J/m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with Arc Welding:** For MIG/MAG welding, H = (V × I × 60) / (v × 1000) J/mm where V is voltage, I is current. Notice that **the same equation form applies** — it's a universal welding concept, but the **η value differs drastically**: η_laser ≈ 0.3–0.6 (keyhole) vs η_arc ≈ 0.7–0.85.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy of a Laser Photon (KTU Favourite)

**Given:** Wavelength λ of the laser.

**To Derive:** Energy E of a single photon emitted.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Planck's relation**

Energy is proportional to frequency:

$$
E = h \nu
$$

**Step 2 — Replace frequency using c = νλ → ν = c/λ**

$$
E = h \cdot \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the constants**

$$
E = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{\lambda}
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{\lambda} \;\; \text{J}
$$

### Example — Nd:YAG laser (λ = 1.064 μm = 1.064 × 10⁻⁶ m):

$$
E = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.064 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.868 \times 10^{-19} \;\; \text{J} \approx 1.17 \;\; \text{eV}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The photon energy of a fiber laser (1.17 eV) is **~10× higher** than a CO₂ laser photon (0.117 eV). Higher photon energy means electrons in the metal absorb the photon more readily — this is **why fiber lasers are absorbed ~3× better by steel** than CO₂ lasers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (for KTU Numericals)

> [!IMPORTANT]
