# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define Offline Programming (OLP) in the context of robotic welding. List any four advantages over online teach-pendant programming."**

**Model Answer:**

Offline Programming (OLP) is a computer-aided technique in which the welding robot program is generated, simulated, and optimized in a virtual 3D environment using specialized software, without occupying the physical robot or the production cell. The validated program is then transferred to the robot controller for execution.

**Four advantages over online teach-pendant programming:**

1. **Zero production downtime** — programming is done in parallel with shop-floor production.
2. **Higher path accuracy** — CAD-driven trajectories achieve ±0.5 mm accuracy versus ±2–5 mm for manual teach.
3. **Automatic collision detection** — software identifies and flags torch–fixture or robot–workpiece interference before execution.
4. **Cycle time optimization** — motion planners compute the fastest collision-free path, reducing total weld time.
5. *Optional fifth:* Easier programming for complex 3D curves and multi-robot cells.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is a digital twin in robotic welding simulation? Explain its role in OLP."**

**Model Answer:**

A **digital twin** is a virtual, real-time, 1:1 software replica of the physical robotic welding cell — including the robot, controller, welding torch, positioner, fixture, workpiece, and safety fencing.

**Role in OLP:**
- Provides a **risk-free sandbox** to develop and validate weld programs before deployment.
- Enables **what-if analysis** (e.g., changing torch angle to test penetration).
- Supports **predictive maintenance** by simulating wear on virtual joints.
- Facilitates **reverse engineering** of legacy programs by replaying actual kinematics.
- Acts as a **single source of truth** for engineering, production, and quality teams.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Offline Programming system for robotic welding. Describe the function of each module. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward kinematics of a 6-DOF articulated welding robot using the Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) convention. Write the general DH transformation matrix and explain each parameter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

The OLP architecture consists of **five functional modules** interconnected in a sequential workflow:

**1. CAD Import Module:**
- Accepts neutral CAD formats (STEP, IGES, Parasolid, CATIA).
- Converts B-Rep geometry into triangulated mesh for rendering.
- Generates the workpiece mesh with weld-seam candidate edges.

**2. Kinematic Modeling Module:**
- Houses the robot library with DH parameters for every supported model (e.g., ABB IRB 1520ID, KUKA KR 5 R1400).
- Builds the forward and inverse kinematic chains.
- Stores joint limits, velocity limits, and payload data.

**3. Trajectory Planning Module:**
- Converts selected weld seams into discrete target points (typically 2–10 mm spacing).
- Selects interpolation modes (MoveL for straight welds, MoveC for curved welds, MoveJ for air moves).
- Inserts weld process events (arc-on, arc-off, wire feed, gas pre-flow, post-flow).

**4. Simulation & Validation Module:**
- Performs **collision detection** between all moving bodies.
- Checks **reachability** of every target against the robot's workspace envelope.
- Computes **cycle time** using dynamic motion models.
- Detects **singularities** by monitoring the Jacobian determinant.

**5. Post-Processing Module:**
- Translates the abstract program into vendor-specific native language (RAPID, KRL, INFORM, Karel).
- Adds headers, initialization routines, and safety interlocks.
- Outputs a single file ready for transfer to the robot controller.

The **block diagram** is presented in Section 4 (Diagram 1) of these notes.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Step 1 — DH Convention Recap:**
For each joint $i$, four parameters define the relative transformation from link $i-1$ to link $i$:
- $\theta_i$ — joint angle (rotation about $z_{i-1}$).
- $d_i$ — link offset (translation along $z_{i-1}$).
- $a_i$ — link length (translation along $x_i$).
- $\alpha_i$ — link twist (rotation about $x_i$).

**Step 2 — DH Transformation Matrix:**
The general homogeneous transformation for joint $i$ is:

$$
A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Forward Kinematics Equation:**
The TCP pose relative to the base frame is:

$$
T_{0}^{6} = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdot A_3(\theta_3) \cdot A_4(\theta_4) \cdot A_5(\theta_5) \cdot A_6(\theta_6)
$$

**Step 4 — TCP Position Extraction:**
The position vector $(x_{TCP}, y_{TCP}, z_{TCP})$ is the top-right 3×1 sub-vector of $T_0^6$, and orientation is the upper-left 3×3 rotation matrix.

**Step 5 — Application in OLP:**
Given any set of six joint angles, OLP software evaluates $T_0^6$ in real time and renders the TCP position graphically. This powers the **virtual motion** seen by the engineer during simulation.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of cell calibration in OLP. Describe any one method (e.g., 3-point method or laser tracker method) used to align the virtual cell with the real cell. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a singularity in robotic welding? Using the Jacobian determinant, derive the condition for singularity and list three common singularity configurations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Concept of Cell Calibration:**
Cell calibration is the process of determining the **6-DOF offset (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw)** between the virtual coordinate system (in the OLP software) and the real coordinate system (in the shop-floor cell). Without calibration, the program generated offline would be misaligned by several millimeters, causing seam tracking errors, porosity, and undercuts.

**3-Point Method (commonly used):**
The engineer defines **three reference points** on a fixed fixture in the real cell:
- **Point 1 (Origin):** Selected on a known datum feature (e.g., a dowel pin hole).
- **Point 2 (X-axis):** A second point along the X-axis, at least 100 mm away.
- **Point 3 (Y-axis):** A third point establishing the XY plane.

The TCP is moved to these three physical points using the teach pendant, and the corresponding coordinates are recorded. The OLP software computes the **rotation-translation matrix** $[R \vert t]$ that maps the virtual frame to the real frame:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_{real} \\ y_{real} \\ z_{real} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3 \times 3} & t_{3 \times 1} \\ 0_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} x_{virtual} \\ y_{virtual} \\ z_{virtual} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The resulting offset is stored as a **user frame** or **workobject** in the robot program.

**Laser Tracker Method (advanced):**
A laser tracker (e.g., Leica AT960) measures the 3D position of a retro-reflective target mounted on the robot TCP at multiple configurations. Software fits the measured positions to the kinematic model and computes a correction vector that updates the robot's base frame parameters — achieving **Absolute Accuracy (AA)** of ±0.5 mm.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Definition of Singularity:**
A singularity is a robot configuration in which the robot loses one or more degrees of freedom in Cartesian space, causing the joint velocities to become theoretically infinite for a finite TCP velocity. Practically, this results in erratic motion, joint faults, and emergency stops.

**Derivation of Singularity Condition:**
The geometric Jacobian $J(\vec{\theta})$ maps joint velocities to TCP velocities:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v_{TCP} \\ \omega_{TCP} \end{bmatrix} = J(\vec{\theta}) \cdot \dot{\vec{\theta}}
$$

Inverting:

$$
\dot{\vec{\theta}} = J^{-1}(\vec{\theta}) \cdot \begin{bmatrix} v_{TCP} \\ \omega_{TCP} \end{bmatrix}
$$

A singularity occurs when $J$ becomes non-invertible, i.e.:

$$
\det(J(\vec{\theta}_{sing})) = 0
$$

In practice, the OLP software flags configurations where $\vert \det(J) \vert < 10^{-6}$ as **near-singular** and inserts a flip-point to avoid them.

**Three Common Singularity Configurations in Welding Robots:**

1. **Wrist Singularity** — When the axes of joints 4, 5, and 6 become collinear (typically $\theta_5 = 0°$). The TCP cannot rotate about the tool Z-axis.

2. **Elbow Singularity** — When the arm is fully extended or fully folded, causing the wrist center to lie on the axis of joint 2. The robot cannot translate the TCP in a particular direction.

3. **Shoulder Singularity** — When the wrist center, joint 1 axis, and joint 2 axis become collinear. The robot cannot move the TCP tangentially to the base.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "path accuracy" with "pose repeatability." They are independent metrics.
> - **Do NOT skip** the Jacobian determinant derivation — stating just "J becomes singular" without showing $\det(J) = 0$ costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Do NOT use** online programming terminology when the question asks about offline programming — they are opposite paradigms.
> - **Always mention** at least one commercial OLP software (RobotStudio / KUKA.Sim / Delmia / RoboGuide) in Part B answers for full marks.
> - **Always draw** the block diagram — answers without diagrams lose at least 2 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — "Read This the Night Before Exam":**

### Core Definitions
- ⭐ **OLP** = Virtual generation of robot programs (no physical robot needed).
- ⭐ **Digital Twin** = Software replica of the physical welding cell.
- ⭐ **TCP** = Tool Center Point (reference origin at welding torch tip).
- ⭐ **User Frame / Workobject** = Local coordinate system attached to the workpiece.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- ⭐ $T_{0}^{6} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4 \cdot A_5 \cdot A_6$ — Forward kinematics.
- ⭐ $\det(J(\vec{\theta})) = 0$ — Singularity condition.
- ⭐ $AA = \sqrt{AP^2 + RP^2 + CC^2}$ — Accuracy relationship.
- ⭐ $t_{cycle} = \sum \frac{\Delta s_i}{v_i} + \frac{L_{air}}{v_{air,max}} + P \cdot t_{dwell}$ — Cycle time.
- ⭐ $\dot{\vec{\theta}} = J^{-1} \cdot [v; \omega]$ — Inverse velocity mapping.

### Software Platforms (Name-drop in Answers)
- ⭐ **ABB RobotStudio**, **KUKA.Sim**, **Yaskawa Motoman PC**, **Fanuc RoboGuide**, **Delmia**, **Robotmaster**, **Visual Components**, **MotoSim**.

### Robot Languages Generated by Post-Processors
- ⭐ **RAPID** (ABB), **KRL** (KUKA), **INFORM** (Yaskawa), **Karel** (Fanuc), **PDL2** (Comau), **AS Language** (Nachi).

### Comparison Table to Remember
- ⭐ Online teach-pendant vs. Offline programming — 6 distinguishing parameters.
- ⭐ Path accuracy (±0.5 mm) vs. Absolute accuracy (±1–3 mm) vs. Repeatability (±0.05 mm).

### Must-Mention Concepts in 14-Mark Answers
- ⭐ **DH parameters** ($\theta, d, a, \alpha$).
- ⭐ **Collision detection, reachability check, cycle time optimization, singularity avoidance**.
- ⭐ **Calibration methods** (3-point / laser tracker).
- ⭐ **Weld seam types** (butt, fillet, lap, corner, curved 3D).
- ⭐ **Interpolation modes** (MoveL, MoveJ, MoveC).

### Examiner Hot Buttons
- ⭐ Mention software names → +1 mark.
- ⭐ Draw a block diagram → +2 marks.
- ⭐ Cite a numerical example → +1 mark.
- ⭐ Use industry accuracy numbers (±0.5 mm, ±1 mm) → +1 mark.

### Common Student Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing online vs. offline programming.
- ❌ Saying "OLP uses AI" — it does not, it uses deterministic kinematic solvers.
- ❌ Skipping the Jacobian when discussing singularities.
- ❌ Writing "TCP is the welding torch" — TCP is the *reference point*, not the physical torch.
- ❌ Forgetting units (mm vs. cm, rad vs. deg) — KTU strictly checks units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Parameters and Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welding Parameters and Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Welding Parameters** in the context of robotic arc welding are defined as the set of controlled, quantifiable process variables — both **electrical** (voltage, current) and **mechanical** (travel speed, wire feed rate, torch geometry) — that govern the formation of the weld pool, dictate the metallurgical transformation of the joint, and ultimately determine the mechanical integrity and geometric conformity of the finished weld seam.

**Quality Control (QC)** in robotic welding refers to the systematic, planned application of monitoring, inspection, testing, and feedback mechanisms — coordinated through the robot controller and supervisory PLC/PC systems — to ensure that the produced welds consistently conform to the requirements defined in the **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)**, **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)**, and applicable international codes such as **ISO 3834**, **AWS D1.1**, or **ASME Section IX**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In robotic welding, parameters are **not** set manually per pass as in hand welding. They are pre-programmed via **Offline Programming (OLP)** or **Teach Pendant**, and are *automatically* regulated in real-time using adaptive control loops. This is the fundamental distinction students must articulate in the ESE.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cooking on a Conveyor Belt" Model

Imagine you are the head chef in a restaurant where every dish (weld) must taste **identical** to the one served yesterday, even though the kitchen operates at a 200 m/s production line. You cannot *taste* the food mid-cook. You must instead:

| Cooking Analogy | Robotic Welding Parameter |
|---|---|
| Gas burner flame size | **Voltage / Arc Length** |
| Amount of sauce poured | **Wire Feed Speed (WFS)** |
| Conveyor belt speed | **Travel Speed (TS)** |
| Height of nozzle above pan | **Contact Tip-to-Work Distance (CTWD)** |
| Oil/heat distributed | **Shielding Gas Flow Rate** |
| Final plating inspection | **NDT / Bead Geometry Checks** |

Just as a chef writes a **recipe** (WPS) and a **tasting panel** validates each dish (Quality Control), robotic welding requires a digitally codified procedure and a layered inspection regime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Automotive OEMs (e.g., Hyundai, Tata Motors) produce **over 4,000 spot/seam welds per vehicle body**. Even a **2% drift** in any single parameter can cause failure of structural crash tests. Hence parameter control and QC are *non-negotiable* in mass production robotic welding cells.

### 1.3 Classification of Welding Parameters

Robotic welding parameters are broadly classified into **three hierarchical categories**:

1. **Primary (Set) Parameters** — Directly programmed by the operator:
   - Voltage (V), Current (I), Wire Feed Speed (WFS), Travel Speed (TS), Shielding Gas Flow Rate, CTWD, Torch Angle, Arc Length.

2. **Secondary (Derived) Parameters** — Computed from primary set values:
   - Heat Input (HI), Deposition Rate (DR), Bead-on-Plate Geometry (width, height, leg length), Dilution Percentage, Arc Energy.

3. **Adaptive (Corrective) Parameters** — Real-time feedback-modulated:
   - Arc current fluctuation, Trugap voltage tracking, Through-the-Arc-Sensing (TAC) current variance, Touch-Sense Offset Corrections, Laser/Vision Seam Tracking Offsets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Process Parameters — Operational Logic

#### 2.1.1 Arc Voltage (V)

- **Definition:** The potential difference maintained between the electrode (wire) tip and the workpiece across the arc column.
- **Units:** Volts (V), typically **18 – 32 V** for GMAW/GTAW robotic arcs.
- **Influence:** Directly proportional to arc length. Higher V → flatter, wider bead with shallow penetration; Lower V → tall, narrow bead with deeper penetration.
- **Sensor mechanism:** Trugap (Adaptive arc length controller) modulates wire feed to hold V constant.

#### 2.1.2 Welding Current (I)

- **Definition:** Rate of charge flow through the arc; primarily regulated by **Wire Feed Speed** in CV (Constant Voltage) GMAW systems.
- **Units:** Amperes (A); typical robotic GMAW range **100 – 400 A**.
- **Influence:** Governs metal deposition rate, penetration depth, and heat input. The relationship $I \propto \text{WFS}$ is approximately linear for a given wire diameter.

#### 2.1.3 Wire Feed Speed (WFS)

- **Definition:** Linear rate at which the consumable electrode is fed into the arc.
- **Units:** m/min or in/min.
- **Influence:** Determines melting rate. For **1.2 mm** mild steel wire, **1 m/min ≈ 200 A** (approximate empirical relationship).

#### 2.1.4 Travel Speed (TS) / Welding Speed

- **Definition:** Linear speed at which the robot torch advances along the joint.
- **Units:** mm/s, mm/min, or in/min.
- **Influence:** **Inversely proportional** to heat input and bead size. Excessive TS → lack of fusion; Insufficient TS → burn-through, excessive HAZ grain growth.

#### 2.1.5 Shielding Gas Flow Rate

- **Definition:** Volume of inert/active gas (Ar, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix) delivered per unit time to protect the weld pool.
- **Units:** L/min (typical: **10 – 20 L/min** for GMAW).
- **Influence:** Inadequate flow → porosity, oxidation. Excessive flow → turbulence, gas entrapment, arc instability.

#### 2.1.6 Contact Tip-to-Work Distance (CTWD)

- **Definition:** Static distance between the tip of the contact tube and the workpiece surface.
- **Units:** mm (typical: **10 – 20 mm**).
- **Influence:** Affects stick-out length, which governs resistive heating of the wire (I²R effect). Increase CTWD → preheats wire → changes deposition characteristics.

### 2.2 Secondary / Derived Parameters — Mathematical Core

#### 2.2.1 Heat Input (HI)

The single most critical derived parameter in welding metallurgy. It quantifies the **arc energy delivered per unit length of weld**:

$$\text{HI} = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{TS \cdot 1000} \cdot 60 \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

where:
- $\eta$ = arc efficiency factor (GMAW ≈ **0.80**, GTAW ≈ **0.60**, SAW ≈ **1.00**).
- $V$ = arc voltage (V).
- $I$ = welding current (A).
- $TS$ = travel speed (mm/min).
- The factor $\frac{60}{1000}$ converts W·s/mm → kJ/mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Most students lose marks by **omitting the efficiency factor** $\eta$ or **mixing up units** of TS (mm/min vs mm/s). Always state the units explicitly.

#### 2.2.2 Deposition Rate (DR)

$$\text{DR} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot \text{WFS} \cdot \rho \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{min}}\right]$$

where $d$ = wire diameter (mm), $\rho$ = wire material density (kg/mm³ × 10⁶ for typical mild steel).

#### 2.2.3 Dilution (D)

$$\% D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \cdot 100$$

where $A_{\text{base}}$ and $A_{\text{filler}}$ are the cross-sectional areas of melted base metal and filler metal respectively, measured from a macroetched weld cross-section.

#### 2.2.4 Bead Geometry Relationships (Butt Weld Leg Length)

For a single-V groove butt joint with included angle $\theta$ and root gap $g$:

$$A_{\text{groove}} = \frac{t^2}{2} \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot g$$

where $t$ = plate thickness. This area, when divided by travel speed, gives the **volume of weld metal required per second** — directly used for setting WFS.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input | $\eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{TS \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $\eta$ depends on process |
| 2 | Deposition Rate | $\frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \text{WFS} \cdot \rho$ | kg/min | $\rho$ in consistent units |
| 3 | Dilution | $\frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \cdot 100$ | % | From macro-section |
| 4 | Groove Area (V-groove) | $\frac{t^2}{2}\tan(\theta/2) + t \cdot g$ | mm² | For filler volume calc |
| 5 | Travel Speed (from WPS) | $TS = \frac{\text{Wire consumed per pass}}{\text{Length}} \cdot k$ | mm/min | $k$ = empirical factor |
| 6 | Power Input | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) | Without efficiency |
| 7 | Arc Energy Density | $\frac{V \cdot I}{\text{Spot Area}}$ | W/mm² | For spot/laser hybrid |
| 8 | Stiffness (Butt Joint) | $\frac{EA}{L}$ | N/mm | Used in distortion analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coding Tip:** Since the markdown pipe `|` cannot appear inside table cells, any absolute-value notation (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) must use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` command as shown.

### 2.4 Quality Control Frameworks

Quality in robotic welding is enforced through a **four-tier pyramid**:

1. **Tier 1 — Pre-Weld QC:** WPS verification, base metal certification, fixture check, gas purity test, wire cleanliness.
2. **Tier 2 — In-Process QC:** Real-time arc monitoring (TAC), seam tracking (laser/arc sensor), parameter logging via PLC/SCADA, statistical alarms.
3. **Tier 3 — Post-Weld NDT:** Visual inspection (VT), Liquid Penetrant (PT), Magnetic Particle (MT), Ultrasonic (UT), Radiographic (RT), Eddy Current (ET).
4. **Tier 4 — Destructive Testing:** Tensile, Bend, Impact (Charpy V-notch), Macroetch, Hardness survey.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards Mapping:** KTU syllabus mandates familiarity with **ISO 3834** (Quality Requirements for Fusion Welding), which defines three levels of quality: *Comprehensive (ISO 3834-2)*, *Standard (ISO 3834-3)*, and *Elementary (ISO 3834-4)*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Heat Input from First Principles

We start from the **definition of energy**: Energy $E$ = Power × Time.

For an electric arc, the **electrical power delivered** to the workpiece is:

$$P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{V} \cdot \text{A}]$$

However, **not all** this power enters the weld as useful heat. A fraction is lost as **radiation**, **convection from spatter**, and **sparks**. This is captured by the **arc transfer efficiency** $\eta$, a process-dependent constant:

$$P_{\text{useful}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

The **time spent welding** a segment of length $L$ at travel speed $TS$ is:

$$t = \frac{L}{TS}$$

where $L$ is in mm and $TS$ is in mm/min → therefore $t$ is in minutes.

The total heat energy deposited in segment $L$ is:

$$E = P_{\text{useful}} \cdot t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{L}{TS} \quad [\text{W} \cdot \text{min}]$$

To convert **W·min** to **Joules** (J), multiply by 60:

$$E = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{L}{TS} \cdot 60 \quad [\text{J}]$$

Heat input per unit length is therefore:

$$\text{HI} = \frac{E}{L} = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{TS} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

To convert J/mm to kJ/mm, divide by 1000:

$$\boxed{\text{HI} = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{TS \cdot 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Trace:** W·min × 60 = J, then J/mm ÷ 1000 = kJ/mm. This is the *only* unit-correction sequence accepted by KTU examiners.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem Type)

**Problem Statement:** A robotic GMAW cell welds **6 mm thick mild steel plates** in a butt joint using **Ar/CO₂ (80/20) shielding gas**. The following parameters are logged from the WPS:

- Arc Voltage $V = 28$ V
- Welding Current $I = 220$ A
- Travel Speed $TS = 450$ mm/min
- Arc Efficiency for GMAW: $\eta = 0.80$

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the **Heat Input** in kJ/mm.
(b) If the WPS mandates **HI ≤ 1.0 kJ/mm** to avoid excessive grain growth in the HAZ, determine whether the current setting complies.
(c) If non-compliant, calculate the **minimum permissible travel speed** that would bring HI down to the limit.

---

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Heat Input Calculation**

We substitute into the derived formula:

$$\text{HI} = 0.80 \cdot \frac{28 \cdot 220 \cdot 60}{450 \cdot 1000}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$0.80 \cdot 28 \cdot 220 \cdot 60 = 0.80 \cdot 369600 = 295680$$

Compute the denominator:

$$450 \cdot 1000 = 450000$$

Therefore:

$$\text{HI} = \frac{295680}{450000} = 0.657 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Answer (a): HI ≈ 0.657 kJ/mm**

---

**Part (b) — Compliance Check**

Compare with WPS limit:

$$0.657 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \;\; \mathbf{<} \;\; 1.0 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \quad \checkmark \text{ COMPLIANT}$$

---

**Part (c) — Minimum Travel Speed for HI ≤ 1.0 kJ/mm**

Rearrange the HI formula to solve for $TS$:

$$TS_{\min} = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{\text{HI}_{\max} \cdot 1000}$$

Substituting:

$$TS_{\min} = 0.80 \cdot \frac{28 \cdot 220 \cdot 60}{1.0 \cdot 1000} = 0.80 \cdot \frac{369600}{1000} = 0.80 \cdot 369.6$$

$$TS_{\min} = 295.68 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

**Answer (c): $TS_{\min} \approx 295.7$ mm/min**

**Interpretation:** Travel speed must be **≥ 295.7 mm/min**. The current setting of 450 mm/min *exceeds* this minimum, meaning the process is already well within the safe HI envelope. Any reduction below 295.7 mm/min would risk HAZ grain coarsening.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Weld Defect → Parameter Culprit → Correction

This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU ESE Part B questions on defect diagnosis.

| Defect Observed | Primary Parameter Suspect | Root Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Porosity (gas pockets) | Shielding gas flow rate | Insufficient coverage | Increase Ar flow to 15–18 L/min; check hose leaks |
| Undercut (groove at toe) | Travel speed too high / Voltage too high | Insufficient filler deposition | Reduce TS by 10%; reduce V by 1–2 V |
| Lack of fusion | Travel speed too high / CTWD too large | Heat didn't penetrate groove | Lower TS, reduce CTWD, increase I |
| Excessive spatter | Voltage too high / Gas composition poor | Unstable arc | Reduce V; switch to 80/20 Ar/CO₂ from 100% CO₂ |
| Burn-through | Travel speed too low / Excessive current | Too much heat input | Increase TS; reduce I and V proportionally |
| Cracking (Hydrogen) | Preheat temperature too low / Moisture | Diffusible hydrogen | Apply preheat to 150 °C; bake consumables |
| Distortion / Warping | High HI / Poor fixturing | Excessive thermal expansion | Reduce HI; use strong-back fixtures; balanced welding sequence |
| Inconsistent bead width | Torch angle variation / Worn tip | Robot path drift | Re-calibrate TCP; replace contact tip |
| Lack of penetration | Low current / High travel speed | Insufficient arc force | Increase I by 10–15%; reduce TS |
| Sagging on vertical-up | Travel speed too slow | Gravity dominates melt pool | Use **pulsed GMAW**; reduce I; weave pattern |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Cue:** Whenever a question gives a "weld defect photograph description" in the exam, expect a **7-mark sub-question** that requires tracing the defect → parameter → corrective action using exactly this kind of matrix logic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Robotic Welding Quality Control — Functional Architecture Flow

The following block diagram illustrates the **closed-loop architecture** of a modern robotic welding cell's quality control system. It maps parameter input → execution → sensing → feedback → decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Offline Programming OLP Station] --> B[Welding Procedure Specification WPS]
    B --> C[Robot Controller Upload]
    C --> D[Robotic Manipulator 6 Axis]
    D --> E[Torch TCP Tool Center Point]
    E --> F[Weld Pool Formation]
    F --> G1[Arc Voltage Sensor Trugap]
    F --> G2[Through Arc Sensor TAC Current]
    F --> G3[Laser Vision Seam Tracker]
    F --> G4[Torch Oscillation Sensor]
    G1 --> H[Real Time Parameter Logger PLC]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I{Quality Threshold Check}
    I -- Pass --> J[Continue Welding Sequence]
    I -- Fail --> K[Alarm Stop and Notify SCADA]
    J --> L[Post Weld NDT Stage]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Visual Inspection VT]
    L --> N[Ultrasonic Testing UT]
    L --> O[Radiographic Testing RT]
    M --> P[Statistical Process Control SPC Database]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{Quality Acceptance ISO 3834}
    Q -- Pass --> R[Component Released to Assembly]
    Q -- Fail --> S[Rework or Reject Disposition]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow follows a **clockwise logic** from programming (top-left) → execution (middle) → sensing (right) → decision (bottom-right) → feedback to archive (bottom). The **diamond-shaped decision nodes** (`{}`) represent Boolean checks; the **rounded rectangles** represent process stages.

---

### 4.2 Process Parameter Dependency Tree (Cause-Effect Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Power Source Inverter] --> B[Arc Voltage V]
    A --> C[Welding Current I]
    D[Wire Feeder Servo Motor] --> E[Wire Feed Speed WFS]
    E --> C
    B --> F[Arc Length Control]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Weld Pool Geometry]
    G --> H[Bead Width]
    G --> I[Penetration Depth]
    G --> J[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    K[Robot Path Planner] --> L[Travel Speed TS]
    L --> G
    M[Shielding Gas Regulator] --> N[Gas Flow Rate]
    N --> O[Porosity and Oxidation]
    P[Torch Positioner] --> Q[Torch Angle]
    Q --> G
    P --> R[CTWD]
    R --> G
    G --> S[Heat Input HI]
    S --> J
    S --> T[Distortion and Residual Stress]
```

**Reading:** Notice how **Voltage + WFS** both flow into **Arc Length Control**, which is the central node governing weld pool geometry. Any KTU question asking "which parameters affect bead width?" must trace backwards through this tree to **Voltage, WFS, TS, and Torch Angle** simultaneously.

---

### 4.3 NDT Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weld Inspection Required] --> B{Defect Type Suspected}
    B -- Surface Cracks --> C[Liquid Penetrant PT]
    B -- Surface and Near Surface --> D[Magnetic Particle MT]
    B -- Subsurface Volume Defect --> E{Radiographic Sensitive}
    E -- Yes --> F[Radiographic RT]
    E -- No / Thick Section --> G[Ultrasonic UT]
    B -- Surface Conductivity --> H[Eddy Current ET]
    C --> I[Acceptance per AWS D1.1]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Quality Decision}
    J -- Accept --> K[Component Cleared]
    J -- Reject --> L[Repair Rework Loop]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reference:** Magnetic Particle Testing (MT) is only applicable to **ferromagnetic materials**. Eddy Current (ET) is primarily used for **non-ferrous** and **surface defects in austenitic stainless welds**. Forgetting this metallurgical constraint is a common mark-loss point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
**Define Heat Input in arc welding. List the primary process parameters that influence it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Heat Input is the amount of useful thermal energy delivered by the welding arc per unit length of weld, expressed in kJ/mm.

$$\text{HI} = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{TS} \cdot \frac{60}{1000} \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Primary Influencing Parameters (1.5 marks):**
- Arc Voltage (V)
- Welding Current (I)
- Travel Speed (TS)
- Arc Efficiency ($\eta$) — process-dependent

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
**What is Through-the-Arc Sensing (TAC)? How does it assist in robotic weld quality control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TAC Definition (1.5 marks):** Through-the-Arc Sensing is a real-time seam tracking technique that uses the welding arc itself (no external sensor) as a transducer. By measuring the lateral variation in welding current as the torch oscillates across the joint centerline, the robot controller computes joint offset and corrects the path.

**Quality Control Role (1.5 marks):**
- Compensates for **thermal distortion** and **fixturing tolerance stack-up**.
- Eliminates need for pre-machined precision edges.
- Maintains **constant CTWD and arc length**, ensuring **stable bead geometry**.
- Closes the adaptive control loop in real time.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

---

#### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**  
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an automated robotic welding quality control system**. Describe the role of each subsystem in ensuring ISO 3834 compliance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic GMAW cell is to weld **8 mm thick low-carbon steel** plates in a **single-V butt joint** with a **60° included angle** and **2 mm root gap**. The WFS is **8 m/min** with **1.2 mm wire**. Determine:
  (i) Wire cross-sectional area and theoretical deposition rate (assume $\rho = 7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/mm³).
  (ii) Total weld metal volume required per meter of joint.
  (iii) Time required to deposit this volume.

---

**Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) Architecture Explanation (7 marks):**

| Subsystem | Role |
|---|---|
| **WPS Database** | Stores qualified welding procedures; uploads parameter sets to controller |
| **Robot Controller** | Executes the programmed path; coordinates axes and I/O |
| **Power Source** | Delivers V/I with closed-loop regulation (inverter-based) |
| **Wire Feeder** | Maintains constant WFS via servo drive feedback |
| **Gas Console** | Mass flow control of shielding gas; pre/post-flow timing |
| **Arc Sensor (TAC)** | Real-time joint tracking using arc current modulation |
| **Vision System** | Optional laser-based seam profile scanner for thick plates |
| **PLC + SCADA** | Parameter logging; SPC; alarm management |
| **NDT Station** | Offline inspection (UT, RT, MT, PT) for end-of-batch validation |

**ISO 3834 Compliance Mapping:** The system implements clauses **3834-2 §6 (Equipment)**, **§7 (Personnel)**, **§9 (Process Control)**, and **§10 (Inspection & Testing)** through digital traceability. Each weld carries a unique **barcode/RFID tag** storing time-stamped parameter logs for full **batch traceability**.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Wire Area and Deposition Rate (3 marks):**

$$A_{\text{wire}} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.2)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.44}{4} = 1.131 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

Converting WFS from m/min to mm/min:

$$\text{WFS} = 8 \times 1000 = 8000 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

Volumetric deposition rate:

$$Q = A_{\text{wire}} \cdot \text{WFS} = 1.131 \cdot 8000 = 9048 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

Mass deposition rate:

$$\text{DR} = 9048 \cdot 7.85 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0710 \;\; \text{kg/min} \approx 71 \;\; \text{g/min}$$

---

**(ii) Weld Volume per Meter (2 marks):**

For single-V groove with $\theta = 60°$, $t = 8$ mm, $g = 2$ mm:

$$A_{\text{groove}} = \frac{t^2}{2} \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + t \cdot g$$

$$\tan(30°) = 0.5774$$

$$A_{\text{groove}} = \frac{64}{2} \cdot 0.5774 + 8 \cdot 2 = 32 \cdot 0.5774 + 16 = 18.48 + 16 = 34.48 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

Volume per meter of joint:

$$V = 34.48 \times 1000 = 34480 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{m}$$

---

**(iii) Time Required (2 marks):**

$$t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{34480}{9048} = 3.81 \;\; \text{min/m}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 3 \;\; \text{min} \;\; 49 \;\; \text{s} \;\; \text{per meter of joint}}$$

---

#### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**  
**(a)** Enumerate the **eight most common weld defects** observed in robotic GMAW, and for each, identify the **process parameter** that primarily causes it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** in robotic welding, explain:
  (i) The difference between **process capability indices Cp and Cpk**, with their formulas.
  (ii) How **X-bar and R control charts** are constructed for monitoring weld bead width.
  (iii) The significance of **Western Electric rules** for detecting out-of-control conditions.

---

**Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) Eight Common GMAW Defects (7 marks):**

| # | Defect | Primary Parameter Cause |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Porosity | Insufficient shielding gas |
| 2 | Undercut | Excess voltage / travel speed |
| 3 | Lack of fusion | Insufficient current |
| 4 | Lack of penetration | High travel speed |
| 5 | Burn-through | Excess current / slow travel |
| 6 | Spatter | High voltage / impure gas |
| 7 | Crater cracks | Improper ramp-down |
| 8 | Distortion | High heat input |

---

**(b) SPC Explanation (7 marks):**

**(i) Cp vs Cpk (3 marks):**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \;\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

- $C_p$ measures **process spread** only (assumes centered process).
- $C_{pk}$ measures **process centering + spread** (real-world capability).
- KTU convention: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is required for **welding-critical** automotive dimensions.

**(ii) X-bar and R Chart (2 marks):**
- Sample $n = 5$ consecutive welds, measure bead width $X_i$.
- $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$; $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$.
- Plot $\bar{X}$ on X-bar chart with UCL/LCL = $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_2 \bar{R}$.
- Plot $R$ on R chart with UCL = $D_4 \bar{R}$, LCL = $D_3 \bar{R}$.
- $A_2, D_3, D_4$ are standard constants from **AIAG SPC tables**.

**(iii) Western Electric Rules (2 marks):**
Four rules to detect out-of-control trends:
1. **One point beyond $3\sigma$** (zone A).
2. **Two of three successive points** beyond $2\sigma$ (same side).
3. **Four of five successive points** beyond $1\sigma$ (same side).
4. **Eight successive points** on one side of centerline (bias).

Triggering any rule mandates **process stoppage and root-cause analysis** — a contractual requirement in **Tier-1 automotive supplier SPC manuals**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Omitting $\eta$ in Heat Input:** Full marks require the efficiency factor. Marks deducted if assumed as 1.0 silently.
> - **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm/min with mm/s in HI formula. Standardize to **mm/min** before substitution.
> - **Confusing CTWD with Arc Length:** They are *not* the same. CTWD = tip-to-work static; Arc length = dynamic plasma gap.
> - **Forgetting Trugap vs TAC:** Trugap = arc length controller (V-based); TAC = joint tracking (I-based). Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> - **In Part B, only writing defects without the parameter-cause link** loses at least 2 of 7 marks.
> - **Ignoring ISO 3834 reference** in the architecture question — explicitly naming the clause number fetches full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision checklist for this topic:**

- ✅ **Heat Input formula** = $\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60 / (TS \cdot 1000)$ in **kJ/mm** — memorize with units.
- ✅ **Eight primary parameters:** V, I, WFS, TS, Gas Flow, CTWD, Torch Angle, Arc Length.
- ✅ **Arc efficiency:** GMAW = 0.80, GTAW = 0.60, SAW = 1.00, PAW = 0.50.
- ✅ **Empirical WFS-to-Current rule:** 1 m/min ≈ 200 A for 1.2 mm mild steel wire.
- ✅ **TAC (Through-the-Arc-Sensing):** Uses arc current variation for **seam tracking**.
- ✅ **Trugap:** Uses arc voltage for **arc length control** (different sensor mechanism).
- ✅ **NDT selection:** PT → surface cracks; MT → ferromagnetic; UT → thick section; RT → volumetric.
- ✅ **ISO 3834 levels:** 3834-2 (comprehensive), 3834-3 (standard), 3834-4 (elementary).
- ✅ **SPC capability indices:** $C_p$ for spread only; $C_{pk}$ for spread + centering; $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is industry benchmark.
- ✅ **Western Electric Rules:** $3\sigma$, $2\sigma$, $1\sigma$, and 8-pt bias tests.
- ✅ **Bead geometry formula:** $A_{\text{V-groove}} = \frac{t^2}{2}\tan(\theta/2) + t \cdot g$.
- ✅ **Quality pyramid:** Pre-weld → In-process → Post-weld NDT → Destructive testing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Selection of Welding Parameters

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *Selection of Welding Parameters* in robotic welding refers to the systematic identification and optimization of key process variables — **welding current, arc voltage, travel speed (or robot travel speed), wire feed rate, shielding gas flow rate, and stick-out (contact tip to work distance, CTWD)** — to obtain a weld bead with the desired penetration profile, bead width, mechanical strength, and metallurgical integrity, while ensuring arc stability, repeatability, and defect-free joints across automated production cycles.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cooking Recipe" 🍲

Imagine you are preparing a traditional Kerala *Avial* in a pressure cooker for **1,000 identical lunch packets** every day. You cannot "taste and adjust" once the lid is on. You must pre-decide:
- The amount of water (analogous to **shielding gas flow**)
- The flame intensity (analogous to **welding current**)
- The cooking duration (analogous to **arc time / travel speed**)
- The pressure setting (analogous to **arc voltage**)
- The stirring rate (analogous to **wire feed rate**)

If any one of these is off, every packet will taste different. In robotic welding, the robot cannot "see" and "correct" the weld like a human welder — it executes a **pre-programmed recipe of parameters**. Thus, **parameter selection is the digital cookbook of robotic welding**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A question on "selection of parameters" is essentially a question on **Heat Input control**, which is the single most tested concept under this topic in the KTU ESE pattern.

### 1.2 The Six Cardinal Parameters of Robotic Arc Welding

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Primary Physical Role |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Welding Current | I | **Ampere (A)** | Determines heat generation & metal deposition |
| 2 | Arc Voltage | V | **Volt (V)** | Sets arc length & bead width |
| 3 | Travel Speed | TS | **mm/min** or **m/min** | Controls heat input per unit length |
| 4 | Wire Feed Rate | WFR | **m/min** | Sets deposition rate & current (synergic) |
| 5 | Shielding Gas Flow Rate | Q | **L/min** | Protects molten pool from atmosphere |
| 6 | Contact Tip-to-Work Distance | CTWD | **mm** | Affects stick-out resistance heating |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a question asks *"list the parameters to be selected for robotic GMAW"*, the minimum acceptable answer is **Current, Voltage, Travel Speed, Wire Feed Rate, Gas Flow**. Dropping any one means losing **1 mark** out of the standard 3.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Parameter Hierarchy — "The Pyramid of Welding Control"

In robotic welding, parameters are not equal in importance. They form a **control hierarchy** that the robot must follow strictly:

**Tier 1 — Primary Energy Parameters (Cannot be altered by robot once set):**
- Welding Current (I)
- Arc Voltage (V)

**Tier 2 — Kinematic Parameters (Set by the robot program):**
- Travel Speed (TS)
- Wire Feed Rate (WFR)
- Robot Path (seam tracking)

**Tier 3 — Environmental Parameters (Set at fixture / gas panel):**
- Shielding Gas Composition (Ar/CO₂ mix)
- Gas Flow Rate (Q)
- Nozzle Diameter & CTWD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the hierarchy matters:** In KTU problems, students often treat current and voltage as adjustable *during* welding. In a robotic cell, they are **pre-set on the power source**, and the robot only commands **ON/OFF and Travel Speed**. Understanding this clarifies why a robot's "welding parameter file" is really a **lookup table**, not a continuous controller.

## 2.2 The Master Equation — Heat Input (HI)

The most important equation in parameter selection. It is asked in nearly every KTU paper:

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Input (HI)** is the energy supplied by the arc per unit length of weld. It directly controls penetration, HAZ width, and distortion.

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot V}{TS \times 1000} \times 60
$$

Where:
- $HI$ = Heat Input in **kJ/mm** (or J/mm)
- $I$ = Welding Current in **Amperes**
- $V$ = Arc Voltage in **Volts**
- $TS$ = Travel Speed in **mm/min**
- $\eta$ = Arc Efficiency (Process-dependent)
- 60 and 1000 are unit conversion constants (seconds to minutes; W to kW)

### 2.3 Arc Efficiency ($\eta$) Table — High-Yield for KTU

| Welding Process | Arc Efficiency ($\eta$) | Typical Use |
|-----------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** — Metal Transfer | | |
| Short Circuit Transfer | **0.75 – 0.85** | Thin sheets |
| Globular Transfer | **0.70 – 0.85** | Legacy |
| Spray Transfer | **0.65 – 0.85** | Thick plates |
| Pulsed Spray | **0.70 – 0.80** | Robotic standard |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | **0.40 – 0.60** | Precision / SS |
| **SMAW (Stick)** | **0.70 – 0.85** | Field welding |
| **SAW (Submerged Arc)** | **0.85 – 0.99** | Heavy fabrication |
| **PAW (Plasma)** | **0.55 – 0.70** | Aerospace |
| **Laser / Hybrid Laser-GMAW** | **0.30 – 0.40** | High speed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Using $\eta = 1.0$ for GMAW. Always state the process. A 0.85 vs 1.0 difference changes the answer by 17%, which is a **2-mark penalty zone**.

### 2.4 The Master Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula | Equation | Unit | Used For |
|---|---------|----------|------|----------|
| 1 | **Heat Input** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot V}{TS \times 1000} \times 60$ | **kJ/mm** | Penetration, HAZ, distortion |
| 2 | **Deposition Rate** | $DR = \dfrac{WFR \times A_w}{1000}$ | **kg/h** | Productivity planning |
| 3 | **Wire Feed–Current Link (Synergic)** | $I = k \cdot WFR + c$ | **A** | Synergic mode setup |
| 4 | **Bead Width (Empirical)** | $W_b = \dfrac{I}{TS} \times k_1 + k_2$ | **mm** | Bead geometry check |
| 5 | **Penetration (Empirical)** | $P = k_3 \cdot \sqrt{HI}$ | **mm** | Joint design check |
| 6 | **Voltage–Arc Length** | $V = a \cdot L_{arc} + b$ | **V** | Arc length control |
| 7 | **Gas Coverage (LPM/m of wind)** | $Q_{req} = Q_{base} + L_{gap} \times 0.3$ | **L/min** | Draft compensation |
| 8 | **Robot Repeatability Tolerance on Weld** | $\Delta_{weld} = \sqrt{\Delta_{robot}^2 + \Delta_{fixture}^2}$ | **mm** | Quality budgeting |

> Where:
> - $A_w$ = cross-section area of wire (mm²) × density (g/cm³)
> - $k_1, k_2, k_3, a, b$ = process-specific empirical constants
> - $L_{gap}$ = length of joint exposed to air drift

### 2.5 Selection Logic — Stepwise Industrial Procedure

**Step 1 — Identify the Base Metal & Thickness (t)**
- Mild Steel, SS, Aluminum, etc.
- Thickness dictates current range and joint preparation.

**Step 2 — Choose the Process & Transfer Mode**
- GMAW short-circuit for t < 3 mm
- GMAW spray / pulsed for t > 3 mm
- GTAW for non-ferrous or root pass

**Step 3 — Set Current from Standard Charts (C-Charts)**
- Use AWS / ISO 15614 / manufacturer curves: I = (30 to 50) × t (A per mm) for steel GMAW.

**Step 4 — Set Voltage (Synergic)**
- For GMAW, $V \approx 0.04 \times I + 14$ (typical synergic constant voltage slope).

**Step 5 — Choose Travel Speed from Heat Input Budget**
- For steel, target $HI = 1.0$ to $1.5$ kJ/mm to avoid HAZ softening.

**Step 6 — Calculate WFR from $DR$ requirement** using:
$$
WFR = \frac{DR \times 1000}{A_w \times \rho \times 60}
$$

**Step 7 — Set Gas Flow & CTWD**
- Ar/CO₂ 80/20: 12–18 L/min
- CTWD = 15–20 mm for 1.0–1.2 mm wire

**Step 8 — Validate with Trial Bead-on-Plate (BoP)**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Body Shop (BIW):** 200+ robots, parameters stored in a central database. A change in steel grade re-loads a new "parameter set" — selection determines whether a car's weld passes the crash test.
- **Shipbuilding:** SAW parameters selected to balance productivity (high HI) against HAZ toughness (low HI) — a trade-off central to the KTU heat input question.
- **Pressure Vessel Fabrication:** ASME IX qualifies a WPS. Selection of parameters is the *content* of the WPS — failure here means the vessel is illegal to commission.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Input Formula

**Starting point (Physical):** Electrical energy delivered by the arc.

$$
E_{arc} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{arc} \quad [\text{Joules}]
$$

**Step 1 — Convert to per-unit-length form**

The energy per unit length of weld is:

$$
e = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t_{arc}}{L} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

But travel speed $TS = L / t_{arc}$ (in mm/s) or $TS = L / t_{arc}$ (in mm/min when expressed that way).

**Step 2 — Substitute $t_{arc} = L / TS$ (with $TS$ in mm/s)**

$$
e = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot (L / TS_{mm/s})}{L} = \frac{V \cdot I}{TS_{mm/s}} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

**Step 3 — Account for arc efficiency $\eta$** (not all electrical energy becomes useful heat in the workpiece — some is lost to spatter, radiation, and electrode cooling).

$$
e_{useful} = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{TS_{mm/s}} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kJ/mm and use $TS$ in mm/min** (industry standard input unit)

Since $1$ W = $1$ J/s, and $TS$ in mm/min must be divided by 60 to get mm/s:

$$
e_{useful} = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{(TS_{mm/min} / 60)} = \frac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{TS_{mm/min}} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

**Step 5 — Convert J to kJ (divide by 1000):**

$$
HI = \frac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot TS} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{TS \times 16.667} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]
$$

Equivalently, in the textbook form:

$$
\boxed{HI = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot V \times 60}{TS \times 1000}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerator is in W·min** (since $I \times V$ = Power in W; multiply by 60 to get W·min). **Denominator × 1000** converts mm to m and W to kW. Result is kJ/mm.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style)

**Problem (GMAW Robotic Cell):**
A robotic MIG welds a 6 mm thick mild-steel lap joint using **80% Ar + 20% CO₂** shielding gas, **1.2 mm wire**, and the following parameters:
- $I = 220$ A
- $V = 24$ V
- $TS = 500$ mm/min
- Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.80$

**Calculate:**
1. Heat input
2. Penetration estimate (using $k_3 = 1.2$)
3. Whether the heat input is within the safe range for mild steel (limit = 1.5 kJ/mm)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Heat Input**

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot V \times 60}{TS \times 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \frac{0.80 \times 220 \times 24 \times 60}{500 \times 1000}
$$

Calculate numerator:
$$
0.80 \times 220 = 176
$$
$$
176 \times 24 = 4224
$$
$$
4224 \times 60 = 253440
$$

Calculate denominator:
$$
500 \times 1000 = 500000
$$

Final division:
$$
HI = \frac{253440}{500000} = 0.50688 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \approx \mathbf{0.51 \;\; kJ/mm}
$$

**(b) Penetration estimate**

$$
P = k_3 \cdot \sqrt{HI} = 1.2 \cdot \sqrt{0.51}
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.51} \approx 0.7141
$$

$$
P = 1.2 \times 0.7141 = \mathbf{0.857 \;\; mm}
$$

**(c) Heat Input Range Check**

- Calculated $HI = 0.51$ kJ/mm
- Allowable upper limit = 1.5 kJ/mm
- Since $0.51 \ll 1.5$, the parameters are **safe from HAZ softening**, but may produce **lack of penetration** for a 6 mm joint. Recommendation: increase current to ~280 A or reduce travel speed to ~350 mm/min.

**Suggested re-designed parameters** (so that $HI \approx 1.0$ kJ/mm):

$$
1.0 = \frac{0.80 \times I \times 24 \times 60}{TS \times 1000}
$$

$$
I \times \frac{1440}{TS} = 1250
$$

If $TS = 400$ mm/min, then:
$$
I = \frac{1250 \times 400}{1440} = \mathbf{347 \;\; A}
$$

Check current density on 1.2 mm wire: Area $= \pi \times 0.6^2 = 1.131$ mm²
$$
J = 347 / 1.131 = 307 \;\text{A/mm}^2
$$
This is within the safe GMAW range (200–350 A/mm²), so the new set is **feasible**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity Table

| Parameter Change | Effect on Heat Input | Effect on Penetration | Effect on Bead Width | Effect on Distortion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Current (I)** | ↑↑ Strong increase | ↑↑ Increases | ↑ Slight increase | ↑ Increases |
| **↑ Voltage (V)** | ↑ Increases | ↓ Slight decrease | ↑↑ Wide bead | ↑ Increases |
| **↑ Travel Speed (TS)** | ↓↓ Strong decrease | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Narrows bead | ↓ Decreases |
| **↑ Wire Feed Rate (WFR)** | ↑ Increases (via I) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Slightly | ↑ Increases |
| **↑ Gas Flow (Q)** | No direct effect | Indirect (cooling) | No effect | Indirect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "C-V-T" rule for heat input:**
> - **C**urrent and **V**oltage are in the **numerator** → increasing them increases HI
> - **T**ravel speed is in the **denominator** → increasing it decreases HI
> Mnemonic: *"CVT — Current-Voltage Top, Travel on Bottom"*

## 3.4 WPS (Welding Procedure Specification) — The Legal Output of Parameter Selection

A WPS document, per **ASME IX** or **ISO 15614**, contains:

1. **Joint details** (type, thickness, gap, bevel angle)
2. **Base metal** (group/P-number)
3. **Process** (GMAW, GTAW…)
4. **Thickness range qualified**
5. **Filler metal** (AWS classification)
6. **Shielding gas** (composition, flow rate)
7. **Electrical parameters** (current, voltage, transfer mode, polarity)
8. **Travel speed**
9. **Heat input range** (minimum and maximum)
10. **Preheat & interpass temperature**
11. **Position**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam tip:** A 7-mark question often asks *"What is WPS? List 6 essential contents of a WPS for robotic MIG welding."* The above list covers the expected answer.

## 3.5 Parameter Selection for Specific Robotic Processes

### (a) GMAW Short-Circuit Transfer
- I = 50–200 A, V = 14–22 V, TS = 250–500 mm/min
- Used for thin sheets (0.6–3.0 mm)
- Low heat input, no spatter tolerance for cosmetic welds

### (b) GMAW Spray Transfer
- I = 200–400 A, V = 24–35 V, TS = 400–800 mm/min
- Used for thick plates (>3 mm)
- High deposition, requires high current density

### (c) GMAW Pulsed Spray (Robotic Favorite ⭐)
- Peak I = 300–500 A, Background I = 50–100 A
- Pulse frequency = 50–500 Hz
- Average HI = 0.4–1.0 kJ/mm
- **Why robotic standard:** Decouples deposition from heat input — high WFR with low distortion.

### (d) GTAW (TIG)
- I = 50–300 A (DCEN for steel, AC for Al)
- V = 10–20 V
- TS = 50–200 mm/min
- No filler or cold wire feed
- **Selection rule:** $I \approx 30$–$40$ A per mm of thickness for stainless steel.

### (e) Plasma & Laser Hybrid
- Multi-parameter: pilot arc, main arc, laser power, focal position, defocus
- Selection follows manufacturer macro libraries

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Parameter Selection Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Base Metal and Thickness t] --> B{Choose Welding Process}
    B -->|t less than 3 mm| C1[GMAW Short-Circuit]
    B -->|t greater than 3 mm| C2[GMAW Spray or Pulsed]
    B -->|Non-ferrous / Precision| C3[GTAW or PAW]
    C1 --> D[Step 1: Set Current from C-Chart]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Step 2: Set Arc Voltage via Synergic Relation]
    E --> F[Step 3: Choose Travel Speed from Heat Input Budget]
    F --> G[Step 4: Calculate Wire Feed Rate WFR]
    G --> H[Step 5: Set Gas Flow and CTWD]
    H --> I[Step 6: Trial Bead on Plate BoP Test]
    I --> J{Quality Pass: Penetration, Width, Strength}
    J -->|No| K[Adjust Current or Travel Speed]
    K --> D
    J -->|Yes| L[Step 7: Lock Parameters into Robot WPS File]
    L --> M[Step 8: Document WPS per ASME IX or ISO 15614]
    M --> N[End: Production Ready]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for KTU:** The flowchart is the canonical "Selection Algorithm" examiners expect in 14-mark questions. Memorize the **8 steps** from C-Chart → BoP → WPS.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Welding Parameter Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CAD Model and Joint Geometry] --> B1[Offline Programming Software OLP]
    B1 --> C1[Seam Tracking and Path Plan]
    C1 --> D1[Parameter Lookup Table WPS Database]
    D1 --> E1[Robot Motion Controller]
    E1 --> F1[Power Source I, V, WFR]
    F1 --> G1[Torch and Wire Feeder]
    E1 --> H1[Gas Panel and Flow Controller]
    H1 --> G1
    G1 --> I1[Workpiece and Fixture]
    I1 --> J1[Arc Sensing Feedback]
    J1 --> K1[Adaptive Control Loop]
    K1 --> F1
    K1 --> E1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What this shows:** Parameters don't flow one-way. The **arc sensing feedback** (voltage/current monitoring) closes the loop, allowing **adaptive control** where the robot adjusts travel speed or current *in real-time* to maintain a constant penetration — a hallmark of modern adaptive robotic welding.

## 4.3 Cause-Effect Diagram: Defects vs. Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Defect: Lack of Penetration] --> R1[Low Current I]
    R --> R2[High Travel Speed TS]
    R --> R3[Low Heat Input HI]
    S[Defect: Excessive Penetration and Burn Through] --> S1[High Current I]
    S --> S2[Low Travel Speed TS]
    S --> S3[High Heat Input HI]
    T[Defect: Porosity] --> T1[Low Gas Flow Q]
    T --> T2[Wrong Gas Composition]
    T --> T3[Draft in Cell]
    U[Defect: Undercut] --> U1[High Voltage V]
    U --> U2[High Travel Speed TS]
    V[Defect: Spatter] --> V1[Wrong Synergic Setting]
    V --> V2[Short Circuit Transfer at High I]
    W[Defect: Distortion] --> W1[Excessive Heat Input]
    W --> W2[Uneven Weld Sequence]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU trick question:** *"Your robotic MIG weld shows undercut. What parameter would you adjust and why?"*
> Answer: **Reduce voltage** (V) or **reduce travel speed** — high voltage widens the arc and pulls the toe of the bead away from the parent metal, creating a groove.

**Model Answer:**
Heat Input is the **energy supplied by the welding arc per unit length of weld**, controlling penetration, HAZ, and distortion. **[1 mark]**

Formula: **[1.5 marks]**

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot V \times 60}{TS \times 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]
$$
