## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the stepwise procedure for **selecting welding parameters** in a robotic GMAW cell. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A robotic MIG welds a **8 mm thick low-carbon steel** plate using **1.2 mm wire**, 80% Ar + 20% CO₂ gas, with the following set: I = 260 A, V = 26 V, TS = 450 mm/min, $\eta$ = 0.82. **Calculate the heat input**. Comment on whether it lies within the safe range for mild steel (recommended 0.8–1.5 kJ/mm). **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**
1. **Identify base metal & thickness** → mild steel, 8 mm → choose GMAW spray or pulsed. **[1 mark]**
2. **Choose transfer mode** → pulsed spray for robotic precision. **[1 mark]**
3. **Set current from C-chart**: I ≈ 35–50 A/mm × 8 mm = 280–400 A. Select ~300 A. **[1 mark]**
4. **Set arc voltage synergic**: $V = 0.04 \times I + 14 \approx 26$ V. **[1 mark]**
5. **Choose travel speed from HI budget**: Target HI = 1.0 kJ/mm → TS ≈ 400 mm/min. **[1 mark]**
6. **Calculate WFR** from $DR = 4$ kg/h, wire area = 1.131 mm², density = 7.85 g/cm³ → WFR ≈ 6.0 m/min. **[1 mark]**
7. **Set gas flow (15 L/min) and CTWD (18 mm)**. **[0.5 mark]**
8. **Validate by bead-on-plate test**, then lock into WPS. **[0.5 mark]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

Step 1 — Substitute values: **[1 mark]**

$$
HI = \frac{0.82 \times 260 \times 26 \times 60}{450 \times 1000}
$$

Step 2 — Compute numerator: **[2 marks]**
- $0.82 \times 260 = 213.2$
- $213.2 \times 26 = 5543.2$
- $5543.2 \times 60 = 332592$

Step 3 — Compute denominator: **[1 mark]**
- $450 \times 1000 = 450000$

Step 4 — Division: **[1 mark]**

$$
HI = \frac{332592}{450000} = 0.7391 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \approx \mathbf{0.74 \;\; kJ/mm}
$$

Step 5 — Comment: **[2 marks]**
The calculated HI = **0.74 kJ/mm** is **slightly below** the lower limit of the recommended safe range (0.8 kJ/mm). This suggests **insufficient heat input**, which may lead to **lack of fusion or under-penetration** in the 8 mm plate. Recommended action: **increase current to ~300 A** or **reduce travel speed to ~370 mm/min** to bring HI into the 0.9–1.0 kJ/mm range.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** What is a **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)**? List **eight essential contents** of a WPS for robotic GMAW. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Derive the **Heat Input formula** from first principles, clearly stating the meaning of every term. A robotic MAG weld uses I = 300 A, V = 28 V, TS = 600 mm/min, $\eta$ = 0.85. Calculate HI. If the same parameters are used but travel speed is reduced to 400 mm/min, what is the percentage increase in HI? **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**
A **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** is a **documented, qualified procedure** that specifies all welding variables required to produce a weld of acceptable quality per a specific code (ASME IX, ISO 15614, AWS D1.1). **[2 marks]**

Eight essential contents: **[5 marks — 0.5 + 0.5 + ...]**
1. **Joint type & preparation** (groove angle, root gap, root face)
2. **Base metal specification** (P-number / group)
3. **Welding process** (GMAW) and transfer mode (pulsed spray)
4. **Filler metal** (AWS classification, e.g., ER70S-6)
5. **Shielding gas** (composition, flow rate)
6. **Electrical parameters** (current range, voltage range, polarity)
7. **Travel speed range**
8. **Heat input range (min & max)**, preheat and interpass temperature

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Derivation:** **[3 marks]**
- Power of arc $P = V \cdot I$ [W]
- Energy over arc time $t$ = $V \cdot I \cdot t$ [J]
- Per unit length: $L = TS \cdot t$ → $t = L / TS$
- Substitute: $e = V \cdot I \cdot L / TS$ → per unit length: $e = V \cdot I / TS$ [J/mm]
- Multiply by arc efficiency $\eta$ and convert units →

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \times 60}{TS \times 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

Term meanings: $\eta$ = arc efficiency, $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $TS$ = travel speed, 60 and 1000 are unit converters.

**Calculation Case 1 (TS = 600 mm/min):** **[1.5 marks]**

$$
HI_1 = \frac{0.85 \times 300 \times 28 \times 60}{600 \times 1000} = \frac{428400}{600000} = 0.714 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Calculation Case 2 (TS = 400 mm/min):** **[1 mark]**

$$
HI_2 = \frac{0.85 \times 300 \times 28 \times 60}{400 \times 1000} = \frac{428400}{400000} = 1.071 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Percentage Increase:** **[1.5 marks]**

$$
\% \Delta HI = \frac{HI_2 - HI_1}{HI_1} \times 100 = \frac{1.071 - 0.714}{0.714} \times 100 = \frac{0.357}{0.714} \times 100 = \mathbf{50\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (How students lose marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting arc efficiency** — using $\eta = 1$ instead of 0.85 → 2 marks lost
> 2. **Unit mix-up** — using $TS$ in m/min instead of mm/min → 1000× error → 4 marks lost
> 3. **Missing the 60** — forgetting to convert W to W·min → 1.5 marks lost
> 4. **In WPS question** — only listing 4–5 items instead of 8 → 1.5–2.5 marks lost
> 5. **In derivation** — skipping the arc efficiency step or not defining $\eta$ → 1 mark lost

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Heat Input** is the **master variable**: $HI = \dfrac{\eta I V \times 60}{TS \times 1000}$ in **kJ/mm**
- ✅ **Six primary parameters** to select: Current, Voltage, Travel Speed, Wire Feed Rate, Gas Flow, CTWD
- ✅ **Process-dependent efficiency** is critical: GTAW ≈ 0.5, GMAW spray ≈ 0.75, SAW ≈ 0.9
- ✅ **Current** → penetration control; **Voltage** → bead width control; **Travel Speed** → heat input control
- ✅ **C-V-T rule** (Current/Voltage Top, Travel Bottom in HI formula)
- ✅ **Synergic mode** links WFR to current — used in 90% of robotic MIG cells
- ✅ **Pulsed spray transfer** is the robotic standard — decouples heat from deposition
- ✅ **WPS** is the *output* of parameter selection — legally required by ASME IX / ISO 15614
- ✅ **Safe HI range for mild steel**: 0.8–1.5 kJ/mm; for stainless: 0.5–1.0 kJ/mm (to avoid sensitization)
- ✅ **Empirical current rule**: I ≈ 30–50 A per mm of thickness (steel GMAW)
- ✅ **Trial bead-on-plate (BoP)** is the validation gate before locking parameters into production
- ✅ **Defect-to-parameter mapping**: Porosity → Gas; Undercut → Voltage↑ or TS↑; Lack of penetration → Current↓ or TS↑; Spatter → wrong synergic settings
- ✅ **Adaptive control** (closed-loop arc sensing) can vary travel speed ±20% in real-time to maintain HI

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Mnemonic — "PCVT-WG":**
> **P**rocess → **C**urrent → **V**oltage → **T**ravel Speed → **W**ire feed → **G**as flow.
> Always state them in this order in your exam answer to score full marks on procedure-type questions.

---
*End of Notes — Module 2: Selection of Welding Parameters | OERUT723 — Robotic Welding Technology | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monitoring and Controlling Weld Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monitoring and Controlling Weld Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weld Quality Monitoring and Control** in robotic welding is defined as the *real-time acquisition, interpretation, and closed-loop regulation* of process parameters (electrical, mechanical, thermal, and geometrical) during automated arc welding to ensure that the resultant weldment satisfies predefined metallurgical, mechanical, and dimensional acceptance criteria as per codes such as **ISO 3834**, **AWS D1.1**, and **EN 1090**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Robotic Welding Technology (OERUT723)*, this topic sits at the intersection of **mechatronics**, **process metallurgy**, and **manufacturing automation**. The syllabus places specific emphasis on *sensor-based in-process monitoring*, *adaptive control*, and *statistical quality assurance*.

### 1.1.1 Quality Attributes of a Robotic Weld

A weld is deemed acceptable only when the following attributes lie inside the prescribed tolerances:

1. **Geometric Quality** – bead width, height, undercut, leg length, toe angle.
2. **Metallurgical Quality** – grain structure, absence of porosity, lack of fusion (LOF), lack of penetration (LOP), cracks.
3. **Mechanical Quality** – tensile strength, hardness, impact toughness, fatigue life.
4. **Dimensional Accuracy** – distortion, angularity, alignment with respect to the robot's TCP (Tool Center Point).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Patient on an ICU Bed"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a patient admitted to the ICU. The doctors do **not** wait until the patient collapses to take action. They continuously attach multiple sensors — ECG for the heart, pulse oximeter for oxygen, temperature probe, blood-pressure cuff — and an alarm/infusion system *automatically* adjusts medication dosage based on the readings.

A **robotic weld pool** is the patient. The weld pool is a **miniature, violently turbulent metallurgical reactor** running at temperatures between **1500 °C and 20000 °C** (plasma), with mass flow rates of metal transfer reaching **5–10 g/s** in GMAW. Just as the ICU team monitors:

| ICU Component | Welding Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| ECG Electrodes | Arc voltage sensors | Detect heartbeat of the arc (electrical signature) |
| Pulse oximeter | Optical / photodiode sensors | Read "oxygen saturation" of the pool (brightness, IR emission) |
| Thermometer | Pyrometers / IR cameras | Read pool/HAZ temperature |
| Blood-pressure cuff | Wire-feed speed sensor | Detect filler metal flow stability |
| Ventilator | Gas flow meter | Shielding gas coverage |
| Auto-injector | Closed-loop controller (PID / Fuzzy) | Auto-corrects current, voltage, travel speed |

This analogy will reappear throughout the notes whenever a complex sensor concept arises.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Arc temperature:** **6000 K – 20000 K**
- **Weld pool temperature:** **1700 °C – 2500 °C** for steel
- **Speed of sound in steel:** **≈ 5960 m/s**
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant (σ):** **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
- **Standard acceptance limits for undercut (per AWS D1.1):** **≤ 0.8 mm** for plates ≤ 25 mm thick; **≤ 1.6 mm** for thicker plates.
- **Heat Input (HI) limit for low-alloy steel to avoid HAZ softening:** **≤ 2.0 kJ/mm**.
- **Typical process sampling rate for arc sensors:** **1 kHz – 50 kHz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> The 2024 scheme specifically lists *sensors*, *closed-loop control*, and *quality metrics* as CO2 outcomes. Memorising the relationship between these constants and the corresponding control variable is a high-yield activity for the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Weld Quality Monitoring

Every monitoring strategy in robotic welding rests on three pillars:

### Pillar A — **Sensing (Data Acquisition)**
The robot (or its auxiliary controller) acquires signals using **on-board** and **external** sensors. The choice of sensor is dictated by the *defect signature* one wishes to detect.

### Pillar B — **Signal Processing (Feature Extraction)**
Raw signals are notoriously noisy. Engineers extract *statistical features* — mean (μ), standard deviation (σ), RMS, kurtosis, skewness — and feed them into the controller.

### Pillar C — **Actuation (Closed-Loop Control)**
Based on the extracted features, the controller adjusts **welding current (I)**, **arc voltage (V)**, **wire feed speed (WFS)**, **travel speed (TS)**, and **torch orientation**.

---

## 2.2 Sensor Taxonomy Used in Robotic Welding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks **"Classify the sensors used for weld monitoring and give one application of each."** Memorise this taxonomy.

### 2.2.1 **Arc Sensors**
- **Principle:** The arc itself is treated as a sensor. As the welding torch moves closer/farther from the workpiece, arc current/voltage fluctuates.
- **Mechanism:** $V_{arc} = f(d, I)$ where $d$ = arc length.
- **Application:** **Seam tracking** in butt and lap joints; deviation detection up to ±0.1 mm.
- **Advantage:** No external hardware needed — purely electrical.
- **Limitation:** Cannot detect geometrical defects like porosity.

### 2.2.2 **Optical / Vision Sensors (2-D and 3-D)**
- **Passive imaging:** Mounted CCD/CMOS camera observes the arc light through narrow-band filters (e.g., **laser stripe at 808 nm**).
- **Active imaging:** A structured laser line is projected onto the joint; triangulation yields a 3-D profile.
- **Application:** Joint detection before welding; bead inspection after welding; **real-time pool geometry measurement**.
- **Sampling:** Up to **1000 fps** with modern industrial cameras.

### 2.2.3 **Infrared / Thermal Sensors**
- **Pyrometers** measure the pool's spectral radiance at a single wavelength (typically **1.6 μm** or **2.2 μm**).
- **IR cameras** yield 2-D thermal maps of the HAZ.
- **Application:** Penetration control, cooling rate estimation, HAZ grain-size prediction.

### 2.2.4 **Acoustic Emission (AE) Sensors**
- Piezoelectric transducers attached to the workpiece detect stress waves (**20 kHz – 1 MHz**) generated by:
  - Crack initiation
  - Porosity formation
  - Solidification events
- **Application:** In-process defect detection in high-strength steel welds.

### 2.2.5 **Force / Spatter / Fume Sensors**
- **Force sensors** on the robot wrist detect abnormal contact.
- **Spatter sensors** (photodiodes) detect ejected droplets, indicating instability.

---

## 2.3 Closed-Loop Control Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> A 14-mark KTU question is almost always framed around **"Explain the closed-loop control architecture used in adaptive robotic welding with a neat block diagram."** Prepare both PID and Fuzzy.

### 2.3.1 **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) Control**

The controller continuously minimises the error $e(t) = SP - PV$ where $SP$ is the setpoint and $PV$ is the measured process variable.

The control output is governed by:

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

- $K_p$ → proportional gain (immediate response)
- $K_i$ → integral gain (eliminates steady-state error)
- $K_d$ → derivative gain (anticipates future error)

In welding, $PV$ can be the arc voltage, $SP$ being the desired voltage. The output $u(t)$ drives the **wire-feed motor inverter**.

### 2.3.2 **Fuzzy Logic Control**

Useful when the mathematical model of the weld pool is **non-linear and ill-defined**. Membership functions translate linguistic rules such as:

> *"If voltage is HIGH and current is LOW, then reduce wire feed speed."*

### 2.3.3 **Adaptive / Model-Referenced Control**

The controller continuously re-tunes its $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ based on a **reference model**. Popular in **pulsed-GMAW** and **laser-hybrid welding**.

### 2.3.4 **Neural Network / AI-Based Control**

Modern systems use **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** for vision-based defect classification and **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)** for time-series prediction of bead geometry.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD**: The following table is the highest-frequency content for the 3-mark and 14-mark questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Typical Range / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (HI)** | $\displaystyle HI = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | $\mathrm{kJ/mm}$ | Steel: **0.8 – 2.5** |
| 2 | **Heat Input (alternate)** | $\displaystyle HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | $\mathrm{J/mm}$ | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (**0.6 GMAW**, **0.8 GTAW**, **0.9 SAW**) |
| 3 | **Travel speed derivation** | $\displaystyle S = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{HI}$ | $\mathrm{mm/s}$ | — |
| 4 | **Wire feed ↔ current** | $\displaystyle I = k \cdot WFS + C$ | A | Linear in CV/CC mode |
| 5 | **Process Capability Index** | $\displaystyle C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | **≥ 1.33** acceptable, **≥ 1.67** excellent |
| 6 | **Adjusted Capability Index** | $\displaystyle C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | dimensionless | **≥ 1.33** acceptable |
| 7 | **Stefan–Boltzmann radiance** | $\displaystyle E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | $\mathrm{W/m^{2}}$ | $\sigma = \mathbf{5.67\times10^{-8}\;W/m^{2}K^{4}}$ |
| 8 | **Wien's displacement (peak λ)** | $\displaystyle \lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}$ | m | $b = \mathbf{2.898\times10^{-3}\;m\cdot K}$ |
| 9 | **Acoustic emission attenuation** | $\displaystyle A(x) = A_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | dB | $\alpha$ in steel ≈ **0.1 – 0.5 dB/mm** |
| 10 | **Pool cooling rate** | $\displaystyle \frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{2\pi k (T - T_0)^{2}}{q}$ | $\mathrm{K/s}$ | Rosenthal's 3-D solution |
| 11 | **Penetration depth (GMAW)** | $\displaystyle h \approx 0.8 \sqrt{\frac{\eta V I}{k \rho c S}}$ | mm | Empirical |
| 12 | **Wider Acceptance Limit for undercut** | $u_{max} = 0.05t$ or **0.8 mm** | mm | AWS D1.1 |

> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When the question says *"state any four monitoring techniques"*, pair each technique with one formula from above.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Industry

| Industry | Typical Weld Quality Strategy |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (BIW – Body-in-White)** | 100 % vision inspection + SPC charting of robot cycle times |
| **Shipbuilding** | Phased-array UT after welding; adaptive GMAW during welding |
| **Aerospace (Ti, Inconel)** | In-process pyrometry + acoustic emission + CT scan post-weld |
| **Nuclear pressure vessels** | Continuous wire-feed monitoring + IR thermography + manual UT |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input (HI) and its Practical Significance

### 3.1.1 Starting Definition

The **Heat Input per unit length** is defined as the *energy delivered by the arc per millimetre of weld length*. Mathematically:

$$
HI = \frac{\text{Arc Power} \times \text{Time}}{\text{Weld Length}}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Express arc power.
The instantaneous electrical power delivered by the arc is:
$$
P_{arc} = V \cdot I
$$
where $V$ = arc voltage (Volts) and $I$ = welding current (Amperes). Units: **Watts** = **Joules/second**.

**Step 2** — Express travel time per millimetre.
If the robot moves at travel speed $S$ (mm/s), the time spent to weld $1$ mm of length is:
$$
\Delta t = \frac{1}{S} \quad \text{(seconds per mm)}
$$

**Step 3** — Multiply power by time to obtain energy per mm.
$$
E_{per\;mm} = P_{arc} \cdot \Delta t = V \cdot I \cdot \frac{1}{S}
$$
This yields units of $\mathrm{J/mm}$.

**Step 4** — Include arc transfer efficiency $\eta$.
Not all arc energy enters the workpiece. A fraction is lost as spatter, radiation, and fumes. The *effective* heat input is therefore:
$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$
For GMAW: $\eta \approx \mathbf{0.6}$ – **0.8**.
For GTAW: $\eta \approx \mathbf{0.4}$ – **0.6** (lower because arc is colder).
For SAW: $\eta \approx \mathbf{0.9}$ – **1.0**.

**Step 5** — Convert to kJ/mm (the KTU/industry-preferred unit).
$$
HI \; (\mathrm{kJ/mm}) = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

The factor $\dfrac{60}{1000} = 0.06$ converts $\mathrm{J/mm}$ into $\mathrm{kJ/mm}$ while changing the speed unit from $\mathrm{mm/s}$ to $\mathrm{mm/min}$ implicitly.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

> **Given:** GMAW on 6 mm mild-steel lap joint, $V = 22$ V, $I = 180$ A, $S = 6$ mm/s, $\eta = 0.8$.
> **Find:** Heat input in kJ/mm.

**Solution:**

$$
HI = \frac{0.8 \times 22 \times 180}{6} = \frac{3168}{6} = 528 \;\mathrm{J/mm} = 0.528 \;\mathrm{kJ/mm}
$$

Since this is well within the **0.8 – 2.5 kJ/mm** envelope, no HAZ softening is expected. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Process Capability Indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$

These indices quantify *how well the process stays inside specification limits*. They are heavily emphasised in **SPC (Statistical Process Control)** for robotic welding — a frequent 7-mark sub-question.

### 3.2.1 Derivation of $C_p$

**Step 1** — Define the *tolerance window*.
The specification band has width:
$$
W = USL - LSL
$$
where $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit and $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit.

**Step 2** — Define the *natural process spread*.
For a normally distributed process, **99.73 %** of measurements lie within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$. So the natural spread is $6\sigma$.

**Step 3** — Form the ratio.
$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Tolerance Width}}{\text{Natural Spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Step 4** — Interpretation.
- $C_p = 1.00$ → process just fits (very poor).
- $C_p = 1.33$ → industry minimum acceptable.
- $C_p = 1.67$ → excellent (six-sigma quality).

### 3.2.2 Why $C_p$ is Insufficient

$C_p$ only measures the **width ratio**; it ignores **process centring**. A process can have a high $C_p$ yet produce all rejects because its mean $\mu$ is offset from the midpoint.

**Step 5** — Introduce $C_{pk}$ that penalises off-centre processes.

The distances from $\mu$ to each specification limit are:
$$
d_{upper} = USL - \mu, \qquad d_{lower} = \mu - LSL
$$

Each distance must accommodate $3\sigma$ on the relevant side. Hence:
$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The above formula uses `\min\!\left( \ldots \right)` and **avoids the bare pipe `|`** to prevent breaking the surrounding markdown tables.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example — Bead Height Monitoring

> **Given:** A robot measures bead height (mm) over 25 samples.
> - $\mu = 3.10$ mm
> - $\sigma = 0.18$ mm
> - $LSL = 2.50$ mm, $USL = 3.70$ mm
> **Find:** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

**Solution:**

$$
C_p = \frac{3.70 - 2.50}{6 \times 0.18} = \frac{1.20}{1.08} = 1.111
$$

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{3.70 - 3.10}{3 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.60}{0.54} = 1.111
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{3.10 - 2.50}{3 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.60}{0.54} = 1.111
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.111,\; 1.111) = 1.111
$$

**Interpretation:** The process is *marginally capable* ($C_{pk}$ slightly below the 1.33 target). The robot operator must reduce $\sigma$ (e.g., by stabilising wire feed) or re-centre $\mu$ at **3.10 mm** (already centred). Recommended action: tighten wire-feed accuracy. ⚙️

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Penetration Depth Using Rosenthal's Line-Source Model

A classic KTU derivation. The model assumes a *moving point source of heat* on a semi-infinite plate.

### 3.3.1 Starting Equation

For a moving point source at velocity $u$ along the $x$-axis, the peak temperature at a point $(x, y, z)$ below the surface is:

$$
T - T_0 = \frac{q}{2\pi k R} \cdot \exp\!\left[ -\frac{u(R + x)}{2\alpha} \right]
$$

where:
- $q = \eta V I$ — arc power (Watts)
- $k$ — thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\alpha$ — thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- $R = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2}}$ — radial distance from the source.

### 3.3.2 Deriving the Penetration Depth

At the *trailing edge* of the pool directly under the arc ($x \to 0$, $y \to 0$), $R \to z$. The condition for *melting* is $T = T_m$ (melting point). Solving for $z = h$:

$$
T_m - T_0 = \frac{q}{2\pi k h}
$$

$$
h = \frac{q}{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)}
$$

Since $q = \eta V I$:

$$
h = \frac{\eta V I}{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)}
$$

This shows that **penetration depth is linearly proportional to arc power** and inversely proportional to plate conductivity.

### 3.3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Mild Steel

> **Given:** Steel plate, $k = 50$ W/m·K, $T_m - T_0 = 1500$ K (for mild steel), $V = 24$ V, $I = 200$ A, $\eta = 0.8$.
> **Find:** Theoretical penetration.

**Solution:**

$$
h = \frac{0.8 \times 24 \times 200}{2\pi \times 50 \times 1500}
$$

$$
h = \frac{3840}{471238.9} = 0.00815 \;\text{m} \approx 8.15 \;\text{mm}
$$

> **Note:** This is the *theoretical maximum* assuming perfect coupling; real weld pools achieve ~**70 – 80 %** of this value.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Acoustic-Emission Attenuation in the Workpiece

Useful for understanding why AE sensors must be placed *close* to the weld.

**Step 1** — Define a stress wave of amplitude $A_0$ at the source.
**Step 2** — Energy dissipates as it propagates through the metal's grain boundaries.

The Beer–Lambert analogue gives:

$$
A(x) = A_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}
$$

Taking the natural log:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A(x)}\right) = \alpha x
$$

For a known $A(x)/A_0$ ratio measured at two positions, $\alpha$ can be estimated. Typical $\alpha$ in **structural steel** = **0.1 – 0.5 dB/mm**, meaning after **10 mm** the amplitude drops by **63 – 99 %**. Therefore, AE sensors are mounted within **5–20 mm** of the weld line.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Adaptive Weld Quality Control

Below is a Mermaid **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that traces the signal from the welding arc back to the welding power source. This is a high-yield diagram for the 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WPS[Welder Power Source]
        INV[Inverter 0 to 1000 Hz PWM]
        INV --> ARC[Arc Plasma 6000K]
    end

    subgraph SENS[Sensor Suite]
        AS[Arc Sensor V and I]
        OS[Optical Camera 1000 fps]
        IR[IR Pyrometer 1.6 micrometer]
        AE[Acoustic Emission PZT]
        WS[Wire Feed Tachometer]
    end

    ARC -->|Emits light and sound| SENS
    ARC -->|Electrical signal| AS

    SENS --> DAQ[DAQ and ADC 16 bit 50 kHz]

    DAQ --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]

    DSP -->|Extract features mu sigma kurtosis| FE[Feature Extraction Block]

    FE --> DEC{Decision Logic}

    DEC -->|PID Controller| PID[PID Kp Ki Kd]
    DEC -->|Fuzzy Logic| FL[Fuzzy Inference Engine]
    DEC -->|Neural Network| NN[CNN RNN Classifier]

    PID --> INV
    FL --> INV
    NN --> INV

    DEC --> LOG[SPC Database Cp Cpk Charts]
    LOG --> HMI[Operator HMI Touch Screen]

    HMI -->|Manual override| DEC
```

### 4.1.1 How to Read the Diagram in an Exam

- **WPS block (left):** The inverter produces a high-frequency PWM waveform that drives the arc.
- **SENS block (top right):** Five parallel sensor streams — each is a *separate evidence source*.
- **DAQ/DSP pipeline:** Converts analogue noise into discrete-time features.
- **Decision Logic:** Heart of the system; switches between PID, Fuzzy, and AI based on weld type.
- **Feedback arrow (back to INV):** The defining feature of *closed-loop* — the welding source is corrected every **20–50 ms**.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Quality Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Welding Cycle] --> B[Acquire Sensor Data at 10 kHz]
    B --> C{Compute Bead Geometry Error}
    C -->|Error within band 0.1 mm| D[Continue Welding]
    C -->|Error exceeds 0.1 mm| E[Activate Closed Loop]
    E --> F[Adjust Wire Feed and Voltage]
    F --> G{Error Resolved?}
    G -->|Yes| D
    G -->|No 3 retries| H[Trigger Alarm and Stop Robot]
    H --> I[Log Defect in SPC Database]
    I --> J[Operator Inspection]
    J --> K[Resume or Repair]
```

---

## 4.3 Sensor Placement Schematic (Top View of Workpiece)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Plate[Workpiece Plate 1000 mm x 500 mm]
        direction LR
        T1[Torch Position]:::torch
        S1[AE Sensor at 10 mm]:::sensor
        S2[IR Pyrometer at 50 mm]:::sensor
        S3[Vibration Sensor at 100 mm]:::sensor
        C1[Vison Camera above]:::sensor
    end

    classDef torch fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram intentionally uses plain text labels inside the nodes (e.g., `AE Sensor at 10 mm`) without bold or HTML markup, in compliance with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

---

## 4.4 Adaptive Control Loop — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Arc Voltage 22 V] --> SUM((+))
    FB[Feedback from Arc Sensor] -->|Subtract| SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error e of t]
    ERR --> CTRL[PID Controller]
    CTRL --> ACT[Power Source Inverter]
    ACT --> PLANT[Weld Pool Plant]
    PLANT -->|Disturbance: gap, wear| OUT[Measured Voltage]
    OUT --> FB
```

> **Reading aid:** `SUM` is a summing junction; the negative sign on the feedback path is a standard control-systems convention (negative feedback).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any four sensors used for in-process weld quality monitoring and state the physical quantity each one measures.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Arc sensor** – measures *arc voltage and current* for seam tracking.
2. **Optical/CCD camera** – measures *pool geometry and bead width* via image processing.
3. **Pyrometer / IR camera** – measures *pool temperature* using IR radiance.
4. **Acoustic Emission sensor** – measures *stress waves* to detect crack initiation.
5. *(Any other: wire-feed tachometer, spatter photodiode, etc.)*

> Each sensor carries **0.75 mark** for naming + **0.75 mark** for the measured quantity. Total 3.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Heat Input in arc welding. Write its formula and indicate the typical safe limit for low-alloy steel.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Heat Input is the *energy delivered by the arc per unit length of weld*. It directly governs the HAZ microstructure and distortion.

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad [\mathrm{J/mm}] \quad \text{or} \quad HI = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\mathrm{kJ/mm}]
$$

For **low-alloy / Cr-Mo steels**, the safe upper limit is **2.0 kJ/mm** to avoid HAZ softening and excessive grain growth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU ESE mandates an **internal choice**. Both options below are fully solved.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Modified]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop adaptive control architecture** used in robotic welding for monitoring and controlling weld quality. Discuss the role of **PID** and **Fuzzy logic** controllers in this context. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A robotic GMAW cell welds 6 mm mild-steel lap joints. The measured parameters are $V = 24$ V, $I = 200$ A, $S = 8$ mm/s, arc efficiency $\eta = 0.8$.
  **(i)** Calculate the **Heat Input** in kJ/mm and comment on its adequacy for the application.
  **(ii)** If the standard deviation of bead-height measurements is $\sigma = 0.20$ mm, mean $\mu = 3.0$ mm, with $USL = 3.5$ mm and $LSL = 2.5$ mm, compute **$C_p$ and $C_{pk}$**. Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q. (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (reproduce in answer sheet):**

```
   Setpoint (e.g., V = 22 V)
         │
         ▼
   ┌────────────┐
   │  Summing   │◄─── Feedback from Arc Sensor
   │  Junction  │      (measured V)
   └─────┬──────┘
         │ e(t) = SP – PV
         ▼
   ┌────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐
   │   PID /    │───▶│  Welding    │
   │  Fuzzy     │    │  Inverter   │
   └────────────┘    └──────┬──────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                       [ ARC / POOL ]
                            │
                            ▼ (sensed back)
                  [ Arc Sensor + Optical + IR ]
```

**Explanation (bullet-form for clarity):**

1. The **setpoint** (e.g., 22 V arc voltage) is compared with the **measured voltage** from the arc sensor.
2. The error $e(t)$ is fed into the **PID controller**:
   - $K_p$ term acts *immediately* on small deviations.
   - $K_i$ term eliminates steady-state offset due to wear of contact tip.
   - $K_d$ term dampens oscillations when the robot accelerates.
3. The PID output drives the **welding inverter**, which adjusts the **wire-feed motor speed** in real time.
4. When the welding condition is non-linear (e.g., varying joint gap from 0 to 1.5 mm), a **Fuzzy Logic Controller** is preferred. Linguistic rules such as:
   - *IF gap is LARGE AND voltage is LOW THEN increase WFS.*
   are evaluated, and the crisp output adjusts the inverter.
5. The **sampling frequency** is typically **10 – 50 kHz** for electrical signals; **1 kHz** for vision.
6. The architecture is **adaptive**: gains are retuned online if the error trend persists, ensuring consistent bead geometry despite torch wear or plate thermal distortion.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks diagram, 3 marks PID, 2 marks Fuzzy.)*

---

#### **Solution to Q. (b) — Heat Input & SPC (7 marks)**

**Part (i) — Heat Input (3.5 marks):**

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} = \frac{0.8 \times 24 \times 200}{8}
$$

$$
HI = \frac{3840}{8} = 480 \;\mathrm{J/mm} = 0.48 \;\mathrm{kJ/mm}
$$

**Comment:** The value **0.48 kJ/mm** lies well within the **0.4 – 2.5 kJ/mm** envelope recommended for 6 mm mild-steel lap joints in GMAW. ✅
Penetration will be adequate; HAZ softening is unlikely because $HI < 2.0$ kJ/mm.

**Part (ii) — $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ (3.5 marks):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{3.5 - 2.5}{6 \times 0.20} = \frac{1.0}{1.2} = 0.833
$$

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{3.5 - 3.0}{3 \times 0.20} = \frac{0.5}{0.6} = 0.833
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{3.0 - 2.5}{3 \times 0.20} = \frac{0.5}{0.6} = 0.833
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.833,\; 0.833) = 0.833
$$

**Comment:** $C_{pk} < 1.00$ indicates the process is **NOT capable** — a significant fraction of welds will fall outside specification. The recommended action is to **reduce $\sigma$** by:
- Cleaning the contact tip (less arc wander).
- Tightening wire-feed control loop.
- Calibrating the robot's TCP (reducing positional variance).

*(Mark split: 1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark answer + 1 mark comment.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Modified]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle and application of the following in weld quality monitoring:
  **(i)** Arc sensor **(ii)** Acoustic emission sensor **(iii)** Infrared pyrometer. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** With reference to **ISO 3834** and **AWS D1.1** quality requirements, describe the **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** methodology for robotic arc welding. Include a sketch of a typical **X̄–R control chart** and explain how out-of-control points are detected. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q. (a) — Sensor Principles (7 marks)**

**(i) Arc Sensor (≈ 2.5 marks)**
- The arc is treated as a *variable resistor*. As the torch-to-workpiece distance $d$ changes, arc voltage $V$ changes linearly.
- The robot sweeps the torch laterally; $V$ dips when over the joint centre.
- The minimum-voltage point is used as the **seam-tracking reference**.
- **Application:** Real-time joint tracking in **butt and fillet welds** without external optics.

**(ii) Acoustic Emission Sensor (≈ 2.5 marks)**
- Piezoelectric crystals (typically PZT) are bonded to the workpiece.
- They detect *transient stress waves* (20 kHz – 1 MHz) emitted by:
  - Crack initiation
  - Porosity nucleation
  - Solidification shrinkage
- The RMS amplitude of AE correlates with defect severity.
- **Application:** In-process detection of hydrogen cracking in **low-alloy steel welds**.

**(iii) Infrared Pyrometer (≈ 2 marks)**
- Measures the *spectral radiance* of the weld pool at a narrow wavelength (typically **1.6 μm** or **2.2 μm**).
- Uses **Stefan–Boltzmann / Wien's law** to infer temperature:

$$
T = \frac{b}{\lambda_{max}}
$$

- **Application:** Real-time penetration control in **GTAW** of stainless-steel tubes.

---

#### **Solution to Q. (b) — SPC and Control Charts (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Sampling plan (1 mark):**
A subgroup of **n = 5 welds** is sampled every hour. The mean $\bar{X}$ and range $R = X_{max} - X_{min}$ are plotted.

**Step 2 — Control limits (2 marks):**

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \overline{\bar{X}} + A_2 \overline{R}, \qquad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \overline{\bar{X}} - A_2 \overline{R}
$$

For $n = 5$, $A_2 = 0.577$.

$$
UCL_R = D_4 \overline{R}, \qquad LCL_R = D_3 \overline{R}
$$

For $n = 5$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $D_3 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Out-of-control rules (2 marks) — Western Electric Rules:**
1. Any single point outside $3\sigma$ limits.
2. Two of three consecutive points beyond $2\sigma$ on the same side.
3. Four of five consecutive points beyond $1\sigma$ on the same side.
4. Eight consecutive points on the same side of the centreline (run-rule).

**Step 4 — ISO 3834 / AWS D1.1 linkage (2 marks):**
- **ISO 3834-2** mandates documented process control for **critical welds**.
- **AWS D1.1 Table 6.1** lists *acceptable* vs *unacceptable* discontinuities; SPC charts provide *objective evidence* of compliance.
- The robotic cell's PLC archives every subgroup, producing the **Audit Trail** demanded by both standards.

> **Sketch (reproduce):** X-axis = subgroup number (1, 2, 3 … 25); Y-axis = bead height (mm). Centreline = $\overline{\bar{X}}$; UCL and LCL as horizontal lines. Points outside UCL/LCL trigger an alarm.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Read Carefully**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the arc-efficiency factor $\eta$** in the Heat Input formula — costs **1 mark**. Always state whether GMAW ($\eta = 0.8$) or GTAW ($\eta = 0.5$).
> 2. **Mixing units** — using $S$ in mm/min while the formula expects mm/s. State your unit convention explicitly.
> 3. **Citing $C_p$ without $C_{pk}$** — examiners explicitly require both. Centring matters.
> 4. **Naming a sensor without explaining its measured quantity** — half-mark deduction. Always pair name + physical variable.
> 5. **Reproducing the block diagram without arrows** showing *feedback* — the loop must visibly close back to the controller, or the diagram is treated as an "open-loop sketch" with a **2-mark penalty**.
> 6. **Memorising only one control strategy (typically PID)** — fuzzy/adaptive are higher-COG marks and often appear as the differentiator in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print before ESE):**

- ☐ **Heat Input** $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S}$ — units $\mathrm{J/mm}$ or $\mathrm{kJ/mm}$; remember the 60/1000 conversion.
- ☐ **Process Capability** — $C_p$ measures width; $C_{pk}$ measures centring; minimum acceptable = **1.33**.
- ☐ **Stefan–Boltzmann** $\sigma = 5.67\times10^{-8}\;\mathrm{W/m^{2}K^{4}}$ — used in pyrometer calibration.
- ☐ **Wien's displacement** $\lambda_{max} = b/T$ — at 2500 K, peak emission ≈ **1.16 μm**.
- ☐ **Five sensor categories**: arc, optical/vision, IR/pyrometer, acoustic emission, force/spatter.
- ☐ **PID tuning order**: tune $K_p$ → $K_i$ → $K_d$.
- ☐ **Fuzzy** suits non-linear conditions; **NN/AI** suits image-based defect classification.
- ☐ **Sampling rates**: electrical = 10–50 kHz; vision = 1 kHz; AE = 100 kHz – 1 MHz.
- ☐ **Acceptance limits** (AWS D1.1): undercut ≤ **0.8 mm** for thin plate; porosity < 2 % of projected length.
- ☐ **ISO 3834-2** = full quality requirements; **ISO 3834-4** = elementary requirements.
- ☐ **Penetration depth** scales linearly with arc power (Rosenthal: $h \propto q$).
- ☐ **Penetration depth** scales inversely with thermal conductivity.
- ☐ **AE sensor placement**: within **5–20 mm** of the weld (attenuation $\alpha \approx 0.1$–$0.5$ dB/mm).
- ☐ **Closed-loop feedback** must visibly close the block diagram; otherwise 2-mark penalty.
- ☐ **Adaptive control** retunes PID gains online; preferred for pulsed-GMAW and laser-hybrid welding.

---

**End of Module 2 — Monitoring and Controlling Weld Quality (KTU 2024 Scheme, OERUT723).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-destructive Testing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-destructive Testing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, also called **Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE)** or **Non-Destructive Inspection (NDI)**, is a branch of engineering science that deals with the examination, inspection, and evaluation of materials, components, and welded structures **without impairing their future usefulness**. In the context of robotic welding technology, NDT refers to a group of analytical techniques employed to detect surface-breaking and sub-surface discontinuities in welds, such as porosity, cracks, lack of fusion, slag inclusions, undercuts, and incomplete penetration, while leaving the workpiece fully serviceable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** NDT is defined as the systematic application of physical principles (electromagnetic, acoustic, radiographic, thermal, etc.) to assess the integrity of welded joints without causing damage, ensuring conformance to design codes such as **ASME Section IX**, **ISO 5817**, and **AWS D1.1**.

The governing philosophy rests on the principle of **inference** — we do not "see" the defect directly, but we measure a physical signal (ultrasonic echo, X-ray attenuation, magnetic flux leakage, dye capillary action) that is altered by the presence of a flaw, and then **interpret** that signal to characterize the defect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Destructive Testing (DT):**
> - **NDT:** Material remains fit for use after inspection (e.g., X-ray, ultrasound).
> - **DT:** Material is consumed or destroyed (e.g., tensile test, Charpy impact, macro-etching).
> - In robotic welding cells, NDT is preferred because 100% in-line inspection is possible without scrapping the part.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stethoscope of the Welder"

Imagine a doctor using a **stethoscope** to listen to your heartbeat. The doctor never cuts you open; he/she simply listens to the sound waves bouncing inside your chest. If the rhythm is irregular, that signals a problem.

NDT works in **exactly the same way** for a weld:
- The weld is the "patient."
- The NDT probe (ultrasound, X-rays, magnetic particles) is the "stethoscope."
- The defect (crack, porosity) is the "irregular heartbeat."
- The NDT technician/automated robot is the "doctor" interpreting the signal.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — Bats and Sonar:**
> Bats emit ultrasonic chirps and listen for echoes to detect obstacles. In Ultrasonic Testing (UT), the probe emits high-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) and listens for the echoes bouncing off weld defects — a man-made sonar for metals.

---

## 1.3 Classification of NDT Methods (Taxonomy)

NDT methods are broadly classified by the **physical principle** they exploit:

| **Category** | **Physical Principle** | **Example Method** |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface NDT** | Visual / optical | Visual Testing (VT) |
| **Surface NDT** | Capillarity | Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT) |
| **Surface NDT** | Magnetism | Magnetic Particle Testing (MT) |
| **Sub-surface NDT** | High-frequency sound | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) |
| **Sub-surface NDT** | Ionizing radiation | Radiographic Testing (RT) |
| **Sub-surface NDT** | Electromagnetic induction | Eddy Current Testing (ET) |
| **Volumetric/Volumetric** | Acoustic Emission | Acoustic Emission Testing (AET) |
| **Volumetric/Volumetric** | Thermal | Infrared Thermography (IRT) |
| **Advanced** | Phased array beam steering | Phased Array UT (PAUT) |
| **Advanced** | Time-of-flight diffraction | TOFD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In robotic welding, the most commonly automated NDT methods are **UT, PAUT, TOFD, and VT** because they are amenable to robotic manipulator end-effectors and provide digital signals that can be processed in real time.

---

## 1.4 Discontinuities vs. Defects — A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> A **discontinuity** is an interruption in the physical structure of a weld. A **defect** is a discontinuity that **fails to meet the acceptance criteria** of the applicable code. Not every discontinuity is a defect — some are tolerable by design.

Common weld discontinuities detected by NDT:
1. **Porosity** — gas pockets (cavity-type)
2. **Cracks** — longitudinal, transverse, crater, toe (planar)
3. **Lack of Fusion (LOF)** — failure to fuse base metal
4. **Lack of Penetration (LOP)** — root not fully penetrated
5. **Slag Inclusions** — non-metallic entrapped solids
6. **Undercut** — groove at weld toe
7. **Overlap** — excessive weld metal
8. **Root Concavity / Suck-Back**
9. **Solidification Cracks**
10. **Hydrogen-Induced Cracks (HIC)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Visual Testing (VT)

**Principle:** Direct visual observation of the weld surface using the naked eye or aided by borescopes, magnifying glasses, video cameras, and digital image processing.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Pre-cleaning** — remove spatter, slag, and oil.
2. **Inspection under adequate lighting** — minimum **1075 lux (100 foot-candles)**.
3. **Visual examination** — check for under-cut, crater cracks, profile, alignment, leg length.
4. **Dimensional check** — use fillet weld gauge, bridge cam gauge, hi-lo gauge.
5. **Documentation** — record acceptance as per **AWS QC1 / ASME V Article 9**.

> [!NOTE]
> **VT is the most widely used NDT method — about 80% of all weld defects are caught by visual inspection alone.**

In robotic welding cells, **Laser-based 3D Scanners** and **Structured Light Profilometers** are mounted on robot end-effectors for automated VT of weld bead geometry in real time.

---

## 2.2 Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT / LPI)

**Principle:** A low-surface-tension penetrant liquid is drawn into surface-breaking discontinuities by **capillary action**. Excess penetrant is removed, and a developer (white powder) is applied which reverses the capillary action and draws out the trapped penetrant, producing a visible indication.

**Process Steps (6 Stages):**
1. **Pre-cleaning** — remove contaminants.
2. **Application of Penetrant** — dwell time 5–60 minutes.
3. **Removal of excess penetrant** — water washable, post-emulsifiable, or solvent removable.
4. **Application of Developer** — pulls penetrant out of discontinuity.
5. **Inspection** — indications appear within 5–30 minutes.
6. **Post-cleaning** — remove developer.

**Penetrant Types:**
- Type I — Fluorescent (requires UV light, **365 nm**)
- Type II — Visible dye (red, visible under white light)
- Methods: Water-washable, Post-emulsifiable (lipophilic/hydrophilic), Solvent-removable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity Levels (per ASTM E165):**
> Level 1/2 (ultra-low) → Level 4 (ultra-high)

**Limitations:** Detects only **surface-breaking defects**. Cannot detect sub-surface flaws. Not suitable for porous materials.

---

## 2.3 Magnetic Particle Testing (MT / MPI)

**Principle:** A magnetic field is induced in a ferromagnetic material. At a surface or near-surface discontinuity, the magnetic flux lines **leak out** into the air, creating a **leakage field** that attracts finely divided ferromagnetic particles, forming a visible indication.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Pre-cleaning**
2. **Magnetization** — using **yoke (AC/DC)**, **prod**, **coil**, or **central conductor**
3. **Application of particles** — dry powder or wet fluorescent suspension
4. **Inspection under white/UV light**
5. **Demagnetization**
6. **Post-cleaning**

**Magnetization Directions:**
- **Circular magnetization** — detects **longitudinal** defects
- **Longitudinal magnetization** — detects **transverse** defects
- To detect all orientations, **two orthogonal magnetizations** are needed.

**Current formula:**
$$I = \frac{K \times D}{L}$$
where:
- $I$ = Magnetizing current (A)
- $D$ = Diameter of the part (mm)
- $L$ = Length between prods (mm)
- $K$ = Constant (300 for AC, 450 for DC, per ASME)

> [!WARNING]
> **MT only works on ferromagnetic materials** (carbon steel, low-alloy steel, etc.). It CANNOT inspect austenitic stainless steel, aluminum, copper, or other non-magnetic materials.

---

## 2.4 Radiographic Testing (RT)

**Principle:** Short-wavelength ionizing radiation (X-rays or gamma rays) is passed through the weld. The radiation is **attenuated** differently by sound metal vs. defect zones. The differential attenuation is recorded on a **film** (film radiography) or **digital detector** (DR/CR).

**Beer-Lambert Law of Attenuation:**
$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = Incident intensity
- $I$ = Transmitted intensity
- $\mu$ = Linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹) — depends on material density and photon energy
- $x$ = Thickness of material (cm)

**Contrast Sensitivity (Image Quality Indicator - IQI):**
$$\text{Sensitivity (\%)} = \frac{\text{Thinnest visible wire of IQI}}{\text{Weld thickness}} \times 100$$

Acceptable sensitivity: **2%–4%** as per ASME V Article 2.

**Radiation Sources:**
- **X-rays** — generated by X-ray tube; energy range **50 keV to 25 MeV**; voltages **50 kV – 25 MV**
- **Gamma rays** — emitted by radioisotopes: **Ir-192 (avg 0.38 MeV), Co-60 (avg 1.25 MeV), Se-75 (avg 0.21 MeV)**

**Selection Rule:**
$$E_{\text{optimal}} \approx 150 \text{ to } 250 \, \text{keV per cm of steel thickness}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Safety is paramount!** RT requires strict adherence to **ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable)** principle, use of dosimeters, lead shielding, controlled access zones, and licensing under the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** in India.

---

## 2.5 Ultrasonic Testing (UT)

**Principle:** High-frequency mechanical vibrations (1–25 MHz) are introduced into the weld using a piezoelectric transducer. The sound waves travel through the material, reflect off interfaces, and are received back. Defects produce characteristic echoes.

**Time-Distance Relationship:**
$$d = \frac{v \times t}{2}$$

where:
- $d$ = Distance to reflector (mm)
- $v$ = Velocity of sound in the material (m/s)
- $t$ = Time-of-flight (μs)
- The factor 2 accounts for **pulse-echo** (round trip).

**Velocity in Common Metals:**

| **Material** | **Longitudinal velocity $v_L$ (m/s)** |
|---|---|
| Steel | 5920 |
| Aluminum | 6320 |
| Copper | 4700 |
| Titanium | 6100 |
| Cast Iron | 3500–5600 |
| Water (couplant) | 1480 |

**Types of UT Waves:**
- **Longitudinal (L-wave)** — particle motion parallel to propagation; used in standard pulse-echo UT.
- **Shear (S-wave / Transverse)** — particle motion perpendicular to propagation; best for detecting **delaminations** and inclined defects.
- **Surface (Rayleigh wave)** — propagates along surface; depth of penetration ≈ 1 wavelength; used for detecting surface cracks.
- **Lamb waves** — for plate inspection.

**Shear wave angle beam inspection** (most common for welds) uses a **transducer with a wedge (e.g., 45°, 60°, 70°)** to generate refracted shear waves. **Snell's Law** applies:
$$\frac{\sin \theta_1}{v_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{v_2}$$

**Indications in Weld UT:**
- **Initial Pulse (IP)** — from transducer
- **Back-wall Echo (BWE)** — from far surface
- **Flaw Echo (FE)** — from defect
- **Loss of Back-wall Echo (LBE)** — suggests a large defect absorbing/blocking sound

**Reference Level (DAC — Distance Amplitude Correction):** A standard block (e.g., **IIW V1/V2 block, ASTM E115 E115) is used to draw a curve compensating for material attenuation; all flaw echoes must exceed a threshold above this curve.

**DGS (Distance-Gain-Size) Diagram** — alternative to DAC, relates flaw size to distance and amplification.

---

## 2.6 Eddy Current Testing (ET)

**Principle:** A high-frequency alternating current (typically **100 kHz to 5 MHz**) is passed through a coil, generating a **circular magnetic field** and inducing **eddy currents** in the conductive test piece. Discontinuities disrupt the eddy current flow, changing the coil's impedance. The impedance change is measured and analyzed.

**Depth of Penetration (Standard Depth of Penetration):**
$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{\mu \, \omega}} = 503 \, \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r \, f}}$$

where:
- $\delta$ = Standard depth of penetration (mm)
- $\rho$ = Resistivity of the material (Ω·m)
- $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ = Permeability (H/m)
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = Angular frequency (rad/s)
- $f$ = Frequency (Hz)

> [!NOTE]
> **88% of eddy currents flow within one standard depth of penetration ($\delta$) from the surface.** This makes ET ideal for **surface and near-surface** inspection.

**Applications in Welds:**
- Detection of cracks in austenitic stainless steel welds
- Tube and pipe weld inspection
- Heat-affected zone (HAZ) characterization
- Coating thickness measurement

---

## 2.7 Phased Array Ultrasonic Testing ( PAUT)

**Principle:** A **multi-element transducer** (typically 16, 32, 64, or 128 elements) is used. By **time-delaying** the firing of each element, the ultrasonic beam can be **electronically steered, focused, and swept** without moving the probe.

**Beam Steering Angle:**
$$\sin \theta = \frac{c \times \Delta t}{d}$$

where:
- $\theta$ = Steering angle
- $c$ = Sound velocity
- $\Delta t$ = Time delay between adjacent elements
- $d$ = Element pitch (center-to-center spacing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PAUT Advantages over Conventional UT:**
> - Full weld volumetric coverage in a single scan
> - Sectorial / S-scan / Linear scan imaging
> - Better defect sizing and characterization
> - Faster inspection
> - Easily automated on robotic scanners

---

## 2.8 Time of Flight Diffraction (TOFD)

**Principle:** Two transducers (transmitter + receiver) are placed on either side of the weld. The arrival times of:
- **Lateral wave** (along surface)
- **Tip-diffracted wave** (from crack tip)
- **Back-wall reflection**

…are used to **size the defect with extreme accuracy** (often ± 1 mm) and to determine its **height through the wall thickness**.

**Defect Height Calculation:**
$$h = \frac{(t_d - t_L) \times v}{2}$$

where:
- $h$ = Crack height
- $t_d$ = Time of flight of tip-diffracted wave
- $t_L$ = Time of flight of lateral wave
- $v$ = Sound velocity

> [!TIP]
> **PAUT + TOFD combined** is the gold standard for modern automated robotic weld inspection per **ASME Section V Article 4**.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Method** | **Key Formula** | **Variables** | **Typical Range / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| RT (Beer-Lambert) | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $\mu$ = attenuation coefficient; $x$ = thickness | $\mu$ in cm⁻¹ |
| RT Sensitivity | $S = \frac{t_{\text{IQI}}}{t_{\text{weld}}} \times 100$ | $t$ = thickness | 2–4 % |
| UT (Pulse-echo) | $d = \frac{v t}{2}$ | $v$ = sound velocity; $t$ = TOF | $v_{steel}$ = 5920 m/s |
| Snell's Law (UT) | $\frac{\sin \theta_1}{v_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{v_2}$ | Angles of incidence/refraction | — |
| TOFD crack height | $h = \frac{(t_d - t_L) v}{2}$ | Tip-diffraction and lateral TOF | mm |
| ET Skin Depth | $\delta = 503 \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$ | $\rho$ = resistivity; $f$ = frequency | mm |
| MT Magnetizing Current | $I = \frac{K D}{L}$ | $D$ = diameter; $L$ = prods spacing; $K$ = 300 (AC) / 450 (DC) | Ampere |
| PAUT Steering | $\sin \theta = \frac{c \Delta t}{d}$ | $c$ = sound velocity; $d$ = pitch | degrees |
| Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho v$ | $\rho$ = density; $v$ = velocity | Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| Reflectivity (UT) | $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Acoustic impedances of two media | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!TIP]
> **Acoustic Impedance $Z$ is the single most important parameter in UT** — it determines how much sound is reflected vs. transmitted at an interface. A crack (air-filled) has $Z \approx 0$, so $R \approx 1$ (almost total reflection).

---

## 2.10 Why NDT is Indispensable in Robotic Welding

1. **100% Inspection is Possible** — Unlike batch sampling, automated NDT can inspect every weld.
2. **Real-time Quality Control** — NDT signals can feedback to the robot controller for adaptive control (e.g., adjust welding parameters on-the-fly).
3. **Safety-Critical Applications** — Pressure vessels, nuclear piping, offshore platforms, aircraft structures demand zero-defect welds.
4. **Cost Reduction** — Detecting a defect before installation saves enormous rework costs.
5. **Code Compliance** — ASME, AWS, EN, and ISO standards mandate NDT of welds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pulse-Echo Distance Formula in UT

The transducer emits a short ultrasonic pulse at time $t = 0$. The pulse travels through the material, reflects from a flaw, and returns to the transducer at time $t$.

**Step 1:** The total distance covered by the pulse is **twice** the distance to the flaw (down and back up):
$$s_{\text{total}} = 2 d$$

**Step 2:** The distance is related to time by constant velocity $v$ (assuming homogeneous, isotropic material):
$$s_{\text{total}} = v \times t$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions for total distance:
$$v \times t = 2 d$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $d$:
$$d = \frac{v \, t}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example — A flaw in a steel plate produces an echo at $t = 12 \, \mu s$. The longitudinal velocity in steel is $v = 5920$ m/s = $5.92 \times 10^{6}$ mm/s.

Substituting:
$$d = \frac{(5.92 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm/s}) \times (12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2}$$
$$d = \frac{5.92 \times 12}{2} \text{ mm}$$
$$d = \frac{71.04}{2} \text{ mm}$$
$$d = 35.52 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The flaw lies approximately **35.5 mm** inside the steel plate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Acoustic Impedance Reflectivity

When an ultrasonic wave strikes an interface between two media (e.g., steel–air inside a crack), part is reflected and part is transmitted. The fraction of reflected intensity is governed by the acoustic impedances.

**Step 1:** Acoustic impedance of a medium is defined as:
$$Z = \rho \, v$$

where $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $v$ = longitudinal sound velocity (m/s). The SI unit is **Rayl** (= kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹).

**Step 2:** At a normal-incidence interface between medium 1 and medium 2, the **pressure reflection coefficient** is:
$$R_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

**Step 3:** The **intensity (power) reflection coefficient** is the square of the pressure coefficient (since intensity is proportional to pressure squared):
$$R_I = R_p^{\,2} = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical example — Steel-to-air interface.
- $\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7850$ kg/m³, $v_{\text{steel}} = 5920$ m/s
- $Z_1 = 7850 \times 5920 = 4.6472 \times 10^{7}$ Rayl
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $v_{\text{air}} = 343$ m/s
- $Z_2 = 1.225 \times 343 = 420.2$ Rayl

**Step 5:** Substituting:
$$R_I = \left(\frac{420.2 - 4.6472 \times 10^{7}}{420.2 + 4.6472 \times 10^{7}}\right)^{2}$$
$$R_I = \left(\frac{-4.6472 \times 10^{7}}{4.6472 \times 10^{7}}\right)^{2}$$
$$R_I \approx (-1)^{2} \approx 0.9999$$

**Conclusion:** **~100% of the ultrasonic energy is reflected** at a steel–air interface. This is why cracks (air-filled) produce such strong, easily detected echoes in UT.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Standard Depth of Penetration (Skin Depth) in ET

**Step 1:** An alternating current in the test coil generates a primary alternating magnetic field. By Faraday's law, eddy currents are induced in the test piece. These currents flow in closed loops opposite to the coil current.

**Step 2:** The eddy currents, by Lenz's law, generate their own opposing magnetic field. The net field **decays exponentially** with depth $z$ into the conductor:
$$J(z) = J_0 \, e^{-z / \delta}$$

where $J(z)$ is current density at depth $z$, $J_0$ is surface current density, and $\delta$ is the **standard depth of penetration**.

**Step 3:** Solving Maxwell's equations with the appropriate boundary conditions for a semi-infinite conductor, the skin depth is derived as:
$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu}}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\omega = 2 \pi f$ and $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$:
$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{2 \pi f \, \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \, \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

**Step 5:** With $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, simplifying:
$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \cdot 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{4\pi^2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot f \cdot \mu_r}}$$

$$\delta \approx 503 \, \sqrt{\frac{\rho \, (\mu\Omega\cdot\text{cm})}{\mu_r \, f \, (\text{Hz})}} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** Numerical example — Aluminum alloy ($\rho = 2.82 \, \mu\Omega\cdot$cm, $\mu_r \approx 1$), $f = 100$ kHz.
$$\delta = 503 \times \sqrt{\frac{2.82}{1 \times 10^{5}}}$$
$$\delta = 503 \times \sqrt{2.82 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$\delta = 503 \times 5.31 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\delta \approx 2.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** At 100 kHz, the eddy current inspectable depth in aluminum is about **2.67 mm**. Higher frequencies → shallower inspection, lower frequencies → deeper inspection.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of TOFD Crack Height Equation

**Step 1:** In a TOFD setup, the transmitter emits an ultrasonic pulse at $t = 0$. The pulse can travel by:
- **(a) Lateral wave** — along the surface from transmitter to receiver: time $t_L$
- **(b) Back-wall reflection** — to back wall and back: time $t_B$
- **(c) Tip-diffracted wave** — to the tip of a crack (which acts as a secondary point source): time $t_d$

**Step 2:** For a crack of height $h$ located at depth $z$ from the inspection surface, the tip-diffraction path is longer than the lateral wave path by:
$$\Delta s = c (t_d - t_L)$$

where $c$ = longitudinal sound velocity.

**Step 3:** By geometry of the diffracted wavefront (a Poynting vector analysis), the additional path length is **directly related to the crack height $h$** by:
$$\Delta s = 2h$$

This is because the tip-diffracted wave must travel "up" to the crack tip and then back down to the receiver — a round-trip of the through-thickness dimension occupied by the flaw.

**Step 4:** Equating:
$$c (t_d - t_L) = 2h$$
$$h = \frac{c (t_d - t_L)}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example — In a steel weld, $c = 5920$ m/s = 5.92 mm/μs. The lateral wave arrives at $t_L = 8.0 \, \mu$s and the tip-diffraction signal at $t_d = 12.4 \, \mu$s.
$$h = \frac{5.92 \times (12.4 - 8.0)}{2}$$
$$h = \frac{5.92 \times 4.4}{2}$$
$$h = \frac{26.048}{2}$$
$$h = 13.02 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The crack height is approximately **13 mm** — a critical input for fitness-for-service assessment per **BS 7910 / API 579**.

---

| **Weld Type** | **Mandatory NDT (Typical)** | **Acceptance Standard** |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure vessel circumferential | 100% RT or UT | ASME VIII Div 1, UW-51 |
| Pipeline girth weld | 100% RT (or AUT) | API 1104 |
| Nuclear primary piping | 100% UT + MT/PT | ASME III NB |
| Structural steel | VT + MT or UT | AWS D1.1 |
| Austenitic stainless | PT + RT or UT | ASME B31.3 |
| Aluminum welds | RT or UT (PT supplement) | AWS D1.2 |
| Bridge welds | UT + RT (critical) | AASHTO / AWS D1.5 |
