# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Non-Destructive Testing. List any four common NDT methods used for weld inspection.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) is a group of inspection techniques used to evaluate the integrity of materials, components, or welded joints **without impairing their future usefulness**.

**Four common NDT methods used for weld inspection:**
1. **Visual Testing (VT)** — Direct observation under adequate lighting.
2. **Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT)** — Detects surface-breaking defects using capillary action.
3. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** — Detects surface and near-surface defects in ferromagnetic materials using magnetic flux leakage.
4. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — Uses high-frequency sound waves to detect sub-surface defects.

> [!TIP]
> **Additional valid methods:** Radiographic Testing (RT), Eddy Current Testing (ET), Phased Array UT (PAUT), Time-of-Flight Diffraction (TOFD), and Acoustic Emission Testing (AET).

---

### **Question 2: State Snell's Law as applied in ultrasonic testing. What is the purpose of using a wedge in angle beam inspection?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Answer:**
**Snell's Law in UT:**
$$\frac{\sin \theta_1}{v_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{v_2}$$
where $\theta_1$ is the angle of incidence in the wedge (Plexiglas/PMMA) and $\theta_2$ is the refracted angle in the test material; $v_1$ and $v_2$ are the respective velocities.

**Purpose of the Wedge:**
1. The wedge is made of a low-velocity material (e.g., PMMA, $v \approx 2700$ m/s) so that **longitudinal waves are totally internally reflected** at the wedge-test piece interface, leaving only **shear waves** to enter the test piece.
2. The wedge is shaped at a specific angle (e.g., 45°, 60°, 70°) to produce a well-defined **refracted shear wave angle** in the material.
3. Shear waves are **more sensitive to planar defects** (cracks, lack of fusion) oriented perpendicular to the inspection surface.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (Choice 1):**

#### **(a) Explain in detail the principle, procedure, advantages, and limitations of Radiographic Testing (RT) of welds. Also explain the different types of radiation sources used. (7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
RT uses penetrating ionizing radiation (X-rays or gamma rays) that pass through the test piece. Because a defect (cavity, inclusion, or crack) has a different density or atomic number than the surrounding sound metal, it **attenuates the radiation differently**, producing a shadow image on a detector.

The intensity attenuation is governed by the **Beer-Lambert law**:
$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

**Procedure:**
1. **Surface preparation** — remove surface contaminants.
2. **Source placement** — on one side of the weld at a calculated focal distance.
3. **Film/Detector placement** — on the opposite side, with **IQI (Image Quality Indicator)** placed on the source side.
4. **Exposure** — for a calculated exposure time, controlled by **$\text{mA}\cdot\text{min}$ formula** or **exposure charts**.
5. **Film processing** — develop, fix, wash, dry in darkroom.
6. **Interpretation** — by a qualified **Level II / III** interpreter.

**Geometric Relationships:**
- **Geometric Unsharpness:** $U_g = \frac{(t) \times (d)}{F - t}$ where $F$ = source-to-film distance, $t$ = thickness, $d$ = source focal spot size. Must be ≤ 0.020" (0.5 mm) for high-quality radiography.
- **SFD rule:** $F \geq 10 \times t \times d^{1/3}$ for short exposures.
- **Penetrameter (IQI) Sensitivity:** must achieve 2%–4% depending on code.

**Radiation Sources:**

| **Source** | **Energy** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| X-ray tube (50–400 kV) | 50–400 keV | Thin steel up to 50 mm |
| X-ray tube (Linear Accelerator, LINAC) | 1–25 MeV | Thick steel > 100 mm |
| Se-75 | ~ 0.21 MeV avg | 10–40 mm steel |
| Ir-192 | ~ 0.38 MeV avg | 20–100 mm steel |
| Co-60 | ~ 1.25 MeV avg | 50–200 mm steel |

**Advantages:**
- Provides a **permanent record** (the radiograph).
- Detects **volumetric defects** (porosity, inclusions) very effectively.
- No material limitation (works on any metal).
- Sensitive to **changes in density and thickness**.

**Limitations:**
- **Hazardous ionizing radiation** — requires strict safety protocols (ALARA, AERB licensing).
- **Expensive** equipment and consumables.
- **Directional sensitivity** — planar defects (cracks) parallel to the beam are missed.
- **Slow** — film processing is time-consuming.
- **Thick sections** require very high energy sources.

---

#### **(b) With neat sketches, explain the working principle of Ultrasonic Testing (UT) using the pulse-echo method. Derive the relationship between distance, velocity, and time-of-flight. (7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of Pulse-Echo UT:**
A short-duration **high-voltage electrical pulse** (typically 100–400 V, 0.1–1 μs) is applied to a **piezoelectric crystal** (commonly PZT — lead zirconate titanate). The crystal converts this electrical pulse into a **mechanical vibration** (ultrasonic wave) in the **1–25 MHz** frequency range.

The wave enters the test material through a **couplant** (gel, oil, water) — necessary because air has nearly zero acoustic transmission. The wave travels through the material, reflects off any internal interface (flaw or back wall), and returns to the crystal. The crystal now acts as a **receiver**, converting the returning mechanical echo into an electrical signal.

The signal is amplified and displayed on a **cathode-ray oscilloscope (CRO)** as an **A-scan** — a graph of **amplitude vs. time**.

**Refer to diagram in Section 4.2** (pulse-echo signal flow).

**Typical A-scan Features:**
- **Initial Pulse (IP):** at t = 0 (large spike)
- **Flaw Echo (FE):** if a defect is present (intermediate spike)
- **Back-Wall Echo (BWE):** at the far surface (final spike)
- **Loss of BWE (LBE):** indicates a large defect absorbing energy

**Derivation of Distance Equation:**

Let $d$ = distance from transducer to the reflecting interface (flaw or back wall).

The total path length travelled by the pulse = $2d$ (down and back up).

If the sound velocity in the material is $v$, then the time taken is:
$$t = \frac{2 d}{v}$$

Solving for $d$:
$$d = \frac{v t}{2}$$

**Numerical example:** A flaw in a steel plate produces an echo at $t = 8 \, \mu$s. Given $v_{steel} = 5920$ m/s,
$$d = \frac{(5.92 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm/s}) \times (8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2} = 23.68 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2** in $d = v t/2$ is the **most common mistake** — students write $d = v t$ and lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing acoustic impedance $Z$ with intensity reflection** — remember $R_I = (R_p)^2$.
> 3. **Using wrong velocity** — must convert m/s to mm/μs when time is in μs, or use consistent units throughout.
> 4. **Forgetting the couplant** — examiners specifically look for mention of the need for couplant to exclude air.
> 5. **Confusing longitudinal, shear, and surface waves** — must correctly identify which wave is generated by the chosen probe/wedge combination.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative):**

#### **(a) Explain the principle, procedure, and limitations of Magnetic Particle Testing (MT). How is the magnetizing current calculated? (7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
When a ferromagnetic material is magnetized, magnetic flux lines flow through the metal. In sound metal, flux lines are uniform. At a discontinuity, the flux lines **leak out into the surrounding air** (leakage field). When fine ferromagnetic particles (iron oxide powder or fluorescent-coated particles) are sprinkled on the surface, they are **attracted to the leakage field** and accumulate at the defect site, forming a visible indication.

**Procedure:**
1. **Pre-cleaning** — remove oil, paint, scale.
2. **Magnetization** — using one of the following:
   - **Yoke (AC/DC)** — portable, for local inspection.
   - **Prods** — for spot inspection, current passed through contact points.
   - **Coil** — wraps around the part for circular magnetization.
   - **Central conductor** — for hollow parts like tubes.
3. **Application of particles** — **dry powder** (for AC magnetization, larger surface defects) or **wet fluorescent suspension** (for DC magnetization, finer subsurface defects).
4. **Inspection under white light (dry) or UV-A (fluorescent).**
5. **Demagnetization** — to remove residual magnetism that could interfere with subsequent machining or service.
6. **Post-cleaning.**

**Refer to diagram in Section 4.4** (magnetic flux leakage concept).

**Magnetizing Current Calculation:**

The required magnetizing current is calculated as:
$$I = \frac{K \times D}{L}$$

where:
- $I$ = magnetizing current (Amperes, A)
- $D$ = diameter of the part (mm)
- $L$ = distance between prods (mm)
- $K$ = 300 (for AC, per ASME V), 450 (for DC)

**Numerical example:** A shaft of $D = 100$ mm diameter is to be inspected using prods separated by $L = 200$ mm. Using AC magnetization:
$$I = \frac{300 \times 100}{200} = 150 \text{ A}$$

**Advantages:**
- Fast and inexpensive.
- Highly sensitive to surface and near-surface cracks.
- Indications are visible directly on the surface.

**Limitations:**
- Works **only on ferromagnetic materials** (carbon steel, low-alloy steel). Cannot inspect austenitic SS, aluminum, copper, brass.
- Requires **two orthogonal magnetizations** to detect all defect orientations.
- Surface must be clean.
- Not suitable for sub-surface deep defects (only ~ 2–6 mm deep).
- Demagnetization may be required after inspection.

---

#### **(b) What is Phased Array Ultrasonic Testing (PAUT)? Explain its working principle, advantages over conventional UT, and applications in robotic welding inspection. (7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Phased Array Ultrasonic Testing (PAUT) is an advanced form of UT that uses a **multi-element transducer** (typically 16, 32, 64, or 128 elements) where each element is pulsed **independently with a precisely controlled time delay** (called the **focal law**). This electronic time-delay control allows the ultrasonic beam to be **steered, swept, and focused** without physically moving the probe.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Multi-element probe:** A linear array of small piezoelectric elements spaced at pitch $d$ (typically 0.5–1.0 mm).
2. **Time-delay generator (focal law calculator):** Computes the relative firing time of each element to produce a desired beam angle and focal depth.
3. **Beam steering angle:** $\sin \theta = \frac{c \times \Delta t}{d}$, where $c$ = sound velocity, $\Delta t$ = time delay, $d$ = element pitch.
4. **Sectorial scanning (S-scan):** By varying $\Delta t$ across the array, the beam sweeps through a sector (e.g., –30° to +30° in steel), generating a **2D sector image** of the weld in real time.
5. **Electronic focusing:** Different focal laws can be applied to focus the beam at multiple depths, improving resolution of defects at various positions in the weld.

**Refer to diagram in Section 4.5** (PAUT beam steering).

**Advantages over Conventional UT:**

| **Feature** | **Conventional UT** | **PAUT** |
|---|---|---|
| Probe movement | Manual/robotic scan required | Electronic steering |
| Coverage | Single angle per scan | Multiple angles in one scan |
| Defect sizing | Estimated via DAC | Precise via S-scan imaging |
| Speed | Slow | Up to 10× faster |
| Image | A-scan only | A + S + B + C-scan images |
| Flaw characterization | Limited | Flaw height, length, orientation |
| Repeatability | Operator dependent | Highly repeatable |

**Applications in Robotic Welding Inspection:**
1. **Circumferential welds in pressure vessels and pipelines** — robotic crawler carries PAUT scanner around the weld.
2. **Nozzle inner radius welds** in nuclear piping.
3. **Thick wall austenitic welds** in boiler tubes.
4. **Real-time in-process weld monitoring** during deposition (in tandem with arc sensors).
5. **Fitness-for-service assessment** in offshore platforms.
6. **In-service inspection (ISI)** of critical welds during plant shutdowns.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Non-Destructive Testing Methods**

- **Definition:** NDT inspects materials **without impairing future usefulness**.
- **NDT vs. DT:** NDT is non-invasive; DT is destructive.
- **Discontinuity vs. Defect:** A discontinuity is an interruption in structure; a defect is a discontinuity that **fails to meet code acceptance criteria**.
- **VT:** Cheapest, simplest, 80% of defects caught. **1075 lux** lighting minimum.
- **PT:** Six steps — clean, penetrate, remove, develop, inspect, clean. **Capillary action** is the physics. **Only surface-breaking defects.**
- **MT:** For **ferromagnetic** materials only. Formula $I = K D / L$ (K = 300 AC, 450 DC). Requires **2 orthogonal** magnetizations.
- **RT:** Uses **Beer-Lambert law** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$. Sources: X-ray (variable kV), gamma (Ir-192, Co-60, Se-75). Sensitivity 2–4%. **Radiation hazard — AERB rules.**
- **UT:** Pulse-echo formula $d = v t / 2$. Steel velocity = **5920 m/s**. Couplant required. Acoustic impedance $Z = \rho v$. Reflectivity $R = [(Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)]^2$.
- **Snell's Law:** $\sin \theta_1 / v_1 = \sin \theta_2 / v_2$. Wedge angles 45°, 60°, 70° common.
- **ET:** Skin depth $\delta = 503 \sqrt{\rho / (\mu_r f)}$. **~88% of eddy currents within one $\delta$**. Surface and near-surface only. Conductive materials only.
- **PAUT:** Multi-element probe, electronic beam steering. Formula $\sin \theta = c \Delta t / d$. **S-scan, B-scan, C-scan** imaging.
- **TOFD:** Tip-diffraction for **crack height** sizing. Formula $h = c (t_d - t_L) / 2$. Combined with PAUT = gold standard.
- **Codes:** ASME Section V, ASME Section IX, AWS D1.1, API 1104, ISO 5817, ASTM E165 (PT), ASTM E709 (MT), ASTM E94 (RT), ASTM E114 (UT).
- **Robot-Automation Friendly Methods:** VT (laser scan), UT, PAUT, TOFD, ET. (PT and MT are difficult to automate.)
- **Selection Rule for RT:** $E_{optimal} \approx$ 150–250 keV per cm of steel.
- **Longitudinal velocity (L-wave) in steel = 5920 m/s** — a must-remember KTU numerical constant.
- **Always write units in calculations** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Welding Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Welding Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Welding Techniques** in the context of robotic welding technology refer to a class of non-conventional, high-precision, and high-energy-density joining processes that exceed the operational envelope of traditional Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) and Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW). These techniques are characterized by the use of concentrated energy sources (laser, electron beam, plasma, friction), automation-friendly process signatures, and the ability to join advanced materials (high-strength steels, aluminum alloys, titanium, dissimilar metals) with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ) and distortion.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OERUT723**, Module 2 explicitly addresses the industrial transition from conventional arc-based robotic welding to **high-energy-density beam processes** and **solid-state joining methods**, emphasizing the kinematic, sensory, and control adaptations a robot must execute to deploy them on a production floor.

## 1.2 Classification of Advanced Welding Techniques

The advanced techniques relevant to robotic deployment can be grouped into four families:

| Family | Examples | Energy Source | State of Base Material |
|--------|----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **High-Energy-Density Beam Welding** | Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Electron Beam Welding (EBW) | Coherent photons / accelerated electrons | Molten (fusion) |
| **Plasma-Based Processes** | Plasma Arc Welding (PAW), Micro-PAW, Keyhole-PAW | Constricted ionized gas arc | Molten (fusion) |
| **Solid-State Welding** | Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Friction Stir Spot Welding (FSSW) | Frictional + forging heat | Plasticized (no melting) |
| **Hybrid / Synergic Processes** | Laser-MIG Hybrid, Laser-TIG, Tandem MIG, Plasma-MIG | Coupled sources | Molten (fusion) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sunlight Through a Magnifying Glass" Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Students:** Imagine trying to light a campfire with a match (conventional arc welding) versus using a magnifying glass to focus sunlight onto a single point (advanced high-energy-density welding). The match spreads its energy over a wide area and takes time. The magnifying glass concentrates the same energy — or much more — into a tiny spot, instantly igniting the wood. Robotic advanced welding works on the **same principle of energy concentration**: by focusing kilojoules of energy into a spot of 0.1–2 mm diameter, the power density rises from ~$10^3$ W/mm² (ordinary arc) to $10^6$–$10^8$ W/mm² (laser/EBW). This is what enables deep penetration, narrow seams, and ultra-fast travel speeds.

## 1.4 Why "Advanced" Welding Requires Robotics

A few key performance metrics justify the robotic deployment of these processes:

- **Power Density** ($q''$): The energy flux per unit area on the workpiece.
- **Depth-to-Width Ratio (DWR)**: The ratio of weld penetration depth to bead width on the surface.
- **Travel Speed** ($v$): Linear speed of the torch/beam head relative to the joint.
- **Heat Input per Unit Length** ($Q/v$): Critical for controlling HAZ and distortion.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Industrial Insight:** In automotive thin-sheet aluminum welding (e.g., tailored blanks), laser welding achieves **DWR values of 3:1 to 10:1**, while conventional MIG gives only **0.5:1 to 1:1**. This geometric advantage is what enables the high-speed, low-distortion robotic cells used in Volkswagen, BMW, and Tesla body shops.

---

## 1.5 Pre-Module Conceptual Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    advA[Advanced Welding Techniques]
    advA --> fam1[High-Energy-Density Beam]
    advA --> fam2[Plasma-Based]
    advA --> fam3[Solid-State]
    advA --> fam4[Hybrid Synergic]
    fam1 --> lbw[Laser Beam Welding]
    fam1 --> ebw[Electron Beam Welding]
    fam2 --> paw[Plasma Arc Welding]
    fam2 --> mpaw[Micro Plasma]
    fam3 --> fsw[Friction Stir Welding]
    fam3 --> fssw[Friction Stir Spot]
    fam4 --> hla[Laser-Arc Hybrid]
    fam4 --> tand[Tandem MIG-MAG]
    lbw --> rob1[Robotic Scanner Heads]
    ebw --> rob2[Vacuum Chamber Robots]
    fsw --> rob3[Force-Controlled Spindle Robots]
    hla --> rob4[6-Axis Seam-Tracking Robots]
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Laser Beam Welding (LBW) — Theory of Operation

Laser Beam Welding uses a **coherent, monochromatic, collimated beam of photons** generated by stimulating a lasing medium (CO₂, Nd:YAG, fiber, or disk laser). The beam is focused through optics to a focal spot of 10 µm to 0.6 mm, producing power densities of $10^6$–$10^8$ W/mm².

The welding mode depends on power density:

- **Conduction mode** ($q'' < 10^6$ W/mm²): Heat conducts into the workpiece; shallow, wide weld pool; DWR ≈ 0.5.
- **Keyhole mode** ($q'' > 10^6$ W/mm²): Beam vaporizes metal, forming a deep vapor capillary (keyhole) that traps the beam by multiple reflections; DWR can reach 10:1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Keyhole Formation:** Multiple reflections inside the vapor cavity mean the beam "bounces" down the keyhole, effectively delivering energy to a depth of several millimeters. This is why a 4 kW fiber laser can penetrate 8–10 mm of stainless steel in a single pass.

### Operational Sub-Steps in Robotic LBW

1. **Beam Generation** — Pump energy excites the lasing medium; stimulated emission produces coherent photons.
2. **Fiber Delivery / Mirror Guidance** — Beam routed through optical fiber (for fiber/disk lasers) or mirrors (for CO₂).
3. **Focusing Optics** — Collimator + focusing lens concentrates the beam to a focal spot.
4. **Beam-Material Interaction** — Absorption, reflection, keyhole formation, plasma plume generation.
5. **Shielding Gas Delivery** — Coaxial or side-blown He/Ar to suppress plasma and oxidation.
6. **Robotic Seam Tracking** — Through-the-arc or optical sensors correct torch position to follow the joint.
7. **Solidification** — Weld pool solidifies as the beam moves on, leaving a narrow fusion zone.

## 2.2 Electron Beam Welding (EBW) — Theory of Operation

EBW accelerates electrons emitted from a tungsten filament through a high-voltage potential (typically **60–150 kV**). The electrons are focused by electromagnetic lenses onto the workpiece in a vacuum chamber ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa). Upon impact, kinetic energy converts to heat.

$$P = V \cdot I$$

where $V$ is the accelerating voltage and $I$ is the beam current.

### Why EBW Excels

- **Highest power density of any welding process**: $10^8$–$10^9$ W/mm².
- **Vacuum environment**: Zero contamination; no oxidation; suitable for reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Nb).
- **Extreme depth-to-width ratio**: 30:1 achievable.
- **Low total heat input**: Distortion is minimal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** EBW requires a vacuum chamber, which **limits its use to small or medium components** (gearboxes, turbine blades, sensors). Full-size car bodies cannot be EB-welded. Examiners often test this limitation.

## 2.3 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) — Theory of Operation

PAW is a refinement of TIG welding. A **constricting nozzle** compresses the arc, raising its temperature (up to 30,000 K) and energy density. A separate **non-transferred pilot arc** (between tungsten electrode and nozzle) initiates the process, then a **transferred arc** (between electrode and workpiece) does the welding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Parameter: Plasma Gas Flow Rate.** Typically 0.5–8 L/min for micro-PAW, 8–30 L/min for keyhole PAW. The orifice diameter (typically 2–4 mm) and gas composition (Ar, Ar+H₂, Ar+He) determine arc stiffness.

### PAW Modes
- **Melt-in mode** (low current < 50 A): Conduction-like, no keyhole.
- **Keyhole PAW** (higher current, square-wave current pulsing): The plasma jet punches through the plate, forming a keyhole.

## 2.4 Friction Stir Welding (FSW) — Solid-State Joining

Invented at TWI (UK) in 1991. A **non-consumable rotating tool** with a profiled pin and shoulder plunges into the joint. Frictional heat plasticizes the material (without melting), and the tool's rotation stirs the plasticized material from the leading edge to the trailing edge, where it consolidates under the shoulder forging pressure.

$$T_{peak} \approx (0.7 \text{ to } 0.9) \cdot T_{melt}$$

This is **below the melting point**, so there is no solidification cracking, no porosity from gas, and no HAZ liquation cracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic FSW:** Standard 6-axis articulated robots cannot deliver the **axial force (5–15 kN)** required for steel FSW. Special heavy-payload robots (e.g., KUKA KR 1000, Yaskawa MH50) or gantry/cantilever machines are used. The "Force-Controlled Robotic FSW" concept uses a force/torque sensor in the spindle to maintain constant downforce.

## 2.5 Hybrid Laser-Arc Welding (HLAW)

HLAW couples a laser beam with a MIG/MAG arc in the **same weld pool**. The laser preheats and forms a keyhole; the arc fills the gap, stabilizes the process, and bridges fit-up gaps up to 1–2 mm.

**Synergy Effects:**
- Arc is **stabilized** by the laser-induced plasma.
- Laser is **deeper penetrating** because the arc's preheating reduces reflectivity (especially on aluminum).
- Gap-bridging capability increases from ~0.1 mm (laser alone) to ~2 mm (hybrid).
- Travel speed increases by 2–5× over MIG.

## 2.6 Tandem MIG/MAG (Double-Wire) Welding

Two independently controlled wire feeds share a single torch but deposit two arcs in tandem (leading wire + trailing wire). The total deposition rate is **2–3× conventional MIG** without increasing heat input, making it ideal for thick-plate robotic welding in shipbuilding and pressure vessels.

## 2.7 Narrow-Gap Welding (NGW)

A **narrow groove** (typically 8–12 mm wide, versus 20–25 mm for standard V-grooves) is filled with oscillating or rotating electrodes. The reduction in weld metal volume saves 60–80% of filler metal and heat input. Robotic NGW uses **oscillating torches** programmed with sinusoidal or triangular weave patterns.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for ESE/Module Tests**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Heat Input per unit length | $Q/v = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $\eta$ = process efficiency (0.6 LBW, 0.7 PAW, 0.8 GMAW, 0.85–0.95 EBW) |
| 2 | Power Density | $q'' = \dfrac{4 P}{\pi d^2}$ | W/mm² | $P$ = beam power, $d$ = focal spot diameter |
| 3 | Depth-to-Width Ratio | $\text{DWR} = d_{depth}/w_{bead}$ | dimensionless | Keyhole processes: 3–30; MIG: 0.5–1 |
| 4 | EBW Beam Power | $P_{beam} = V_{acc} \cdot I_{beam}$ | W | $V_{acc}$ typically 60–150 kV |
| 5 | Cooling Rate (3D conduction) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{2\pi k \rho c \cdot (T - T_0)^3}{(q/v)^2}$ | °C/s | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat |
| 8 | FSW Rotational Speed | $N$ | rpm | Typical Al: 500–1500 rpm; Steel: 100–400 rpm |
| 9 | FSW Traverse Speed | $v$ | mm/min | Typical Al: 50–500 mm/min; Steel: 25–100 mm/min |
| 10 | Weld Pool Aspect (keyhole) | $\text{Keyhole stability} \propto P/d \cdot (1/v)$ | — | Higher power, lower speed → stable keyhole |
| 11 | HAZ width estimate | $\text{HAZ} \approx \sqrt{\alpha t_{peak}}$ | mm | $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $t_{peak}$ = time above critical T |
| 12 | Plasma Gas Velocity (PAW) | $v_{gas} = Q/(A_{orifice})$ | m/s | Orifice diameter 2–4 mm |
| 13 | Hybrid process travel speed | $v_{hybrid} \approx 2 \text{ to } 5 \cdot v_{MIG}$ | mm/s | Empirical synergy factor |
| 14 | Tandem deposition rate | $R_{tan} = R_1 + R_2$ | kg/h | Two independent wire feeds |
| 15 | Narrow gap fill passes | $n = h_{plate}/d_{pass}$ | dimensionless | For 25 mm plate, 4–5 passes |
| 16 | Energy per unit length (laser) | $E_L = P_{laser}/v$ | J/mm | 50–200 J/mm typical for steel LBW |
| 17 | Absorption coefficient (laser on Al) | $\alpha_{Al} \approx 0.07$ at 1 µm | — | Fiber laser; rises with temperature |
| 18 | Vacuum level (EBW) | $p$ | Pa | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on units:** The cooling-rate formula above is **3-dimensional conduction** and assumes a thick plate. For thin plate (2-D), the equation changes — examiners may give the plate thickness and ask which regime applies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input per Unit Length

The standard KTU textbook formulation of heat input per unit length, $H$ (in kJ/mm), is:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$$

**Derivation line by line:**

Let the arc deliver electrical power $P = V \cdot I$ (W) to the workpiece. Of this, only a fraction $\eta$ is actually absorbed (the rest is lost as spatter, radiation, and spatter). The useful thermal power is therefore:

$$P_{th} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

Now, in a time interval $t$, the torch travels a length $L = v \cdot t$. The energy deposited in this length is:

$$E = P_{th} \cdot t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$$

Dividing energy by the length $L$ over which it is spread:

$$\frac{E}{L} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{v \cdot t} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \;\; \text{(J/mm)}$$

To convert to kJ/mm, divide by 1000:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The /1000 factor** is purely a unit conversion from J/mm to kJ/mm. Many students forget it and lose 1 mark in the KTU ESE.

### Numerical Example (GMAW)

A robotic MIG cell operates at $V = 28$ V, $I = 250$ A, $v = 8$ mm/s, with $\eta = 0.8$. Compute $H$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{0.8 \cdot 28 \cdot 250}{8 \cdot 1000} \\
  &= \frac{0.8 \cdot 7000}{8000} \\
  &= \frac{5600}{8000} \\
  &= 0.7 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with industry norms for robotic MIG of mild steel.

## 3.2 Derivation of Power Density for a Circular Focal Spot

Assume the focused laser spot is a circle of diameter $d$ (mm). The area is:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Power density is power divided by area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \frac{P}{A} \\
    &= \frac{P}{\pi d^2 / 4} \\
    &= \frac{4 P}{\pi d^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example (Fiber Laser)

A 4 kW fiber laser is focused to a 0.4 mm spot. Compute $q''$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \frac{4 \cdot 4000}{\pi \cdot (0.4)^2} \\
    &= \frac{16000}{\pi \cdot 0.16} \\
    &= \frac{16000}{0.5027} \\
    &\approx 31{,}830 \;\; \text{W/mm}^2 \\
    &\approx 3.18 \times 10^4 \;\; \text{W/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This value is **well above the $10^6$ W/mm² threshold** for keyhole mode. The result is a deep-penetration weld.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the small spot matters:** Halving the spot diameter from 0.4 mm to 0.2 mm **quadruples** the power density. This is why precision focusing optics are critical in robotic LBW.

## 3.3 Derivation of Cooling Rate (3-D Thick Plate, Rosenthal's Equation)

For a **3-D point heat source moving on a thick plate**, Rosenthal (1941) gave the cooling rate at the weld centerline as:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = -2\pi k \rho c \cdot \frac{(T - T_0)^3}{(q/v)^2}$$

Derivation sketch (key transition points):

- Start with the 3-D heat conduction equation in a moving coordinate system attached to the source.
- Apply the point-source Green's function solution: $T - T_0 = \dfrac{q}{2\pi k R} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{v(R + x)}{2\alpha}\right)$, where $R$ is the radial distance and $x$ is the longitudinal distance.
- Differentiate with respect to time, evaluate at the weld centerline ($x = 0$, $R = 0$ in the limit), and simplify to the cubic form above.

### Numerical Example (Mild Steel)

Given: $k = 50$ W/m·K, $\rho c = 4.5 \times 10^6$ J/m³·K, $T - T_0 = 700$ °C, $q/v = 700$ J/mm = $7 \times 10^5$ J/m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dT}{dt} &= -2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 4.5\times 10^6 \cdot \frac{(700)^3}{(7\times 10^5)^2} \\
  &= -2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 4.5\times 10^6 \cdot \frac{3.43 \times 10^{8}}{4.9 \times 10^{11}} \\
  &= -2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 4.5\times 10^6 \cdot 7.0 \times 10^{-4} \\
  &= -2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 3150 \\
  &= -2\pi \cdot 157{,}500 \\
  &\approx -9.9 \times 10^5 \;\; \text{°C/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

A cooling rate near $10^6$ °C/s is characteristic of laser welds and explains the very fine microstructures observed.

## 3.4 FSW Process Parameter Relationship

The empirical "process window" for FSW is governed by a non-dimensional **pseudo-heat index**:

$$H_{FSW} = \frac{\omega^2}{v}$$

where $\omega$ is the rotational speed (rad/s) and $v$ is the traverse speed (mm/s).

- **Too low $H_{FSW}$**: Insufficient heat → tool pin binds, tunnel defect.
- **Too high $H_{FSW}$**: Excess heat → flash, thinning, surface galling.
- **Optimum window** for AA6061-T6: $H_{FSW}$ ≈ 2–5 (in SI units).

### Numerical Example

For 6061-T6 aluminum, optimum $\omega = 1000$ rpm = $104.7$ rad/s, $v = 100$ mm/min = $1.67$ mm/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{FSW} &= \frac{(104.7)^2}{1.67} \\
  &= \frac{10{,}962}{1.67} \\
  &\approx 6564 \;\; \text{(rad}^2/\text{mm·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well above the 2–5 range, indicating the speed should be increased (or $\omega$ decreased). A typical optimum is $v = 300$ mm/min = 5 mm/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{FSW} &= \frac{(104.7)^2}{5} = 2192 \;\; \text{rad}^2/\text{mm·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is on the upper edge — a slightly higher speed (~400 mm/min) would target ~3.0, ideal for AA6061.

## 3.5 Hybrid Laser-Arc Synergy — Effective Heat Input Model

A simplified model for the hybrid process effective heat input is:

$$H_{hyb} = \alpha \cdot H_{laser} + \beta \cdot H_{arc}$$

where $\alpha \approx 0.9$ and $\beta \approx 0.7$ are empirical coupling factors (less than 1 because each source helps the other). The observed travel-speed synergy is:

$$v_{hyb} = v_{laser} + 1.5 \cdot v_{arc}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The arc preheats the metal, increasing the laser absorption coefficient. The laser keyhole, in turn, provides a low-resistance current path, stabilizing the arc at much higher speeds than either alone. This is why hybrid welding is **not merely additive** — it's a true synergy.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Examination Favourite)

| Parameter | GMAW (MIG) | PAW | LBW | EBW | FSW |
|-----------|------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Power density (W/mm²) | $10^2$–$10^3$ | $10^3$–$10^5$ | $10^6$–$10^8$ | $10^8$–$10^9$ | N/A (mechanical) |
| Penetration (mm) | 3–8 | 6–12 | 1–25 | 1–300 | 1–50 |
| DWR (depth/width) | 0.5–1 | 1–3 | 3–10 | 10–30 | N/A |
| Typical travel speed (m/min) | 0.5–1.5 | 0.3–1.0 | 1–10 | 1–5 | 0.05–0.5 |
| Heat input (kJ/mm) | 0.5–1.5 | 0.3–0.8 | 0.05–0.3 | 0.05–0.2 | N/A (mechanical) |
| Atmosphere | Open / shielding gas | Open / shielding gas | Open / shielding gas | **Vacuum required** | Open / shielding gas |
| Filler metal | Yes | Optional | Optional | No | No |
| HAZ severity | Moderate | Moderate | Small | Very small | Low (T < T_melt) |
| Robotic ease | High | High | High (with sensor) | Limited (chamber) | Special (force) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Robotic Laser Beam Welding Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Laser Source Fiber 4kW] --> fbA[Fiber Optic Cable]
    fbA --> clA[Collimator Lens]
    clA --> flA[Focusing Lens]
    flA --> kpA[Keyhole Weld Pool]
    kpA --> wpA[Workpiece on Positioner]
    shA[Shielding Gas Nozzle He-Ar] --> kpA
    robA[6-Axis Robot Arm] --> clA
    robA --> shA
    seA[Seam Tracking Sensor] --> robA
    ctrlA[Robot Controller] --> robA
    ctrlA --> srcA
    seA --> ctrlA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **laser source** delivers photons through a fiber cable to a **collimator–focusing lens** assembly mounted on the robot's end-effector.
- The robot moves the focusing head over the workpiece, maintaining a constant stand-off distance (controlled by an optical seam tracker).
- Shielding gas is delivered **coaxially** with the beam to suppress plasma plume formation and oxidation.
- A central **robot controller** synchronizes the laser power waveform, robot trajectory, and seam-tracker feedback loop.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Robotic Friction Stir Welding Cell

```mermaid
graph TD
    spnA[FSW Spindle with Pin and Shoulder] --> toolA[Tool Engagement Zone]
    toolA --> matA[Plasticized Material Flow]
    matA --> advA[Advancing Side]
    matA --> retA[Retreating Side]
    fzA[Force Torque Sensor] --> spnA
    fzA --> fbcA[Force Feedback Controller]
    fbcA --> robA[Heavy Payload Robot 1000 kg]
    robA --> spnA
    posA[Workpiece Clamped on Positioner] --> toolA
    coolA[Cooling Chuck Optional] --> posA
    invA[Parameter Logging T N v] --> fbcA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The FSW spindle is mounted on a **heavy-payload robot** (1-ton class) because the process requires 5–15 kN of axial downforce.
- A **force/torque sensor** between the spindle and the robot flange provides real-time downforce feedback, allowing the controller to adjust robot Z-position to maintain constant forging pressure.
- The plasticized material flows from the **advancing side** (where tool rotation and traverse are in the same direction) to the **retreating side**.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hybrid Laser-Arc Welding Head

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcB[Laser Source] --> optB[Beam Shaping Optics]
    arcB[MIG Torch with Two Wire Feeds] --> headB[Hybrid Welding Head]
    optB --> headB
    headB --> jointB[Joint Zone 1-2 mm Gap]
    gasB[Coaxial Shielding Gas] --> headB
    senB[Optical Emission Sensor] --> headB
    senB --> ctlB[Hybrid Process Controller]
    ctlB --> srcB
    ctlB --> arcB
    ctlB --> robB[6-Axis Robot with Force Sensor]
    robB --> headB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **laser optics** and **MIG torch** are mechanically integrated into a single **hybrid head** with a precise relative offset (typically 2–5 mm between laser focal point and arc contact tip).
- The **optical emission sensor** monitors the plasma plume and feeds back to the **hybrid process controller**, which modulates laser power and arc current in real time to maintain stability across the joint gap.
- A **6-axis robot** with a force/torque sensor holds the hybrid head, allowing real-time seam tracking and adaptive gap bridging.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Tandem MIG/MAG Torch Internals

```mermaid
graph LR
    wireA[Leading Wire Drive] --> ctA[Contact Tip 1]
    wireB[Trailing Wire Drive] --> ctB[Contact Tip 2]
    ctA --> arcA[Leading Arc Dip Transfer]
    ctB --> arcB[Trailing Arc Spray Transfer]
    arcA --> poolA[Common Weld Pool]
    arcB --> poolA
    gasA[Shielding Gas Ar-CO2] --> ctA
    gasA --> ctB
    pwrA[Synergic Power Source] --> wireA
    pwrA --> wireB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **leading wire** runs at lower current to give a dip-transfer arc (good root fusion).
- The **trailing wire** runs at higher current to give spray transfer (high deposition).
- Both arcs share a **common weld pool**, with the trailing arc's metal deposition filling the crater left by the leading arc.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Narrow-Gap Welding with Oscillating Torch

```mermaid
graph TD
    oscA[Oscillation Mechanism Cam or Servo] --> trA[Robot-Attached Torch]
    grA[Narrow Groove 8-12 mm Wide] --> trA
    lay1[Pass 1 Left Side] --> layN[Pass N Right Side]
    layN --> grA
    senA[Lance Arc Sensor] --> oscA
    senA --> trA
    ctlA[Robot Controller with Weave Pattern] --> oscA
    ctlA --> senA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The torch oscillates in a **triangular or sinusoidal weave pattern** within the narrow groove.
- A **lance arc sensor** (measures the arc voltage variation as the torch weaves) provides feedback for joint tracking.
- The robot lays down **multiple passes**, each on alternating sides, until the groove is filled.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Distinguish between conduction mode and keyhole mode in Laser Beam Welding. Mention the typical power density ranges for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode |
|--------|-----------------|--------------|
| Power density | $< 10^6$ W/mm² | $> 10^6$ W/mm² |
| Pool shape | Wide, shallow, hemispherical | Deep, narrow, nail-head |
| Depth-to-width ratio | < 1 | 3:1 to 10:1 |
| Mechanism | Heat is conducted into the workpiece without vaporization | Beam vaporizes metal; vapor capillary traps beam |
| Typical use | Thin sheet cosmetic welds | Deep-penetration structural welds |

*(1 mark: correct power density; 1 mark: depth/width distinction; 1 mark: mechanism)*

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Why is Electron Beam Welding restricted to a vacuum chamber, and what types of components are typically EB-welded in industry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EBW is performed in a vacuum ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa) for three reasons:
1. **Preventing electron scattering** — Electrons at 60–150 kV would be scattered by air molecules, losing focus and energy. *(1 mark)*
2. **Preventing oxidation and contamination** — Reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Nb) and aerospace alloys must be welded in oxygen-free environments. *(1 mark)*
3. **Avoiding X-ray emission hazards** — High-energy electrons colliding with air produce bremsstrahlung X-rays, which are safely contained by the chamber walls. *(1 mark)*

**Typical applications:** Turbine blades, gearbox housings, missile components, sensors, medical implants, and nuclear-fuel cladding.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Option A) — **14 Marks**
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 2 Internal Choice]**
> **(a)** Explain the principle of operation of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the tool pin and shoulder. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A robotic FSW cell is set up to weld AA6061-T6 plates of 6 mm thickness. The tool rotational speed is 1200 rpm and traverse speed is 200 mm/min. Compute the pseudo-heat index $H_{FSW} = \omega^2 / v$ in SI units (rad²/mm·s) and comment on whether the process is in the optimum window. Take the recommended window for AA6061 as $H_{FSW} = 1500$–$3000$ rad²/mm·s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — FSW Principle (7 marks)**

1. **Tool Components (2 marks):**
   - The FSW tool has two parts: a **shoulder** (larger diameter, 12–25 mm) and a **pin** (smaller, 4–10 mm, with threaded or fluted profile).
   - The **pin** stirs the plasticized material and traverses the joint line.
   - The **shoulder** generates most of the frictional heat (because of its larger contact area) and applies the **forging pressure** that consolidates the material behind the pin.

2. **Process Stages (3 marks):**
   - **Plunge:** The rotating tool is plunged into the joint until the shoulder contacts the surface.
   - **Dwell:** A short pause (1–3 s) to preheat the material and stabilize the temperature.
   - **Traverse:** The tool is moved along the joint at a programmed speed; the rotating pin plasticizes and stirs the material from the **advancing side** to the **retreating side**, where it consolidates under the shoulder.
   - **Pull-out / retract:** At the end of the weld, the tool is retracted, leaving an exit hole.

3. **Microstructural Zones (2 marks):**
   - **Nugget (Stir Zone):** Fully recrystallized fine-grained equiaxed zone.
   - **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ):** Deformed but not recrystallized.
   - **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ):** No deformation, only thermal exposure.
   - **Base Metal:** Unaffected parent material.

> **Sketch (use the Mermaid block from SECTION 4.2 for reference; the examiner expects a labelled hand-sketch with Advancing Side, Retreating Side, Nugget, and TMAZ.)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Given:
- $\omega = 1200$ rpm = $1200 \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ rad/s
- $v = 200$ mm/min

**Step 1:** Convert $\omega$ to rad/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 1200 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} \\
       &= 1200 \times 0.10472 \\
       &= 125.66 \;\; \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Convert $v$ to mm/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{200}{60} = 3.333 \;\; \text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Compute $H_{FSW} = \omega^2 / v$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{FSW} &= \frac{(125.66)^2}{3.333} \\
       &= \frac{15{,}790}{3.333} \\
       &\approx 4739 \;\; \text{rad}^2/\text{mm·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for substitution, 1 for squaring, 1 for division)*

**Step 4:** Comment. The recommended optimum window is $1500$–$3000$ rad²/mm·s. The computed value $H_{FSW} \approx 4739$ is **above** this window.

*(2 marks)*

**Engineering Implication:** The high heat index suggests **excess heat input**, which can cause:
- Surface galling and flash formation.
- Thinning of the weld nugget (loss of mechanical strength).
- Coarse grains in the TMAZ, reducing ductility.

**Corrective Action:** Either **reduce $\omega$** to ~900 rpm or **increase $v$** to ~315 mm/min, which gives $H_{FSW} = \dfrac{(94.25)^2}{5.25} \approx 1692$ rad²/mm·s, the lower edge of the optimum window.

*(Bonus 1 mark for correct engineering action.)*

---

### Question B2 (Option B) — **14 Marks** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Internal Choice]**
> **(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the principle of **Hybrid Laser-Arc Welding (HLAW)**. Discuss the synergy between the laser and the arc, and state **three** industrial applications. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A robotic MIG cell operates at $V = 30$ V, $I = 280$ A, $v = 6$ mm/s, with process efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. A 4 kW fiber laser with absorption coefficient $\alpha = 0.4$ is added to form a hybrid cell at the **same** traverse speed. Compute the **total effective heat input** per unit length using the simplified model $H_{hyb} = \alpha_L \cdot H_{laser} + \beta_A \cdot H_{arc}$, where $\alpha_L = 0.9$, $\beta_A = 0.7$. Comment on whether the hybrid process can be operated at **twice the speed** for the same total heat input. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — HLAW Principle (7 marks)**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** HLAW couples a **focused laser beam** and a **MIG/MAG arc** in the same weld pool, using a single hybrid welding head. The laser preheats the metal and forms a keyhole; the MIG arc fills the keyhole with filler metal, bridges fit-up gaps, and stabilizes the process.

2. **Synergy (3 marks):**
   - **Arc stabilization by laser plasma:** The laser's keyhole provides a low-resistance current path, allowing the arc to remain stable at much higher speeds than MIG alone.
   - **Increased laser absorption:** The arc preheats the metal, lowering its reflectivity. For aluminum, absorption can rise from ~7% to ~30–40%.
   - **Gap bridging:** The arc can bridge fit-up gaps of 1–2 mm that the laser alone cannot tolerate.

3. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Use the Mermaid block from SECTION 4.3 as a reference; expected labels: laser source, MIG torch, hybrid head, weld pool, shielding gas, sensor, robot.

4. **Three Industrial Applications (1 mark):**
   - Automotive tailored blank welding (e.g., VW, Audi body-in-white).
   - Shipbuilding thick-plate butt joints.
   - Pressure vessel circumferential seams.
   - Railway car body aluminum welding.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute arc heat input per unit length.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{arc} &= \frac{0.8 \cdot 30 \cdot 280}{6 \cdot 1000} \\
        &= \frac{6720}{6000} \\
        &= 1.12 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2:** Compute laser energy per unit length.

The laser delivers 4 kW of optical power, but only the fraction $\alpha = 0.4$ is absorbed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{abs} &= 0.4 \cdot 4000 = 1600 \;\; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_L &= \frac{P_{abs}}{v} = \frac{1600 \;\; \text{W}}{6 \;\; \text{mm/s}} = 266.67 \;\; \text{J/mm} = 0.267 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3:** Compute total hybrid heat input.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{hyb} &= 0.9 \cdot 0.267 + 0.7 \cdot 1.12 \\
        &= 0.240 + 0.784 \\
        &= 1.024 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4:** If the speed is **doubled** to $v' = 12$ mm/s, the heat input per unit length becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H'_{hyb} &= \frac{1.024}{2} = 0.512 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Halving the heat input to 0.512 kJ/mm reduces the HAZ width and distortion dramatically. However, the laser absorption may drop (less preheating time) and the keyhole stability may be compromised. In practice, **yes**, hybrid processes can usually run at 1.5×–3× the speed of MIG alone for the same total heat input, which is precisely the source of the productivity gain in industrial hybrid cells.

*(1 mark for conclusion with justified comment)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Advanced Welding Technique Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the /1000 in heat input formula** — leads to a 10× error. Always verify units: kJ/mm requires /1000.
> 2. **Mixing up the FSW advancing and retreating sides** — the advancing side has the tool rotation vector pointing in the direction of travel; the retreating side has them opposing. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 3. **Confusing EBW vacuum requirements with laser welding** — EBW **always** requires a vacuum; LBW does not.
> 4. **Writing "PAW uses higher current than TIG"** — this is *technically* true for keyhole PAW, but the *defining* feature is the **constricting nozzle**, not the current. Examiners look for the nozzle as the key concept.
> 5. **Ignoring the tool force in FSW** — students often write about heat and stir but forget the **forging pressure** from the shoulder that consolidates the weld. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Stating "hybrid welding is just laser + MIG"** — the **synergy** is the key concept. Just adding the two processes does not give the productivity gain. The combination must share the same weld pool.
> 7. **Using SI units inconsistently** — KTU examiners expect mm and mm/s in welding, not m and m/s. Mixing units is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points (High-Density)**

- **Advanced welding techniques** = processes with power density $> 10^5$ W/mm² OR solid-state processes.
- **Four families:** Beam (LBW, EBW), Plasma (PAW), Solid-State (FSW), Hybrid (HLAW, Tandem, NGW).
- **LBW keyhole threshold:** $> 10^6$ W/mm²; DWR can exceed 10:1.
- **Fiber laser absorption on Al:** starts at ~7%, rises with preheat; this is why hybrid (laser + arc) works on aluminum.
- **EBW requires vacuum** ($10^{-2}$–$10^{-4}$ Pa); not suitable for large assemblies.
- **PAW defining feature:** constricting nozzle — NOT the current value.
- **FSW peak temperature:** 0.7–0.9 × $T_{melt}$ — solid-state, no melting.
- **FSW requires high axial force** (5–15 kN for steel) — heavy-payload robots only.
- **FSW pseudo-heat index:** $H_{FSW} = \omega^2 / v$ — process window concept.
- **Hybrid laser-arc welding:** synergy factor of 2–5× speed over MIG; gap-bridging up to 2 mm.
- **Tandem MIG:** two independent wires, leading (dip) + trailing (spray); 2–3× deposition rate.
- **Narrow-gap welding:** groove width 8–12 mm; uses oscillating/triangular weave patterns; saves 60–80% filler.
- **Heat input formula:** $H = \eta V I / (v \cdot 1000)$ — in **kJ/mm** — memorize units.
- **Power density formula:** $q'' = 4P / (\pi d^2)$ — for circular focal spot.
- **DWR comparison:** MIG ≈ 0.5–1, PAW ≈ 1–3, LBW ≈ 3–10, EBW ≈ 10–30.
- **Robotic sensor types for advanced welding:** optical seam tracker, through-the-arc tracker, laser-vision camera, force/torque sensor (FSW).
- **Cooling rate scales as $1/(q/v)^2$** — lower heat input → much higher cooling rate → finer microstructure.
- **Industrial applications:** tailored blanks (LBW), gearbox welding (EBW), aerospace Ti (PAW/EBW), shipbuilding (Tandem, NGW), rail cars (HLAW), aerospace fuel tanks (FSW).
- **KTU favourite exam hook:** "Compare the depth-to-width ratio of MIG vs LBW" — always answer with **numerical DWR ranges** plus mechanism explanation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Welding** is an advanced real-time control methodology in robotic arc welding wherein the welding system **dynamically modifies process parameters** (arc voltage, welding current, wire feed rate, travel speed, and torch position/orientation) in response to **continuous feedback signals** obtained from sensors monitoring the weld joint geometry, arc behaviour, and weld pool dynamics. This ensures consistent weld quality despite variations in joint fit-up, plate misalignment, thermal distortion, and material inconsistencies.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, adaptive welding is classified under the broader umbrella of **Intelligent Welding Systems** and forms a critical sub-module of robotic process automation. It transforms a conventional "teach-and-playback" robot into a **closed-loop intelligent manipulator** capable of self-correction.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Meaning in Welding Context |
|---|---|
| **Joint Tracking** | Real-time identification of weld groove position |
| **Through-the-Arc Sensing (TTAS)** | Using the arc itself as a sensor for joint detection |
| **Seam Finding** | Process of locating the start and end of a weld |
| **Adaptive Control** | Closed-loop modification of welding parameters |
| **Fit-up Tolerance** | Acceptable geometric deviation of the joint |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" of Welding

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine driving a car on a road with potholes and lane shifts, but your hands are tied and cannot move the steering wheel. The car will eventually fall into a pothole or veer off the road. Now imagine an **autonomous car** with cameras and sensors that constantly sense the road geometry and automatically adjust steering, speed, and braking. **Adaptive welding is precisely this autonomous car concept** applied to a welding torch following a weld seam. The robot's "eyes" are its sensors, its "brain" is the adaptive controller, and its "hands" are the welding torch actuators.

### 1.4 Why Adaptive Welding is Necessary

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes understanding the **motivation** behind adaptive welding. Consider the following real-world scenarios where rigid pre-programmed welding fails:

1. **Thermal Distortion:** Heat input causes the workpiece to warp during welding, shifting the seam from its original position.
2. **Fit-up Variations:** Two plates meant to be butted with a 2 mm gap may arrive with a 0.5 mm to 4 mm gap in practice.
3. **Plate Thickness Variations:** Rolling tolerances cause ±5% thickness variation.
4. **Tack Weld Displacement:** Tack welds used for clamping may shift slightly before final welding.
5. **Clamping Errors:** Fixtures cannot guarantee perfect repeatability across hundreds of parts.

### 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU-Expected Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key industrial metrics that examiners expect:
> - **Seam tracking accuracy:** $\pm 0.1$ mm to $\pm 0.3$ mm in modern systems
> - **Sampling rate of optical sensors:** **1 kHz to 10 kHz**
> - **Through-the-arc sampling rate:** **50 Hz to 500 Hz**
> - **Adaptive response time:** **10 ms to 50 ms**
> - **Joint detection lateral resolution:** $\le 0.2$ mm

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Adaptive Welding System

An adaptive welding system can be decomposed into five interconnected functional blocks. Each block performs a distinct task, and the synergy between them is what enables true adaptability.

#### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic Chain

1. **Sensor Data Acquisition Block:**
   - Optical, electrical, or tactile sensors capture raw data about the joint.
   - Data is converted from analog to digital via ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter).
   - Sampling rate must be at least **2× the highest expected frequency** of joint variation (Nyquist criterion).

2. **Signal Pre-Processing Block:**
   - Noise filtering (low-pass, Kalman filter, moving average).
   - Feature extraction (edge detection, cross-correlation with templates).
   - Calculation of joint centreline, gap width, and torch-to-joint lateral offset.

3. **Decision / Control Algorithm Block:**
   - Compares measured offset with the desired trajectory.
   - Computes correction signals using PID, fuzzy logic, or neural network controllers.
   - Outputs corrected values for torch position and welding parameters.

4. **Actuator / Drive Block:**
   - Servo motors and servo valves actuate the welding torch cross-seam, vertical, and torch-angle motions.
   - Wire feeder adjusts wire feed rate; power source adjusts voltage/current.

5. **Welding Process Block:**
   - The corrected torch position and parameters produce the actual weld.
   - This real output becomes the input to the next sensing cycle — **closing the loop**.

### 2.2 Detailed Sensor Taxonomy

| Sensor Type | Principle | Sampling Rate | Accuracy | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Through-the-Arc (TATT)** | Measures arc current/voltage variation due to stand-off change | 50–500 Hz | $\pm 0.3$ mm | High — most economical |
| **Laser Line Triangulation** | Projects laser stripe; measures reflected angle | 1–10 kHz | $\pm 0.05$ mm | Very High — gold standard |
| **Laser Stripe Scanner (2D)** | Scans line laser across seam; builds 2D profile | 100 Hz–2 kHz | $\pm 0.1$ mm | High — for T-joints |
| **Vision (Passive CCD/CMOS)** | Image processing of arc-illuminated groove | 30–60 Hz | $\pm 0.2$ mm | Moderate |
| **Structured Light (3D)** | Pattern projection + stereo cameras | 60 Hz | $\pm 0.15$ mm | Emerging |
| **Tactile / Mechanical Probe** | Physical contact with groove walls | < 10 Hz | $\pm 0.1$ mm | Pre-weld only |
| **Arc Length (Capacitive/Inductive)** | Monitors arc voltage to control stand-off | Up to 1 kHz | Sub-mm | Very High — basic |

### 2.3 Through-the-Arc Sensing (TTAS) — The Workhorse Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TTAS is the most frequently examined adaptive sensing method in KTU papers.** A student must master its operating principle.

#### 2.3.1 Underlying Physics

In Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW), the welding current $I$ depends on the **arc length** $\ell$ according to the static arc characteristic:

$$I = f(\ell, V)$$

Where:
- $I$ = welding current (Amperes)
- $\ell$ = arc length (mm)
- $V$ = arc voltage (Volts)

For a constant voltage power source, when the torch **rises** above the workpiece, $\ell$ increases, current $I$ decreases. When the torch **descends**, $\ell$ decreases, $I$ increases. By oscillating the torch perpendicular to the seam (weaving) and measuring $I$, the joint centreline can be detected because the lateral change in plate height at the groove walls causes current peaks.

### 2.4 Seam Tracking Mathematical Framework

Consider a torch weaving sinusoidally across the seam with amplitude $A$ and frequency $f_w$. The lateral torch position is:

$$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_w t)$$

The measured current signal $I(t)$ during weaving produces a waveform whose **peak locations** correspond to the **groove walls** (where stand-off momentarily decreases). The **midpoint between peaks** is the seam centre.

The lateral error $e_x$ (torch offset from seam centre) is:

$$e_x = \frac{x_{peak,left} + x_{peak,right}}{2} - x_{nominal,centre}$$

### 2.5 Adaptive Control Law — PID Form

The most common adaptive control law is the **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)** controller acting on the lateral offset:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ = control output (lateral correction in mm or motor pulse count)
- $e(t)$ = lateral offset error
- $K_p$ = proportional gain
- $K_i$ = integral gain
- $K_d$ = derivative gain
- $t$ = time

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula for this topic. Master these for guaranteed marks.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arc current dependence on length | $I = f(\ell, V)$ | TTAS principle | A |
| 2 | Arc voltage drop approximation | $V_{arc} = V_0 + E \cdot \ell$ | Linearised arc model | V |
| 3 | Heat input per unit length | $H = \frac{\eta V I}{v}$ | Weld quality metric | J/mm |
| 4 | Lateral weaving position | $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_w t)$ | TTAS oscillation | mm |
| 5 | Lateral offset error | $e_x = \dfrac{x_{L} + x_{R}}{2} - x_{centre}$ | Seam tracking | mm |
| 6 | PID control law | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Adaptive control | pulses |
| 7 | Nyquist sampling criterion | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Sensor selection | Hz |
| 8 | Cross-correlation peak (seam match) | $R(\tau) = \int s(t) \cdot p(t+\tau)\, dt$ | Vision seam finding | — |
| 9 | Kalman state update (linear) | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$ | Sensor fusion | — |
| 10 | Thermal distortion rate (approx.) | $\dfrac{d\delta}{dt} = \alpha \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Compensation need | mm/s |
| 11 | Weld pool area detection (vision) | $A_{pool} = \pi \cdot r_{pool}^2$ | Penetration control | mm² |
| 12 | Wire feed rate to deposition relation | $W = \rho \cdot A_{wire} \cdot v_{feed}$ | Adaptive WFR | g/s |

Where in the above formulas:
- $V_0$ = cathode/anode fall voltage (~15 V typical)
- $E$ = electric field in arc column (~1.5 V/mm)
- $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.6–0.85 for GMAW)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s)
- $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coefficient (1.2×10⁻⁵ /°C for steel)
- $\rho$ = wire density
- $A_{wire}$ = wire cross-sectional area
- $K_k$ = Kalman gain
- $z_k$ = measurement vector

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Adaptive welding is the **enabling technology** behind the manufacture of:
> - **Ship hull panels** (long seams with high distortion — 30+ m lengths)
> - **Automotive BIW (Body-in-White)** sub-assemblies (high fit-up variation)
> - **Pressure vessels and boilers** (zero-defect mandatory)
> - **Offshore platform legs** (thick-plate, high restraint)
> - **Railway bogie frames** (legacy parts with poor fit-up)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linearised Arc Characteristic Model for TTAS

We derive the relationship between arc length variation and measurable current variation. This derivation is **routinely tested in KTU 14-mark problems**.

#### Step 1: Start with the Static Arc Equation

The arc between the wire tip and workpiece behaves like a non-linear resistor. The arc voltage $V_{arc}$ can be written as:

$$V_{arc} = V_0 + E \cdot \ell$$

Where:
- $V_0$ = sum of cathode and anode voltage drops (~15 V)
- $E$ = voltage gradient across the arc column (~1.0–1.5 V/mm)
- $\ell$ = arc length (mm)

#### Step 2: Apply the Power Source Constraint

For a **constant-voltage (CV) power source** (most common in GMAW):

$$V_s = V_{arc} + I \cdot R_c$$

Where:
- $V_s$ = power source set voltage (constant)
- $R_c$ = cable + contact resistance (assumed constant)

#### Step 3: Solve for Current

Rearranging:

$$I = \frac{V_s - V_{arc}}{R_c} = \frac{V_s - V_0 - E \cdot \ell}{R_c}$$

#### Step 4: Differentiate with Respect to Arc Length

$$\frac{dI}{d\ell} = -\frac{E}{R_c}$$

This is the **TTAS sensitivity coefficient**. For typical values ($E = 1.5$ V/mm, $R_c = 0.02$ $\Omega$):

$$\frac{dI}{d\ell} = -\frac{1.5}{0.02} = -75 \text{ A/mm}$$

#### Step 5: Interpret the Result

A **1 mm increase in arc length** produces a **75 A decrease** in current. This high sensitivity is precisely what makes TTAS feasible without external sensors — the arc itself is the transducer.

$$\boxed{\Delta I = -\frac{E}{R_c} \cdot \Delta \ell = -75 \cdot \Delta \ell \text{ A/mm}}$$

#### Step 6: Practical Implication for Seam Tracking

During torch weaving, when the torch crosses the groove wall, the **local stand-off drops** (the groove is slightly deeper than the plate surface). This produces a measurable current spike:

$$\Delta \ell \approx 0.5 \text{ mm (groove depth)} \Rightarrow \Delta I \approx 37.5 \text{ A}$$

This is easily detectable above the baseline current ripple (typically ±5 A).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Lateral Offset Error from Weaving Current Profile

#### Step 1: Set Up the Weaving Signal

The torch moves sinusoidally with amplitude $A$:

$$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_w t)$$

#### Step 2: Hypothesised Current Response

Assume the current profile during weaving is also sinusoidal but **phase-shifted** by $\phi$ due to seam offset:

$$I(t) = I_0 + \Delta I_{max} \sin(2\pi f_w t + \phi)$$

#### Step 3: Extract Seam Centre from Current Peaks

The current peaks occur at times $t_L$ (left wall) and $t_R$ (right wall):

$$t_L = \frac{T_w}{4} - \frac{\phi T_w}{2\pi}, \quad t_R = \frac{3T_w}{4} - \frac{\phi T_w}{2\pi}$$

#### Step 4: Compute Lateral Positions of Walls

$$x_L = A \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = A \cos\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$x_R = A \sin\left(\frac{3\pi}{2} - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = -A \cos\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

#### Step 5: Compute the Midpoint (Seam Centre)

$$x_{centre} = \frac{x_L + x_R}{2} = \frac{A \cos(\phi/2) - A \cos(\phi/2)}{2} = 0$$

This is the **nominal seam centre**. Any deviation from this measured by the controller gives the lateral error $e_x$:

$$\boxed{e_x = x_{torch} - x_{centre}}$$

#### Step 6: Final PID Correction Output

$$\boxed{u(t) = K_p e_x + K_i \int_0^t e_x(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de_x}{dt}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Decision Table

| Joint Type | Material | Distortion Risk | Recommended Sensor | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Butt joint, thin plate** | Mild steel | Low | TTAS or Laser line | Low cost, sufficient accuracy |
| **Lap joint, automotive** | Galvanised steel | Moderate | Laser triangulation | Gap detection capability |
| **T-fillet, shipbuilding** | DH36 steel | Very High | 2D Laser stripe scanner | 3D profile needed |
| **Pipe circumferential** | Stainless steel | Moderate | Vision + TTAS hybrid | Curved surface compensation |
| **Aluminium, automotive** | Al 5xxx/6xxx | High (high $\alpha$) | Optical (coaxial camera) | High thermal expansion |
| **Clad steel, pressure vessel** | SA 387 | Very High | Laser + Kalman filter | Multi-layer deposition |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Adaptive Control Strategies Compared

| Strategy | Complexity | Robustness | Computational Load | KTU Exam Likelihood |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PID** | Low | Moderate | Low | Very High |
| **Fuzzy Logic** | Moderate | High | Moderate | High |
| **Neural Network (ANN)** | High | Very High | High | Moderate |
| **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** | Very High | Very High | Very High | Low (advanced topic) |
| **Adaptive Pole Placement** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Adaptive Welding Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete signal flow** of an adaptive welding system. Study this carefully — it appears almost verbatim in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Trajectory / Set Points] --> B[Comparator - subtract]
    S1[Sensor Block - Arc Voltage or Laser] --> B
    B --> C[Controller Block - PID / Fuzzy / ANN]
    C --> D[Actuator Block - Servo Motors and Wire Feeder]
    D --> E[Welding Torch and Process]
    E --> S1
    E --> W[Welded Joint Output]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000
    style S1 fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (top) processes the *desired* vs *measured* lateral offset.
- The **inner loop** (bottom) closes around the welding process — this is the actual feedback path.
- The **orange reference** flows in from above; the **green process** is measured by sensors; the **red controller** computes corrections.

---

### 4.2 Through-the-Arc Sensing (TTAS) — Detailed Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Robot Controller initiates weave motion] --> A2[Torch oscillates laterally across seam]
    A2 --> A3[Arc length varies as torch crosses groove walls]
    A3 --> A4[Welding current I changes per arc equation]
    A4 --> A5[Current signal sampled via shunt resistor]
    A5 --> A6[ADC converts analog current to digital]
    A6 --> A7[Peak detection algorithm identifies groove walls]
    A7 --> A8[Compute lateral offset error ex]
    A8 --> A9[PID controller computes correction u of t]
    A9 --> A10[Robot adjusts torch lateral position]
    A10 --> A11[Closed loop continues at sampling rate fs]
    style A4 fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#000
    style A8 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000
    style A10 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Laser Triangulation Seam Tracking Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Laser Diode emits stripe] --> L2[Stripe projected onto weld groove]
    L2 --> L3[Reflected stripe captured by CMOS sensor]
    L3 --> L4[Triangulation algorithm computes depth profile]
    L4 --> L5[2D cross-section of joint obtained]
    L5 --> L6[Seam centre and gap width extracted]
    L6 --> L7[Correction sent to robot controller]
    style L1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style L4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style L7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

    H2 --> H3[Level 3 - On-the-fly Adaptive Control]
