## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define adaptive welding. State two situations where conventional robotic welding fails but adaptive welding succeeds.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive welding** is a real-time control strategy in robotic arc welding in which welding parameters (torch position, arc voltage, current, wire feed rate, travel speed) are **automatically adjusted** during the welding process based on continuous sensor feedback monitoring the weld joint, arc, and weld pool.

**Two situations where conventional robotic welding fails:**
1. **Thermal distortion during long seams:** Heat input causes the workpiece to warp, shifting the seam from its pre-programmed path. A teach-and-playback robot cannot detect this shift; adaptive welding continuously senses and corrects.
2. **Variable joint fit-up:** In butt joints of ship hull panels, the gap between plates varies between 1 mm and 4 mm along the seam length. Adaptive welding adjusts arc voltage and travel speed in real time to maintain consistent penetration despite the gap variation.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two situations. Mentioning "closed-loop feedback" is essential.)*

---

#### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List four sensors used for seam tracking in adaptive welding. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Sensor | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Through-the-Arc Sensor (TTAS)** | No additional hardware; arc itself acts as sensor |
| 2 | **Laser Triangulation Sensor** | High accuracy (±0.05 mm); independent of arc |
| 3 | **CCD/CMOS Vision Camera** | Provides full 2D image; suitable for complex joints |
| 4 | **Mechanical Tactile Probe** | Robust; works in presence of heavy arc glare |

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for listing 4 sensors, 1 mark for advantages, 1 mark for overall presentation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations, students must answer **one full question from each module**, with internal choice between two full questions. Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) Explain the principle of Through-the-Arc Sensing (TTAS) for seam tracking with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between arc length variation and welding current. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Principle of TTAS (3 marks):**

TTAS exploits the inherent relationship between arc length $\ell$ and welding current $I$ in a constant-voltage (CV) GMAW process. As the welding torch is moved laterally (weaving motion) across the seam, the arc length changes when it encounters the groove walls. This produces measurable current variations which can be processed to determine the seam's lateral position.

The torch performs a sinusoidal weave: $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_w t)$

Current peaks occur when the torch crosses groove walls because the stand-off distance momentarily decreases at the groove edge (the groove is recessed relative to the plate surface).

**2. Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting with the arc characteristic equation:

$$V_{arc} = V_0 + E \cdot \ell$$

For a constant-voltage source: $V_s = V_{arc} + I R_c$

Substituting:

$$V_s = V_0 + E \cdot \ell + I R_c$$

Solving for $I$:

$$I = \frac{V_s - V_0 - E\ell}{R_c}$$

Differentiating with respect to $\ell$:

$$\frac{dI}{d\ell} = -\frac{E}{R_c}$$

For typical values ($E = 1.5$ V/mm, $R_c = 0.02$ $\Omega$):

$$\frac{dI}{d\ell} = -75 \text{ A/mm}$$

A 1 mm arc length increase → 75 A current decrease. This sensitivity enables reliable seam detection.

The lateral offset error is calculated as:

$$e_x = \frac{x_L + x_R}{2} - x_{nominal}$$

This $e_x$ is fed to the PID controller:

$$u(t) = K_p e_x + K_i \int e_x\, dt + K_d \frac{de_x}{dt}$$

**3. Block Diagram (within 3 marks allocation):** Refer to Section 4.2 above.

---

**(b) Compare Through-the-Arc Sensing and Laser Triangulation Sensing for adaptive welding. Discuss their suitability for ship hull welding. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | TTAS | Laser Triangulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware cost** | Very low (uses arc) | High (laser + camera) |
| **Sampling rate** | 50–500 Hz | 1–10 kHz |
| **Lateral accuracy** | ±0.3 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| **Vertical (height) accuracy** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Gap measurement** | Indirect | Direct |
| **Susceptibility to arc glare** | None | Requires optical filtering |
| **Material conductivity dependence** | Yes (only conductive joints) | No |
| **Joint type versatility** | Butt only | Butt, lap, T-fillet |
| **Calibration effort** | Minimal | Significant |

**Suitability for Ship Hull Welding (3 marks):**

Ship hull panels are typically **20–40 m long butt joints** in **DH36 grade steel** with high thermal distortion. The recommended choice is a **hybrid system**:

- **Initial coarse tracking** using TTAS (cheap, robust, high-temperature tolerant)
- **Fine correction** using laser triangulation (high accuracy for gap control in multi-pass welding)

For **single-pass root runs** in narrow-gap preparations, TTAS alone is sufficient. For **multi-pass fill and cap passes** with variable groove width, laser sensors are essential to prevent lack of penetration or excessive deposition.

Pure TTAS is preferred when:
- Cost is constrained
- Joint is consistent butt type
- Working environment has heavy smoke obscuring optical paths

Pure laser triangulation is preferred when:
- Multiple joint types are welded on the same line
- Gap detection and adaptive voltage are needed
- High cosmetic quality of cap passes is required

**Conclusion:** Shipyards increasingly deploy **dual-sensor hybrid systems** to leverage the strengths of both.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) Explain the architecture of an adaptive welding system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**

An adaptive welding system is a **closed-loop, real-time control system** consisting of five interconnected blocks. The system continuously measures the welding process, compares the measurement with the desired state, computes corrections, and applies them — all within milliseconds.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 (Closed-Loop Adaptive Welding Architecture).

**Function of Each Block (3 marks):**

1. **Reference Input Block:** Stores the desired torch trajectory, voltage, current, wire feed rate, and travel speed from the offline programming phase. Provides set-points for comparison.

2. **Sensor Block:** Captures real-time data about the joint and arc:
   - Arc voltage/current sensors (for TTAS)
   - Laser triangulation sensors (for optical tracking)
   - Vision cameras (for pool monitoring)
   - Temperature sensors (for distortion estimation)
   Outputs: lateral offset $e_x$, vertical offset $e_z$, gap width $g$, pool area $A_{pool}$.

3. **Comparator Block:** Computes error between reference and measured:
   $$e(t) = x_{ref}(t) - x_{measured}(t)$$

4. **Controller Block:** Processes the error using PID, fuzzy logic, or ANN:
   $$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

5. **Actuator / Welding Process Block:** Receives correction signal and:
   - Moves the robot TCP laterally/vertically via servo motors
   - Adjusts power source output (voltage/current)
   - Modulates wire feed rate
   - Adjusts travel speed via external axis

**Closed-loop verification:** The welded joint becomes a new input to the sensor block, ensuring **continuous self-correction**.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the laser triangulation principle for seam tracking. Derive the depth measurement equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 marks):**

In laser triangulation, a laser diode projects a **line or point** onto the workpiece surface at an angle $\alpha$ to the sensor's optical axis. The reflected laser spot is imaged onto a position-sensitive detector (CMOS) through a receiving lens. The position of the spot on the detector depends on the **height of the surface** — this is the triangulation principle.

When the torch passes over a groove, the surface dips, the spot moves on the detector, and the displacement $\delta$ is converted into a depth (height) measurement $h$.

Refer to Section 4.3 for the block diagram.

**Derivation of Depth Equation (4 marks):**

Let:
- $S$ = distance from laser projector to receiving lens baseline (mm)
- $f$ = focal length of receiving lens (mm)
- $\alpha$ = angle between projected laser beam and receiving lens axis
- $\delta$ = spot displacement on detector (mm)
- $h$ = height change of surface (mm)

By similar triangles in the optical setup, the relationship between $\delta$ and $h$ is:

$$\delta = \frac{f \cdot S \cdot \sin\alpha}{S \cdot \cos\alpha - h \cdot \sin\alpha} \cdot \frac{h}{S}$$

For small $h$ compared to $S$, the denominator simplifies:

$$\delta \approx \frac{f \cdot h \cdot \sin\alpha}{S \cdot \cos\alpha}$$

Solving for $h$:

$$\boxed{h = \frac{\delta \cdot S \cdot \cos\alpha}{f \cdot \sin\alpha}}$$

**Numerical Example:**

Let $f = 25$ mm, $S = 100$ mm, $\alpha = 30°$, $\delta = 0.5$ mm:

$$h = \frac{0.5 \times 100 \times \cos 30°}{25 \times \sin 30°} = \frac{50 \times 0.866}{12.5} = 3.46 \text{ mm}$$

This depth corresponds to the groove depth at the seam.

By scanning the laser stripe across the joint at each sampling instant, the **2D cross-sectional profile** of the joint is reconstructed, from which seam centre, gap width, and groove angle are extracted for adaptive correction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TTAS with AVC:** Through-the-Arc Sensing is for *seam tracking* (lateral); Automatic Voltage Control (AVC) is for *arc length control* (vertical). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for mixing these up.
> 2. **Forgetting units in derivations:** Always carry units in intermediate steps (mm, A, V). A derivation without units loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows:** Diagrams without clear signal-flow arrows (with arrowheads) lose at least 1 mark in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 4. **Stating "sensor" without specifying type:** Always say *which* sensor (e.g., "laser triangulation sensor" not just "sensor").
> 5. **Forgetting the closed-loop nature:** Adaptive welding is fundamentally **closed-loop**. Any answer implying open-loop control is conceptually wrong and will lose 2+ marks.
> 6. **Miscounting sub-question marks:** A 7-mark question typically expects **3–4 key points** with **equations or diagrams**. Don't write 2 lines for a 7-mark question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these for guaranteed marks:**

- **Definition:** Adaptive welding = real-time closed-loop modification of welding parameters based on sensor feedback.
- **Core Sensors:**
  - **TTAS** → uses arc current for lateral tracking (cheapest, most common in GMAW)
  - **Laser Triangulation** → external optical sensor for high accuracy (±0.05 mm)
  - **Vision** → CCD/CMOS camera for 2D joint profile
  - **Tactile** → mechanical probe for pre-weld seam finding
- **Key Equation:** $I = \dfrac{V_s - V_0 - E\ell}{R_c}$ — arc current as a function of arc length.
- **Sensitivity:** $dI/d\ell = -E/R_c \approx -75$ A/mm for typical GMAW.
- **PID Law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
- **Nyquist Criterion:** Sampling rate $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ of joint variation.
- **Closed-Loop vs Open-Loop:** Adaptive welding is **closed-loop**; teach-and-playback is open-loop.
- **Industrial Applications:** Ship hulls, automotive BIW, pressure vessels, railway bogies, offshore platforms.
- **Weaving:** Sinusoidal torch oscillation $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_w t)$ is the enabling motion for TTAS.
- **Error Signal:** $e_x = x_{torch} - x_{seam\,centre}$ drives the controller.
- **Hybrid Systems:** Modern production lines combine TTAS (coarse) + Laser (fine) for optimal performance.
- **Controller Types:** PID (most common) → Fuzzy (better robustness) → ANN (highest performance) → MPC (cutting edge).
- **Kalman Filter:** Used for sensor fusion and noise rejection in advanced adaptive systems.
- **Heat Input Formula:** $H = \eta V I / v$ — must be controlled to prevent distortion that adaptive systems compensate for.
- **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The 2024 scheme stresses **block diagrams**, **PID control laws**, and **comparison tables** — practice these formats.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Pass Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multi-Pass Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multi-Pass Welding** is a welding technique in which two or more successive weld beads (passes) are deposited along the joint line to completely fill the joint preparation, achieve the required mechanical properties, and ensure full penetration through the parent metal thickness. It is the standard industrial approach whenever the **plate thickness exceeds the single-pass penetration capability** of the selected welding process.

In robotic welding, multi-pass operation involves automated deposition of these beads in a pre-planned sequence using a programmable robot trajectory, synchronized wire feed, and closed-loop arc control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> Multi-pass welding is the deposition of multiple weld beads in a defined sequence to fill a groove or build up weld metal in thick sections, with each pass contributing to the final weld geometry, microstructure refinement, and mechanical performance of the joint.

## 1.2 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Pass / Bead** | A single run of deposited weld metal along the joint |
| **Layer** | A group of one or more passes deposited in the same horizontal plane |
| **Root Pass** | The first pass deposited at the root of the joint |
| **Fill Passes** | Intermediate passes used to fill the groove volume |
| **Cap Pass** | The final pass that completes the joint surface |
| **Interpass Temperature** | The maximum temperature of the previously deposited bead before the next pass begins |
| **Stringer Bead** | A narrow, straight bead with little or no weaving |
| **Weave Bead** | A bead produced by transverse oscillation of the torch |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

Think of multi-pass welding as **building a brick wall to fill a deep trench**:

- Imagine you have a 25 mm deep groove between two steel plates.
- One large "blob" of molten metal (single pass) cannot fill this trench without sagging, cracking, or losing penetration.
- So instead, you pour metal layer-by-layer. Each layer cools slightly, solidifies, and supports the next.
- The first layer is the **root pass** (bricks at the bottom), middle layers are **fill passes** (stacking bricks), and the top layer is the **cap pass** (the final visible surface).
- A robotic welder is like a programmed brick-layer: it knows the exact path, deposit amount, and waiting time between each layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a single big pass?**
> A single pass on thick plate causes excessive heat input, leading to wide HAZ, distortion, coarse grain, and solidification cracking. Multi-pass welding distributes heat input over time and allows grain refinement between layers.

## 1.4 When is Multi-Pass Welding Required?

Multi-pass welding becomes **mandatory** when:

1. **Plate thickness** > 6 mm in GMAW (MIG/MAG) and > 4 mm in GTAW (TIG).
2. The **groove volume** exceeds the maximum single-pass deposit volume.
3. Required **mechanical properties** (toughness, strength) demand refined microstructure.
4. **Heat input limitations** for the parent material (e.g., low-alloy steels, stainless steels) restrict single-pass energy.
5. Standards such as **ASME Section IX, AWS D1.1, or EN ISO 15614** specify multi-layer deposition for thick joints.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Benchmark Rule of Thumb:**
> For GMAW, single-pass is typically feasible up to **6–8 mm** in butt joints. Beyond this, multi-pass is the industry default. For heavy fabrication (pressure vessels, ship hulls, bridges), plates of **20–100 mm** are routinely multi-pass welded.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Multi-Pass Welding Works — The Engineering Logic

The success of multi-pass welding rests on **three synchronized engineering principles**:

### Principle 1: Distributed Heat Input
Each pass deposits a controlled amount of heat. By spreading the total heat over time and location, the cumulative **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** remains narrower and more predictable.

### Principle 2: Grain Refinement via Thermal Cycling
Each subsequent pass acts as a **localized heat treatment** on the previous pass. The previously deposited bead experiences a tempering/re-austenitizing cycle, which:
- Refines coarse grains
- Relieves residual stress partially
- Improves toughness in the reheated zone

### Principle 3: Geometric Build-Up
The cross-sectional area of the weld groove is filled incrementally, allowing the **weld pool to remain stable** (no sagging, no lack of fusion) at each stage.

## 2.2 Pass Sequencing Strategies

A robotic multi-pass weld must follow a **deliberate pass sequence**. The three most common patterns are:

| Strategy | Description | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cascade (Sequential)** | Passes deposited one after another, each starting where the previous ended | Long straight joints, thin plates |
| **Skip Welding** | Deposits alternating segments (e.g., 1→3→5, then 2→4→6) to balance shrinkage | High restraint joints, thick plates |
| **Block (Symmetric)** | Symmetric deposition from both sides of the joint to minimize distortion | Plate girders, large structures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> In robotic multi-pass welding, the **pass sequence is part of the offline program**. A WPS (Welding Procedure Specification) dictates the sequence, and the robot must execute it with high repeatability using predefined waypoints for each bead.

## 2.3 Bead Geometry — Weave vs Stringer

| Parameter | Stringer Bead | Weave Bead |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Torch Motion | Linear only | Linear + transverse oscillation |
| Bead Width | Narrow (≈ electrode diameter) | Wide (controlled by amplitude) |
| Heat Input | Lower per unit length | Higher due to dwell time |
| Penetration | Deeper | Shallower (wider pool) |
| Use Case | Root pass, deep grooves | Cap pass, wide grooves |

## 2.4 Interpass Temperature Control

The **interpass temperature** ($T_{ip}$) is the temperature of the weldment when the next pass begins. It is a **critical KTU parameter** because:

- Low $T_{ip}$ → harder, less ductile microstructure, risk of hydrogen cracking
- High $T_{ip}$ → coarse grains, reduced toughness, excessive distortion

**Typical industrial limits:**
- Carbon steel: $T_{ip} \leq 250^{\circ}C$
- Low-alloy steel: $T_{ip} \leq 200^{\circ}C$
- Stainless steel: $T_{ip} \leq 150^{\circ}C$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles all essential formulas for multi-pass welding problems asked in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbols & Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Heat Input (per unit length)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | $H$ = kJ/mm; $V$ = Voltage (V); $I$ = Current (A); $S$ = Travel speed (mm/min); $\eta$ = Arc efficiency (0.6–0.85 for GMAW, 0.4–0.6 for GTAW) |
| 2 | **Total Heat Input (Multi-Pass)** | $H_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i$ | Sum of heat inputs from $n$ passes |
| 3 | **Average Heat Input** | $H_{avg} = \dfrac{H_{total}}{n}$ | $n$ = number of passes |
| 4 | **Weld Cross-Section Area (V-groove approx.)** | $A_w = \dfrac{t^2}{2} \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) + b \cdot t$ | $t$ = plate thickness (mm); $\theta$ = groove angle; $b$ = root gap (mm) |
| 5 | **Weld Volume per Pass (Bead)** | $V_b = W_b \cdot D_b \cdot L$ | $W_b$ = bead width; $D_b$ = bead depth (reinforcement included); $L$ = weld length |
| 6 | **Number of Passes (Estimate)** | $n = \dfrac{A_w \cdot L}{V_b} = \dfrac{A_w}{A_b}$ | $A_b$ = cross-sectional area of one bead |
| 7 | **Dilution Ratio** | $D = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100\%$ | $A_{base}$ = melted base metal area; $A_{filler}$ = deposited filler area |
| 8 | **Deposition Rate (GMAW)** | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_w^2 \cdot v_f \cdot \rho}{4}$ | $d_w$ = wire diameter; $v_f$ = wire feed speed; $\rho$ = density |
| 9 | **Travel Speed from Wire Feed** | $S = \dfrac{R_{dep}}{A_b} \cdot 60$ | mm/min |
| 10 | **Heat Input Constraint Check** | $H \leq H_{max}$ (per WPS) | $H_{max}$ specified by procedure (e.g., 1.5 kJ/mm for low-alloy) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multi-pass welding is the **backbone of heavy fabrication industries**:

- **Pressure Vessels:** LPG spheres, boiler shells (typically 20–80 mm thick) demand 10–40 passes per joint.
- **Shipbuilding:** Hull plates (10–40 mm) require multi-pass butt welds with skip sequences.
- **Bridges & Offshore:** Heavy structural members use block sequence for distortion control.
- **Nuclear Piping:** Each pass is documented, and the sequence is mandatory for ASME Section III compliance.
- **Automotive Robots:** Although thinner gauges use single-pass spot or laser welding, robotic **multi-pass MIG/MAG** is used in chassis and sub-frame welding.

> [!TIP]
> In robotic cells, multi-pass welding is implemented using **multi-layer programs** with **touch-sensing or arc-sensing seam tracking** between passes to compensate for thermal distortion of the workpiece.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Heat Input for a Multi-Pass Weld

### Problem Context
A robotic GMAW cell performs a 3-pass weld on a 15 mm thick low-alloy steel plate. Each pass uses the same parameters:
- Voltage $V = 28$ V
- Current $I = 280$ A
- Travel speed $S = 350$ mm/min
- Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.80$ (GMAW typical)

Compute: (a) Heat input per pass, (b) Total heat input for 3 passes, (c) Average heat input.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the governing formula.**

The standard heat input per unit length for arc welding is:

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} \right]
$$

The factor 60 converts travel speed from mm/min to mm/s, and 1000 converts Joules to kilojoules.

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
H = \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 280 \times 60}{350 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator step by step.**

$$
0.80 \times 28 = 22.4
$$
$$
22.4 \times 280 = 6272
$$
$$
6272 \times 60 = 376320
$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator.**

$$
350 \times 1000 = 350000
$$

**Step 5: Divide to obtain $H$.**

$$
H = \frac{376320}{350000} = 1.0752 \quad \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}
$$

$$
\boxed{H \approx 1.075 \; \text{kJ/mm per pass}}
$$

**Step 6: Compute total heat input for 3 passes.**

$$
H_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} H_i = 3 \times 1.0752 = 3.2256
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{total} \approx 3.226 \; \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

**Step 7: Compute average heat input.**

$$
H_{avg} = \frac{H_{total}}{n} = \frac{3.2256}{3} = 1.0752
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{avg} \approx 1.075 \; \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that when all passes use identical parameters, the average heat input equals the per-pass heat input. This is a common KTU short-answer trap — students often confuse $H_{total}$ with $H_{avg}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Estimating the Number of Passes

### Problem Context
A 20 mm thick C-Mn steel plate is welded using GMAW with a V-groove joint preparation:
- Groove angle $\theta = 60^{\circ}$
- Root gap $b = 2$ mm
- Bead cross-section (assumed triangular): width $W_b = 10$ mm, depth $D_b = 4$ mm
- Weld length $L = 1000$ mm

Compute the **number of passes** required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate weld groove cross-section area $A_w$.**

For a V-groove with included angle $\theta$, plate thickness $t$, and root gap $b$:

$$
A_w = \frac{t^2}{2} \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + b \cdot t
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
A_w = \frac{20^2}{2} \cdot \tan\left(\frac{60^{\circ}}{2}\right) + 2 \times 20
$$
$$
A_w = \frac{400}{2} \cdot \tan(30^{\circ}) + 40
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the tangent.**

$$
\tan(30^{\circ}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774
$$

**Step 4: Continue the calculation.**

$$
A_w = 200 \times 0.5774 + 40 = 115.47 + 40 = 155.47 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 5: Calculate cross-sectional area of one bead $A_b$.**

Assuming a triangular bead profile:

$$
A_b = \frac{W_b \cdot D_b}{2} = \frac{10 \times 4}{2} = 20 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 6: Calculate the number of passes.**

$$
n = \frac{A_w}{A_b} = \frac{155.47}{20} = 7.77
$$

**Step 7: Round up to the next whole number (engineering rule).**

$$
\boxed{n = 8 \; \text{passes}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always **round up** when calculating the number of passes. Rounding down would leave groove volume unfilled, causing lack of fusion defects. KTU examiners explicitly mark rounding direction as a 1-mark allocation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Multi-Pass Distortion Control via Symmetric Block Sequence

### Problem Context
A 25 mm thick plate undergoes a 2-layer, 4-pass per layer weld. Layer 1 causes angular distortion $\alpha_1 = 1.5^{\circ}$, Layer 2 causes $\alpha_2 = 1.2^{\circ}$ in the same rotational sense. If a symmetric block sequence is used (alternating sides), the angular distortion from each pass partially cancels.

Compute the **net angular distortion** if each of the 8 passes contributes equally, and the symmetric sequence reduces net distortion by **60%**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Sum the gross distortion from 8 passes (assuming equal contribution).**

$$
\alpha_{gross} = 8 \times \alpha_{pass}
$$

But here, the per-pass contribution is implied through the layer totals. The simpler form:

$$
\alpha_{gross} = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1.5^{\circ} + 1.2^{\circ} = 2.7^{\circ}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the symmetric sequence reduction factor.**

$$
\alpha_{net} = \alpha_{gross} \times (1 - 0.60) = 2.7 \times 0.40
$$

**Step 3: Final result.**

$$
\boxed{\alpha_{net} = 1.08^{\circ}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat input limit compliance | $V, I, S, \eta, H_{max}$ | Confirm $H \leq H_{max}$ | $H = \eta V I \cdot 60 / (S \cdot 1000)$ |
| Pass count estimation | $t, \theta, b, W_b, D_b$ | $n$ | $n = A_w / A_b$ (rounded up) |
| Total energy input | $H$ per pass, $n$ | $H_{total}$ | $H_{total} = n \cdot H$ |
| Productivity check | $L, n, S$ | Total welding time | $T = (n \cdot L) / S$ |
| Dilution control | $A_{base}, A_{filler}$ | $D$ % | $D = A_{base} / (A_{base} + A_{filler})$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multi-Pass Weld Cross-Section (Schematic Representation)

The following Mermaid block represents the **vertical cross-section** of a multi-pass V-groove butt weld, showing how beads stack layer-by-layer to fill the groove.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Plate Top Surface] --> B[Cap Pass Layer 3]
    B --> C[Fill Pass Layer 2 Pass 2]
    C --> D[Fill Pass Layer 2 Pass 1]
    D --> E[Fill Pass Layer 1 Pass 2]
    E --> F[Fill Pass Layer 1 Pass 1]
    F --> G[Root Pass Layer 0]
    G --> H[Root Gap 2mm]
    H --> I[Plate Bottom Surface]
    B --> J[Reinforcement 1 to 3 mm]
    A --> K[Parent Metal Left]
    A --> L[Parent Metal Right]
    K --> M[HAZ Left]
    L --> N[HAZ Right]
    M --> F
    N --> F
```

**Interpretation for Students:**
- The **cap pass** (Layer 3) sits at the top with a slight reinforcement above the plate surface.
- **Fill passes** occupy the middle volume.
- The **root pass** (Layer 0) is at the bottom, critical for penetration.
- **HAZ** (Heat Affected Zone) flanks both sides in the parent metal.

## 4.2 Robotic Multi-Pass Welding Process Flow

This block diagram maps the **complete functional flow** of a robotic multi-pass welding cell, from joint sensing to final inspection.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Joint Preparation and Cleaning] --> P2[Fixture Clamping]
    P2 --> P3[Initial Seam Tracking by Touch Sensor]
    P3 --> P4[Root Pass Weld Layer 0]
    P4 --> P5[Interpass Temperature Monitoring by Pyrometer]
    P5 --> P6{Tip Below Limit}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Wait for Setpoint then Proceed]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Cooling Dwell Time]
    P8 --> P6
    P7 --> P9[Fill Pass Layer 1]
    P9 --> P10[Arc Sensing for Cross Joint Tracking]
    P10 --> P11[Fill Pass Layer 2]
    P11 --> P12[Cap Pass Layer 3]
    P12 --> P13[Robot Retracts Torch]
    P13 --> P14[Visual and NDT Inspection]
    P14 --> P15{Pass All Checks}
    P15 -->|Yes| P16[Unclamp and Eject Part]
    P15 -->|No| P17[Mark for Rework]
    P17 --> P18[Manual Repair Weld]
    P18 --> P14
```

## 4.3 Pass Sequencing Strategies — Comparative Block View

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Cascade Sequential] --> S1A[Pass 1 Start to End]
    S1A --> S1B[Pass 2 Start to End]
    S1B --> S1C[Pass 3 Start to End]
    S2[Skip Welding Alternating] --> S2A[Segment 1A]
    S2A --> S2B[Segment 2A]
    S2B --> S2C[Segment 1B]
    S2C --> S2D[Segment 2B]
    S3[Block Symmetric Both Sides] --> S3A[Left Side Pass 1]
    S3A --> S3B[Right Side Pass 1]
    S3B --> S3C[Left Side Pass 2]
    S3C --> S3D[Right Side Pass 2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Tip:**
> The node IDs above (`S1`, `S1A`, `P1`, etc.) are all alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid conflicts with reserved Mermaid keywords like `end` and `subgraph`. Labels are kept clean (no `**`, `*`, or HTML tables) for guaranteed rendering.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Robotic Multi-Pass Controller

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[Offline Programming Software] --> C2[Weld Procedure Specification WPS Database]
    C2 --> C3[Pass Sequence and Trajectory Generator]
    C3 --> C4[Robot Controller]
    C4 --> C5[Servo Drives for 6 Axes]
    C5 --> C6[Robot Manipulator]
    C4 --> C7[Power Source Interface]
    C7 --> C8[GMAW Power Source]
    C8 --> C9[Wire Feeder]
    C8 --> C10[Shielding Gas Unit]
    C4 --> C11[Sensor Hub]
    C11 --> C12[Arc Sensor]
    C11 --> C13[Touch Sensor]
    C11 --> C14[Pyrometer for Interpass Temp]
    C11 --> C15[Vision Camera for Cap Pass]
    C6 --> C16[Workpiece in Fixture]
    C8 --> C16
    C14 --> C4
    C12 --> C4
    C13 --> C4
    C15 --> C4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **offline programming software** generates the pass sequence and waypoints.
- The **robot controller** synchronizes motion, power source, and wire feed.
- The **sensor hub** closes the loop for interpass temperature, arc stability, and seam tracking.
- The **workpiece in fixture** is the physical target of all control actions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: Define multi-pass welding. List any two situations where multi-pass welding becomes essential.**

**Model Answer:**

Multi-pass welding is a technique in which **two or more successive weld beads are deposited** in a defined sequence along the joint line to completely fill the joint groove and achieve the required mechanical properties. It is essential in the following situations:

1. **Thick plate welding:** When the plate thickness exceeds the single-pass penetration capability of the process (e.g., > 6 mm for GMAW).
2. **Heat input constraints:** When the parent material (e.g., low-alloy steel, stainless steel) demands heat input below the single-pass threshold for thick sections.
3. **Groove volume exceedance:** When the calculated groove cross-section area is greater than what a single bead can safely deposit.
4. **Distortion control:** When the structure requires symmetric or skip sequences to balance shrinkage stress.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, **definition (1 mark) + two reasons (1 mark each)** is the standard KTU breakdown. Always use the term *"deposited beads"* rather than vague phrasing.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q: What is interpass temperature? Why is it controlled in multi-pass robotic welding?**

**Model Answer:**

**Interpass temperature** is defined as **the highest temperature of the previously deposited weld bead at the moment the next pass is started**.

It is controlled in robotic multi-pass welding for the following reasons:

1. **Microstructure control:** Excessive interpass temperature promotes coarse grain growth, reducing toughness in the reheated zone.
2. **Hydrogen cracking prevention:** Low interpass temperatures trap diffusible hydrogen, leading to underbead or toe cracking in hardenable steels.
3. **Distortion management:** Maintaining a low and consistent $T_{ip}$ minimizes cumulative angular distortion in multi-pass welds.
4. **WPS compliance:** Standards like ASME IX and AWS D1.1 specify strict $T_{ip}$ limits (e.g., 250°C for C-Mn steel).

A typical KTU expected numerical: $T_{ip} \leq 250^{\circ}C$ for carbon steel.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B for Module 2 carries 14 marks with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below. Each is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** as per the standard pattern.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model QP]`

**Q: With neat sketches, explain the concept of multi-pass welding. Discuss the various pass sequencing strategies used in robotic multi-pass welding, comparing their advantages and limitations.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Concept and Need for Multi-Pass Welding

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Multi-pass welding is the sequential deposition of two or more weld beads along a joint to fill the groove volume completely. Each bead, called a *pass*, is laid on top of or beside the previous one.

**Need (3 marks):**
- Single-pass penetration is limited (≈ 6 mm for GMAW in butt joints).
- Thick plates (10–100 mm) demand multi-pass for groove fill.
- Heat input restriction on alloyed materials requires heat to be spread across passes.
- Grain refinement between passes improves toughness.
- Allows use of smaller, more stable weld pools, reducing defect risk.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Show a V-groove cross-section with 3–4 layers, label root pass, fill passes, cap pass, and HAZ.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Pass Sequencing Strategies

**Model Answer:**

| Strategy | Procedure | Advantages | Limitations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cascade (Sequential)** | Each pass starts where the previous ended | Simple program, easy torch path | High cumulative shrinkage on long welds |
| **Skip Welding** | Alternating segments (1→3→5, then 2→4→6) | Balances shrinkage, reduces distortion | Complex robot program, more starts/stops |
| **Block (Symmetric)** | Alternate left and right sides of joint | Minimum angular distortion, ideal for thick plates | Requires double-sided access or repositioning |

**Robotic implementation note (1 mark):** In modern robotic cells, the sequence is part of the **WPS file** uploaded to the controller. The robot executes the waypoints with arc-sensing feedback between passes to correct for thermal drift.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: A 3-pass robotic GMAW weld is performed on a 15 mm thick low-alloy steel plate. The welding parameters are: $V = 28$ V, $I = 280$ A, travel speed $S = 350$ mm/min, and arc efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. The V-groove has an included angle of 60° and a root gap of 2 mm. Each bead is assumed to have a triangular cross-section of width 10 mm and depth 4 mm.**

**(a) Calculate the heat input per pass, the total heat input for the weld, and the average heat input. Comment on whether the heat input is suitable for low-alloy steel.**

**(b) Estimate the number of passes required to fill the groove. Justify the rounding direction.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Heat Input Calculations

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Heat input per pass.**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} = \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 280 \times 60}{350 \times 1000}
$$

$$
H = \frac{376320}{350000} = 1.075 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2: Total heat input for 3 passes.**

$$
H_{total} = 3 \times 1.075 = 3.225 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 3: Average heat input.**

$$
H_{avg} = \frac{3.225}{3} = 1.075 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 4: Comment on suitability (2 marks).**

For low-alloy steel, typical WPS heat input limit is $H_{max} \leq 1.5$ kJ/mm to preserve toughness. Since $1.075 < 1.5$, **the heat input is suitable**. However, the **interpass temperature** must still be monitored (limit ≤ 200°C for low-alloy steel) to avoid grain coarsening.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Pass Count Estimation

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Weld groove cross-section area.**

$$
A_w = \frac{t^2}{2} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + b \cdot t = \frac{15^2}{2} \tan(30^{\circ}) + 2 \times 15
$$
$$
A_w = 112.5 \times 0.5774 + 30 = 64.96 + 30 = 94.96 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Bead cross-section area (triangular).**

$$
A_b = \frac{W_b \cdot D_b}{2} = \frac{10 \times 4}{2} = 20 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Number of passes.**

$$
n = \frac{A_w}{A_b} = \frac{94.96}{20} = 4.748
$$

**Step 4: Rounding justification.**

Since $n = 4.748$, we **round up to 5 passes**. A fractional pass would leave groove volume unfilled, leading to **lack of fusion** defects. KTU accepts "round up" as the only correct engineering choice.

**Step 5: Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{n = 5 \; \text{passes required}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the arc efficiency $\eta$:** Many students write $H = V \cdot I \cdot 60 / (S \cdot 1000)$ without $\eta$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in most valuations.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion:** Mixing mm/min with mm/s, or Joules with kJ, costs **1 mark** minimum.
> 3. **Rounding down the pass count:** Always round **up**; rounding down leaves groove volume unfilled (lack of fusion).
> 4. **Confusing $H_{total}$ and $H_{avg}$:** Total heat input is the sum; average is total / n. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **Using the term "multi-layer" interchangeably with "multi-pass":** A *layer* may contain multiple *passes*. Mixing terminology loses **1 mark** in definition questions.
> 6. **Ignoring interpass temperature in long answers:** Any multi-pass question worth 7+ marks expects interpass $T_{ip}$ discussion. Omission costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Multi-pass welding** = sequential deposition of multiple beads to fill a joint groove, used when single-pass is insufficient.
- Required for **plate thickness > 6 mm** in GMAW; mandatory in heavy fabrication.
- **Three main pass sequences:** Cascade, Skip, Block (symmetric).
- **Heat input formula:** $H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60 / (S \cdot 1000)$ in kJ/mm.
- **Total heat input** for $n$ passes: $H_{total} = \sum H_i$; **Average** = $H_{total}/n$.
- **Weld groove area** (V-groove): $A_w = (t^2/2) \tan(\theta/2) + b \cdot t$.
- **Number of passes** = $A_w / A_b$, **rounded up**.
- **Bead area** (triangular approximation): $A_b = (W_b \cdot D_b) / 2$.
- **Interpass temperature** $T_{ip}$ must be controlled (≤ 250°C C-steel, ≤ 200°C low-alloy, ≤ 150°C stainless).
- **Dilution** $D = A_{base} / (A_{base} + A_{filler}) \times 100\%$.
- **Weave beads** are wider with shallower penetration; **stringer beads** are narrow with deeper penetration.
- **Robotic multi-pass** uses **offline-programmed waypoints, arc sensing, touch sensing, and pyrometers** for interpass control.
- **Round up** when calculating $n$; round down only for time/cost estimates.
- **Always include $\eta$** in heat input calculations; **always specify units** (kJ/mm).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding of Advanced Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT723 - ROBOTIC WELDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welding of Advanced Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Welding of Advanced Materials** refers to the specialized joining processes and robotic automation strategies employed to fuse metallic, intermetallic, and hybrid engineering materials that exhibit non-conventional metallurgical, thermal, or mechanical behavior during fusion and solidification. In the context of **Robotic Welding Technology (OERUT723)**, this encompasses the integration of **sensor-guided manipulators**, **adaptive control algorithms**, and **process-specific end-effectors** to join materials whose weldability cannot be addressed by conventional carbon-steel arc welding paradigms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> Advanced materials in robotic welding are defined as those engineering alloys and composites that require **modified thermal cycles**, **inert or reactive shielding atmospheres**, **trajectory compensation**, and **real-time seam tracking** due to their high thermal conductivity, low melting range, high affinity for interstitial elements, or sensitivity to heat-affected zone (HAZ) softening.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Advanced Materials Relevant to Robotic Welding

| Category | Representative Materials | Primary Robotic Welding Challenge |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Lightweight Non-Ferrous | Al, Mg, Ti alloys | High thermal conductivity, oxide layer |
| Corrosion-Resistant | Austenitic/Duplex SS | Sensitization, hot cracking |
| High-Temperature | Ni-superalloys, Co-alloys | Strain-age cracking |
| Advanced Steels | AHSS, UHSS, TRIP | HAZ softening, martensite formation |
| Dissimilar Couples | Al-Steel, Ti-SS | Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) mismatch |
| Non-Metallics | CFRP, GFRP composites | No fusion — ultrasonic/friction based |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Ice-Cube vs. Chocolate vs. Glass" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine trying to melt and re-solidify three different objects using only a single torch:
> 1. **An ice cube (Aluminum)** — Heat escapes sideways faster than you can pour "weld metal" in. You need a *cold* process or a *very fast* robot.
> 2. **A chocolate bar (Stainless Steel / Ni-alloy)** — Easy to melt, but if you heat it too long, sugar crystals (chromium carbides) form at the grain boundaries and make it brittle.
> 3. **A piece of glass (Titanium)** — Above 400 °C, it becomes chemically "greedy" and absorbs oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen from the air, turning brittle. You must weld it inside an *invisible glove* of argon.
>
> **A robot welder is the surgeon that knows exactly how fast to move, how much heat to give, and how much shielding gas to deploy — for each "material personality."**

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical and metallurgical constants govern robotic welding of advanced materials:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Thermal Conductivity (k) of Pure Al ≈ 237 W/m·K** vs. **Mild Steel ≈ 50 W/m·K** (≈ **4.7× higher**)
> - **Melting Point of Ti ≈ 1668 °C** (≈ **900 °C higher** than Al)
> - **Density of Mg ≈ 1.74 g/cm³** (lightest structural metal — **~35% lighter than Al**)
> - **Interpass Temperature Limit for AHSS ≤ 150 °C** (strict robotic thermal management)
> - **Argon Purity for Ti Welding ≥ 99.999% (5N grade)**
> - **Oxygen Threshold for Ti Weld Pool ≤ 200 ppm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Metallurgical Theory — The "Why" Behind the Process

### 2.1.1 Heat Input — The Master Variable in Robotic Welding

Every advanced material responds to a different magnitude of **Heat Input (HI)**, which is the energy delivered per unit length of weld. For a robot, this is a *programmable* variable, set by welding current ($I$), arc voltage ($V)$, and travel speed ($S$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Heat Input Matters:**
> - **Too High HI** → Excessive grain growth in HAZ, sensitization in stainless steel, burn-through in thin Al sheets.
> - **Too Low HI** → Lack of fusion, porosity (especially in Al from trapped hydrogen), cold lap.
> - The robotic controller maintains HI within a **process window** unique to each material.

### 2.1.2 The Weldability Triangle for Advanced Materials

Robotic welding of advanced materials is governed by a three-way trade-off:

```
              [MATERIAL Response]
                     ▲
                    /│\
                   / │ \
                  /  │  \
                 /   │   \
                /    │    \
               /     │     \
              /      │      \
   [PROCESS] ●───────●───────● [ROBOTICS / CONTROL]
   Selection         Heat       Trajectory,
   (GTAW, GMAW,    Input &     Speed, Sealing
   PAW, LBW)       Shielding
```

> **Material Response** = Microstructure, HAZ softening, cracking tendency.
> **Process Selection** = Arc-based, beam-based, or hybrid.
> **Robotics/Control** = Path planning, adaptive sensing, gas management.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential** for Module 2 of OERUT723. The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | Heat Input (arc efficiency $\eta$) | kJ/mm | Master variable for all robotic welds |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}$ | Heat input factor (for power-density comparison) | J/mm | Comparing travel-speed settings |
| 3 | $t_{8/5} = f(HI, T_{pre}, T_{interpass})$ | Cooling time between 800–500 °C | seconds | Governs HAZ microstructure of HSLA/AHSS |
| 4 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L_0$ | Thermal expansion of joint | mm | Distortion compensation in robot path |
| 5 | $\sigma_{CTE} = E \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T$ | CTE-mismatch residual stress | MPa | Dissimilar metal welding |
| 6 | $G = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{S}{V \cdot I}$ | Heat input gradient (inverse) | mm/J | Robotic speed optimization |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number of shielding gas | dimensionless | Gas nozzle / trailing shield design |
| 8 | $Q_{gas} = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Shielding gas flow (Hagen–Poiseuille) | L/min | Trailing-shield for Ti/Mg |
| 9 | $\eta_{arc}$: GMAW ≈ 0.80, GTAW ≈ 0.65, PAW ≈ 0.50, LBW ≈ 0.40 | Arc/Process thermal efficiency | dimensionless | Required for HI formula |
| 10 | $P_{laser} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot I_{dens}$ | Laser power from spot & intensity | W | Robotic laser hybrid welding |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> 1. **Aerospace (Ti, Ni-alloys)** — Robotic plasma + laser hybrid welding of turbine casings; **trailing shields** preserve the Ti weld bead.
> 2. **Automotive (AHSS, Al)** — Robotic GMAW with **adaptive pulse** joins DP600/DP980 body-in-white; **servo-driven torch** follows 3D seams at 1.2 m/min.
> 3. **Shipbuilding (Duplex SS)** — Robotic GMAW with **super-pulse control** maintains the critical 40–50% ferrite–austenite balance.
> 4. **Battery packs (Al-Cu)** — Robotic ultrasonic + friction stir welding replaces traditional soldering to handle **CTE mismatch** and current density.

The robot's **real value** is **repeatability** of these delicate thermal cycles across thousands of joints.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input (HI) From Fundamental Energy Balance

### 3.1.1 Statement

For a robotic arc welding process, derive the **Heat Input per unit length** in kJ/mm from the basic energy delivered by the welding power source.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Electrical Power Delivered to the Arc**
The power source supplies electrical power equal to the product of arc voltage and welding current.

$$
P_{elec} = V \cdot I
$$

Where:
- $V$ = arc voltage in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = welding current in **Amperes (A)**
- $P_{elec}$ = electrical power in **Watts (W) = J/s**

**Step 2 — Apply Arc Thermal Efficiency**
Not all electrical power reaches the workpiece — a fraction is lost as spatter, radiation, and fumes. This is captured by the arc efficiency factor $\eta$.

$$
P_{workpiece} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

Where $\eta$ is a dimensionless factor (e.g., **0.65 for GTAW**, **0.80 for GMAW**).

**Step 3 — Energy per Unit Time is Power**
By definition, energy equals power multiplied by time:

$$
E = P_{workpiece} \cdot t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Relate Time to Travel Distance**
The robot moves the torch at a constant travel speed $S$ along the seam. For a weld length $L$:

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{S}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify**
Substitute the time expression into the energy equation:

$$
E = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L}{S}
$$

**Step 6 — Energy per Unit Length (Heat Input)**

$$
\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert Units to kJ/mm**
Since $V \cdot I$ is in **J/mm** when $S$ is in **mm/s**, dividing by 1000 converts to **kJ/mm**:

$$
\boxed{HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; [\text{kJ/mm}]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight (KTU Exam Style):**
> For robotic GMAW of AA6061-T6, a typical setting is $V = 22$ V, $I = 180$ A, $S = 8$ mm/s, $\eta = 0.80$:
>
> $$
> HI = \dfrac{0.80 \times 22 \times 180}{8 \times 1000} = 0.396 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} = 396 \;\; \text{J/mm}
> $$
>
> This lies **below the 0.6 kJ/mm threshold** for AA6061, ensuring **no HAZ softening** — a critical aerospace requirement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cooling Time $t_{8/5}$ for HSLA / AHSS Steels

### 3.2.1 Statement

The cooling time between **800 °C and 500 °C** (denoted $t_{8/5}$) is the metallurgical clock that determines HAZ hardness. Derive a simplified empirical form for robotic arc welding.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Two-Dimensional Heat Flow Assumption**
For a thick plate and a moving arc (robot travel), heat conduction occurs primarily in 2D (plate thickness ≫ weld width).

**Step 2 — Rosenthal's Thick-Plate Solution (Simplified)**
For a moving point source on a thick plate:

$$
t_{8/5} = \dfrac{(E/(d))^2}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1 - T_0} - \dfrac{1}{T_2 - T_0} \right)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Material-Specific Constants**
For carbon and HSLA steels:
- $k \approx 0.029$ W/(mm·K)
- $\rho \cdot c \approx 0.0044$ J/(mm³·K)
- $T_1 = 800$ °C, $T_2 = 500$ °C, $T_0 = 25$ °C (preheat)

**Step 4 — Empirical Inversion (Engineering Form)**
Inverting the formula and grouping all material constants into one empirical constant $C$, the KTU-friendly form becomes:

$$
t_{8/5} = C \cdot \left( \dfrac{HI}{d} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1 - T_0} - \dfrac{1}{T_2 - T_0} \right)^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Practical Form (used in CEW / EN 1011)**
For steels, the EN 1011 / IIW recommended form is:

$$
\boxed{t_{8/5} = \left( \dfrac{HI}{d} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{C_{th}}{\left(500 - T_0\right)} - \dfrac{C_{th}}{\left(800 - T_0\right)} \right) }
$$

Where:
- $d$ = plate thickness (mm)
- $C_{th}$ ≈ **0.67** for 2D heat flow in steel
- $T_0$ = preheat/interpass temperature (°C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for the robot:**
> The robot's **interpass temperature sensor + adaptive HI controller** maintains $t_{8/5}$ within the **5–12 s window** required for **DP780** to obtain a fine-grained HAZ without excessive martensite.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CTE-Mismatch Residual Stress (Dissimilar Welding)

### 3.3.1 Statement
Derive the residual stress developed at the interface of a **bi-material joint** (e.g., Al–Steel) due to a single thermal cycle from the robot.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free Thermal Strain of Each Material**

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \alpha_1 \cdot \Delta T \qquad \varepsilon_2 = \alpha_2 \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Constraint Imposed by Rigid Bonding**
If the robot solidifies a continuous weld bead, the two materials are *mechanically tied* along the interface, so they must share a common strain $\varepsilon_{common}$.

**Step 3 — Internal Force Equilibrium**
Let $A_1$, $A_2$ be the cross-sectional areas and $E_1$, $E_2$ the Young's moduli. The internal force must vanish:

$$
A_1 \cdot E_1 \cdot (\varepsilon_{common} - \varepsilon_1) + A_2 \cdot E_2 \cdot (\varepsilon_{common} - \varepsilon_2) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Common Strain**

$$
\varepsilon_{common} = \dfrac{A_1 E_1 \varepsilon_1 + A_2 E_2 \varepsilon_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting Thermal Strains**

$$
\varepsilon_{common} = \dfrac{\Delta T \left( A_1 E_1 \alpha_1 + A_2 E_2 \alpha_2 \right)}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}
$$

**Step 6 — Residual Stress in Material 1**

$$
\sigma_1 = E_1 \cdot (\varepsilon_{common} - \alpha_1 \Delta T) = \dfrac{A_2 E_1 E_2 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Symmetric Form**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{CTE} = \dfrac{E_1 E_2 \, A_2 \, (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \, \Delta T}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for Robotic Path Planning:**
> For Al–Steel lap joints, $\alpha_{Al} \approx 23 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C and $\alpha_{St} \approx 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. A cooling of $\Delta T = 500$ °C generates $\sigma_{CTE} \approx 100$–150 MPa — **exceeding the yield strength of pure Al**. This is why **robotic Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** is preferred for Al–Steel battery enclosures — it is a *solid-state* process with much smaller $\Delta T$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
> **Q1.** Define "Heat Input" in robotic arc welding. Why is it a critical process parameter when welding advanced materials like **AA7075** or **Ti-6Al-4V**?

**Model Answer (Examiner-Expected):**
Heat Input (HI) is the **energy delivered per unit length of weld** by the welding arc, expressed in **kJ/mm**:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

where $\eta$ is arc efficiency, $V$ is arc voltage, $I$ is current, and $S$ is travel speed. For **AA7075 (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu)**, excessive HI causes **burn-through** and **liquation cracking** along grain boundaries. For **Ti-6Al-4V**, high HI enlarges the HAZ, where $\alpha$-phase coarsening reduces fatigue strength. A robotic system precisely controls HI by synchronizing the **wire feed rate (WFR)** with the **TCP velocity**, ensuring consistent microstructure.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q2.** What is a **"trailing shield"**? Why is it mandatory in robotic welding of titanium?

**Model Answer:**
A **trailing shield** is an auxiliary gas-nozzle attachment fixed behind the welding torch that continues to flow **high-purity argon (≥ 99.999%)** over the *cooling* weld bead, protecting it from atmospheric contamination until the temperature drops below **≈ 400 °C** (the critical oxygen-embrittlement threshold of Ti). For robotic welding of Ti, the moving torch exits the primary nozzle too quickly for the HAZ to cool in air — leading to **titanium-oxide formation, discoloration, and embrittlement**. The trailing shield is therefore mandatory in **robotic Ti aerospace welds** (e.g., fuselage, engine pylons).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, Model Question Bank]**

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat schematic the **classification of advanced materials** relevant to robotic welding. Discuss the metallurgical challenges encountered when welding **duplex stainless steel (2205)** and **AA6061-T6 aluminum** using a robotic GMAW cell.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robotic GMAW cell is used to weld a **3 mm AA6061-T6 sheet** for an aerospace skin panel. The parameters are: $V = 22$ V, $I = 160$ A, $S = 9$ mm/s, $\eta = 0.80$. Compute (i) the **Heat Input**, (ii) the **Heat Input Gradient (G)**, and (iii) comment on whether this HI is **acceptable** for AA6061-T6 (critical HI limit ≈ 0.6 kJ/mm).

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification & Challenges (7 Marks)

**Classification (3 marks):**
1. **Lightweight Non-Ferrous** — Al, Mg, Ti alloys.
2. **Corrosion-Resistant** — Austenitic, Ferritic, Duplex SS.
3. **High-Temperature** — Ni-superalloys (Inconel), Co-alloys.
4. **Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS/UHSS/TRIP).**
5. **Dissimilar Metal Couples** — Al–Steel, Ti–SS, Cu–Al.
6. **Non-Metallic Composites** — CFRP/GFRP (joined by FSW, ultrasonic, not fusion).

**Duplex SS (2205) Challenges (2 marks):**
- Requires a **ferrite–austenite balance of 40–60%**; too high HI causes excessive ferrite, reducing toughness.
- Risk of **sigma-phase precipitation** above 950 °C, leading to embrittlement.
- Robotic control uses **super-pulse GMAW** with **real-time ferrite number (FN) sensors**.

**AA6061-T6 Challenges (2 marks):**
- High thermal conductivity (≈ 167 W/m·K) demands **higher current** and **faster travel speed**.
- The **T6 temper is lost** in the HAZ → ~30–40% strength reduction → robot pre-positions the seam to allow **post-weld artificial aging** if needed.
- Mg and Si in the alloy form **oxide film** → requires **AC-GTAW** or **mechanical cleaning + push-pull GMAW**.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 22$ V
- $I = 160$ A
- $S = 9$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.80$
- Critical HI for AA6061-T6 ≤ 0.6 kJ/mm

**Step 1 — Heat Input**

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{0.80 \times 22 \times 160}{9 \times 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{2816}{9000} = 0.313 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat Input Gradient G**

$$
G = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{S}{V \cdot I} = \dfrac{9}{22 \times 160} = \dfrac{9}{3520} = 2.557 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{mm/J}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceptability Check**

$$
HI = 0.313 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \;\; < \;\; 0.6 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{ACCEPTABLE}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The robotic GMAW setting is **safe** for AA6061-T6 — HAZ softening will be **minimal**, and the joint will retain ≥ 90% of base-metal strength. The robot's **adaptive pulse** may further reduce HI by 10–15%, giving additional safety margin.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **robot-specific challenges** of welding **titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)**. With the help of a **functional block diagram**, explain how a robotic GTAW cell addresses these challenges using a **trailing shield, oxygen analyzer, and adaptive arc-length control**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a dissimilar metal joint between **Inconel 718** ($\alpha_1 = 13 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, $E_1 = 200$ GPa) and **SS 304** ($\alpha_2 = 17.3 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, $E_2 = 193$ GPa) with equal cross-sections ($A_1 = A_2$), compute the residual stress generated after a cooling of $\Delta T = 600$ °C using the CTE-mismatch model derived in Section 3.3.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Robotic Ti Welding (7 Marks)

**Challenges (3 marks):**
1. **Atmospheric contamination** — Ti reacts with O₂, N₂, H₂ above 400 °C.
2. **High melting point (1668 °C)** — demands precise arc-length control.
3. **Low thermal conductivity (≈ 7 W/m·K)** — heat concentrates in a narrow zone, risking burn-through in thin sheets.

**Robotic Solutions (4 marks):**
- **Trailing Shield** — Maintains inert atmosphere over cooling weld.
- **Oxygen Analyzer** — Closed-loop feedback; if O₂ > 50 ppm in chamber, robot halts.
- **Adaptive Arc-Length Control** — Arc sensor measures $V$ at 10 kHz; robot adjusts TCP height to maintain constant $V$ → constant HI.

#### Part (b) — CTE-Mismatch Residual Stress (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $E_1 = 200$ GPa, $\alpha_1 = 13 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- $E_2 = 193$ GPa, $\alpha_2 = 17.3 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- $A_1 = A_2 = A$
- $\Delta T = 600$ °C

**Step 1 — Apply the Derived Formula**

$$
\sigma_{CTE} = \dfrac{E_1 E_2 \, A_2 \, (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \, \Delta T}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify for Equal Areas** $A_1 = A_2 = A$

$$
\sigma_{CTE} = \dfrac{E_1 E_2 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T}{E_1 + E_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
\sigma_{CTE} = \dfrac{(200 \times 10^9)(193 \times 10^9) (17.3 - 13) \times 10^{-6} \cdot 600}{200 \times 10^9 + 193 \times 10^9}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Numerator**

$$
\text{Num} = (200 \times 10^9)(193 \times 10^9) \cdot 4.3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 600
$$

$$
\text{Num} = 3.86 \times 10^{22} \cdot 2.58 \times 10^{-3} = 9.96 \times 10^{19} \;\; \text{(GPa·°C units)}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Denominator**

$$
\text{Den} = 3.93 \times 10^{11} \;\; \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result**

$$
\sigma_{CTE} = \dfrac{9.96 \times 10^{19}}{3.93 \times 10^{11}} = 2.53 \times 10^{8} \;\; \text{Pa} = 253 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The residual stress **253 MPa** approaches the **yield strength of Inconel 718 (≈ 1100 MPa at RT)** but exceeds the **yield strength of SS304 at 600 °C (≈ 200 MPa)**. The robot must therefore use **low HI** + **interpass cooling** + **butter-layer (Inconel filler)** to keep the SS304 side from creeping.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (KTU 2024 Evaluator Insights):**
> 1. **Missing the unit kJ/mm** in the Heat Input answer → **−1 mark** (units are mandatory).
> 2. **Forgetting the arc efficiency factor $\eta$** in GTAW/GMAW comparison → examiner deducts **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating the 400 °C oxygen threshold** in Ti welding → loses **1 mark** for incomplete answer.
> 4. **Confusing "ferrite number (FN)" with "% ferrite"** in duplex SS — they are *not* equal numerically → **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Writing $E_1, E_2$ in GPa without converting to Pa** in the CTE formula → entire numerical answer gets **0** (units error).
> 6. **Skipping the "comment on acceptability"** in any HI numerical — partial mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Welding of Advanced Materials**

- 🔑 **Master formula:** $HI = \eta V I / (S \times 1000)$ — units **kJ/mm**.
- 🔑 **Arc efficiency hierarchy:** GMAW (0.80) > GTAW (0.65) > PAW (0.50) > LBW (0.40).
- 🔑 **Aluminum robotic welding:** Use **pulsed GMAW** or **AC-GTAW**; **critical HI ≤ 0.6 kJ/mm** for AA6061-T6.
- 🔑 **Titanium robotic welding:** **Trailing shield + 5N argon + O₂ monitor + TC length control**; contamination threshold = **400 °C**.
- 🔑 **Duplex SS (2205):** FN = **30–55**; HI must be controlled to keep ferrite in 40–60% range.
- 🔑 **AHSS (DP780/980):** $t_{8/5}$ window **5–12 s**; interpass ≤ **150 °C**; prefer **laser-GMAW hybrid**.
- 🔑 **Magnesium:** Density **1.74 g/cm³**; high vapor pressure → cover with **high-flow Ar shielding**; **FSW preferred**.
- 🔑 **Ni-superalloys (Inconel):** Susceptible to **strain-age cracking** → use **low HI + fast cooling** + **Inconel filler (matching)**.
- 🔑 **Dissimilar joints:** CTE mismatch → **residual stress** $= E_1 E_2 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T / (E_1 + E_2)$ for equal areas.
- 🔑 **Al–Steel battery packs:** Prefer **FSW (solid state)** over fusion to limit $\Delta T$ and prevent brittle Fe-Al IMC.
- 🔑 **Composites (CFRP):** No fusion welding — use **FSW with metal insert** or **ultrasonic welding**; the robot's **force-control** is critical.
- 🔑 **Robotic process window:** Each material has its own **HI vs. travel speed** map stored in the controller's **weld database**.
- 🔑 **Sensors used in advanced-material robotic cells:** Arc-sensor, IR pyrometer, oxygen analyzer, ferrite sensor, laser seam tracker, ultrasonic C-scan.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Welding of Advanced Materials**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Robotic Welding** refer to the systematic deployment of **programmable, multi-axis articulated manipulators** integrated with **arc-based, resistance-based, or high-energy-density welding processes** (GMAW, GTAW, PAW, SAW, Laser, Plasma, Stud) in **continuous, high-volume, or hazardous manufacturing environments** where **repeatability, deposition efficiency, geometric accuracy, and human-safety optimization** govern the production economics.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3) classifies industrial robotic welding applications across **process-axis, sector-axis, and automation-tier** dimensions, emphasizing real-world deployment scenarios such as **automotive body-in-white (BIW) assembly, heavy structural fabrication, ship-hull panel welding, aerospace component joining, pressure vessel and pipe spool production, and railway rolling-stock manufacturing**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Tireless Master Craftsman"**
>
> Imagine a master welder with three extraordinary superpowers:
> 1. **Perfect Memory** — remembers the exact torch angle, travel speed, and current for every joint, even after 10,000 repetitions.
> 2. **Tireless Arms** — works 24×7 with no fatigue, no tremor, no lunch breaks.
> 3. **X-Ray Eyes (via Sensors)** — instantly detects arc wander, lack of fusion, or pool collapse and self-corrects.
>
> Now scale that one craftsman across **40 identical production cells**, all choreographed by a single digital "conductor" (the **PLC / Robot Controller / MES link**). This is precisely what an **industrial robotic welding cell** does in a modern factory. The "recipe" is the **weld program (TP / OLP)**, the "kitchen" is the **work-cell fixture**, and the "customer" is the **production order from the ERP system**.

## 1.3 Defining Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial KPIs)

The following **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** are the universally accepted yardsticks in KTU-endorsed industrial evaluation of robotic welding:

| KPI | Symbol / Unit | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Repeatability** | **±0.05 mm to ±0.10 mm** | Premium automotive tier |
| **Deposition Rate** | **kg/hr or g/min** | **5–15 kg/hr** for GMAW robotic |
| **Arc-On Time (% Duty Cycle)** | **%** | **70–95 %** in optimized cells |
| **Productivity Gain vs Manual** | **×** | **3× to 5×** typical, up to **10×** in BIW |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | **MTBF (hrs)** | **40,000 – 80,000 hrs** |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | **OEE (%)** | World-class ≥ **85 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every industrial application of robotic welding is justified by demonstrating measurable improvement in at least **two** of the KPIs above — typically **quality (defect-ppm)** and **productivity (units/shift)**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Industrial Robotic Welding Deployment

### Pillar 1 — Volume Justification (Breakeven Analysis Threshold)

> The **economic breakeven volume** (units above which robotics beats manual welding) is the cornerstone of any industrial adoption decision.

**Logic Steps (in plain English):**
- Manual welding has a **low fixed cost** (one welder + one torch) but **high variable cost** (per-piece labour, rework, scrap).
- Robotic welding has **high fixed cost** (robot, controller, positioner, fixture, safety enclosure) but **dramatically low variable cost** per piece.
- The **crossover point** is computed using the breakeven formula given in §2.2.

### Pillar 2 — Repeatability & Geometric Accuracy

- Industrial robotic welding thrives where **geometric tolerance bands are tight** (e.g., automotive sub-frame welds with **±0.5 mm** tolerance).
- The robot's **pose repeatability (RP)** is a hardware constant; the **path accuracy (PA)** depends on TCP calibration, payload, and dynamics.

### Pillar 3 — Throughput & Cycle Time Compression

- A robotic GMAW cell typically achieves **arc-on time ≥ 80 %**, whereas manual welding averages **30–45 %**.
- This single metric is the dominant driver of **3× to 5× productivity gain**.

### Pillar 4 — Process-Level Integration

- Industrial cells integrate **wire feed, gas shielding, water cooling, seam tracking, vision-based adaptive control, and arc-sensing** into a single closed-loop system.

### Pillar 5 — Human-Robot Collaboration Tier

- **Tier 1:** Caged industrial robot (no human access during operation).
- **Tier 2:** Power-and-force-limited collaborative robot (cobot) — emerging in low-hazard arc welding.
- **Tier 3:** Hand-guiding teach pendants for low-volume heavy fabrication.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Breakeven Quantity | $N_{be} = \dfrac{F_{r} - F_{m}}{V_{m} - V_{r}}$ | $N_{be}$ = breakeven units (pieces); $F_{r}, F_{m}$ = fixed cost of robot / manual (₹); $V_{r}, V_{m}$ = variable cost / piece (₹) | Decides whether robotic cell is justified economically for a given part volume |
| 2 | Deposition Rate | $R_{d} = \dfrac{I \times k}{1000 \times 60}$ | $R_{d}$ = deposition rate (g/s); $I$ = welding current (A); $k$ = mass coefficient (g/(A·min)) | Sets wire-feed parameters and throughput |
| 3 | Arc-On Time % | $\% \text{Arc} = \dfrac{T_{arc}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100$ | $T_{arc}$ = arcing time (s); $T_{cycle}$ = total cycle time (s) | Diagnostic KPI for cell efficiency |
| 4 | Productivity Gain | $G_{p} = \dfrac{N_{r}}{N_{m}} \times \dfrac{T_{m}}{T_{r}}$ | $N_{r}, N_{m}$ = units in robotic / manual cell; $T_{r}, T_{m}$ = cycle times (s) | Quantifies ROI of automation |
| 5 | Cost Per Weld Length | $C_{L} = \dfrac{C_{wire} + C_{gas} + C_{power} + C_{labour}}{L_{weld}}$ | $C_{L}$ = ₹/mm; $L_{weld}$ = weld length (mm) | Process-cost engineering and tendering |
| 6 | OEE | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability; $P$ = Performance; $Q$ = Quality (all fractions) | Industry-standard cell health index |
| 7 | Robot Reach Constraint | $r_{TCP} \le L_{1} + L_{2} + \ldots + L_{n}$ | $L_{i}$ = individual link lengths (mm) | Work-cell layout validation |
| 8 | Sealing-Current / Adaptive Gain | $K_{a} = \dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta z}$ | $\Delta I$ = current correction (A); $\Delta z$ = torch height error (mm) | Touch-sensing & arc-sensing control law |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Etiquette Note:** In the table above, all absolute-value and conditional bars (e.g., $\lvert \Delta z \rvert$) are written with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown-table parsing errors — a common pitfall in KTU digital answer submission.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** use **Formula 1 (Breakeven)** to decide robot deployment per BIW station; thresholds are typically **N₆ ≥ 25,000–60,000 units/year**.
- **Shipyards** use **Formula 3 (Arc-On %)** to justify investment in **twin-arc / tandem GMAW** systems, pushing arc-on to **>90 %**.
- **Pressure-vessel fabricators** use **Formula 5 (Cost/Weld)** for ISO 3834-compliant cost reporting and L1/L2 tendering.
- **Aerospace Tier-1 suppliers** use **Formula 8 (Adaptive Gain Kₐ)** to validate **PAW and LBW seam-tracking controllers** for turbine blade and fuselage panel welding.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Breakeven Quantity for Robotic vs Manual Welding Cell

This is the **most frequently asked numerical in KTU ESE Module 3**, so we derive it rigorously with every algebraic transition shown.

### Given
A fabricator is comparing:
- **Manual cell:** Fixed cost $F_{m} = 200{,}000$ ₹ (welder station, basic tools, fume hood); Variable cost $V_{m} = 250$ ₹/piece.
- **Robotic cell:** Fixed cost $F_{r} = 5{,}000{,}000$ ₹ (robot + controller + positioner + fixture + safety); Variable cost $V_{r} = 40$ ₹/piece (wire, gas, power, maintenance amortized).

### To Find
$N_{be}$ — the number of pieces at which the **Total Cost of robotic cell equals Total Cost of manual cell**.

### Step 1 — Write the Total Cost equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{m} &= F_{m} + V_{m} \cdot N \\
TC_{r} &= F_{r} + V_{r} \cdot N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $TC$ is the sum of one-time fixed cost plus a linearly-scaling variable cost over $N$ production units.

### Step 2 — Set the costs equal at the breakeven point

At $N = N_{be}$, the two cost lines cross:

$$
F_{m} + V_{m} \cdot N_{be} = F_{r} + V_{r} \cdot N_{be}
$$

**Logic:** This is the definition of breakeven — the manual cell and robotic cell are economically equivalent.

### Step 3 — Isolate $N_{be}$ by gathering like terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{m} \cdot N_{be} - V_{r} \cdot N_{be} &= F_{r} - F_{m} \\
N_{be} \cdot (V_{m} - V_{r}) &= F_{r} - F_{m} \\
N_{be} &= \dfrac{F_{r} - F_{m}}{V_{m} - V_{r}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $V_m > V_r$ always (manual labour is more expensive per piece), and $F_r > F_m$ always (robot is a large capital expense), so the numerator and denominator are both positive — giving a sensible, positive $N_{be}$.

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{be} &= \dfrac{5{,}000{,}000 - 200{,}000}{250 - 40} \\
&= \dfrac{4{,}800{,}000}{210} \\
&= 22{,}857.14 \text{ pieces}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Numerator captures the upfront capital premium; denominator captures the per-piece saving — their ratio is the volume at which the savings "pay back" the premium.

### Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion

$$
\boxed{N_{be} \approx 22{,}858 \text{ pieces}}
$$

For any production volume $N > 22{,}858$ pieces/year, the **robotic cell is the lower-cost option**. The fabricator should also verify the **Payback Period (PP)** in years:

$$
PP = \dfrac{F_{r} - F_{m}}{N \cdot (V_{m} - V_{r})}
$$

Assuming $N = 60{,}000$ pieces/year:

$$
PP = \dfrac{4{,}800{,}000}{60{,}000 \times 210} = \dfrac{4{,}800{,}000}{12{,}600{,}000} = 0.381 \text{ years} \approx 4.6 \text{ months}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pattern:** Always show **units in every step** and **write the conclusion in a complete sentence**. Marks are explicitly awarded for the *final boxed answer* and the *engineering interpretation* — not just arithmetic.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Productivity Gain in a BIW Robotic Spot-Welding Cell

> [!NOTE]
> Spot welding is the single most common industrial robotic welding application — typically **6,000–8,000 spots per vehicle body** in modern BIW.

### Given
- **Manual cell:** 1 welder, **40 spots/hr**, **2 shifts/day** × 8 hr.
- **Robotic cell:** 2 robots, **180 spots/hr/robot** × 2 robots, **2 shifts/day** × 8 hr, **arc-on time 88 %**.

### Step 1 — Compute daily output of the manual cell

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{m, day} &= 40 \times 1 \times 2 \times 8 = 640 \text{ spots/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Output = Rate × Robots × Shifts × Hours per shift.

### Step 2 — Compute daily output of the robotic cell

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{r, day} &= 180 \times 2 \times 2 \times 8 = 5{,}760 \text{ spots/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute productivity gain

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{p} &= \dfrac{N_{r, day}}{N_{m, day}} = \dfrac{5{,}760}{640} = 9.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A **9× productivity gain** confirms the textbook claim of "3× to 10× gain" in BIW, with the upper end achieved in dedicated, well-fixtured robotic cells.

### Step 4 — Compute gain normalized for manpower

Manual: 640 spots / 1 operator = **640 spots/man-day**.
Robotic: 5,760 spots / 1 supervisor + 1 loader = **2,880 spots/man-day**.

$$
G_{p, man} = \dfrac{2{,}880}{640} = 4.5
$$

**Logic:** Even after accounting for the human cell-loader, robotics delivers **4.5× output per worker per day** — the true labour productivity metric used by automotive OEMs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Industrial Application vs Process vs Robot Type

| Industrial Sector | Dominant Welding Process | Robot Type | Typical Work-Piece | Key Industrial Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Automotive BIW** | **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** | Heavy-payload articulated (≥ 200 kg) | Car body, doors, sub-frames | High spot-count, dimensional repeatability |
| **Automotive BIW (arc)** | GMAW, Laser-MAG Hybrid | Medium-payload articulated (10–50 kg) | Roof, floor pan, A/B/C pillars | Low heat-input, distortion control |
| **Heavy Earth-Moving Equipment** | SAW, FCAW, GMAW | Heavy-payload + 2-axis positioner | Excavator booms, dump-truck beds | Long fillet welds, high deposition |
| **Shipbuilding** | Tandem GMAW, SAW | Gantry / H-frame + column-and-boom | Hull panels, decks, bulkheads | Very long welds, harsh environment |
| **Aerospace** | LBW, PAW, GTAW, FSW | High-precision articulated (6-axis) | Engine casings, turbine blades, fuselage skins | Low distortion, exotic alloys, NDT compliance |
| **Pressure Vessels & Pipe Spools** | GTAW root + GMAW fill, SAW | Positioner-mounted articulated | Bends, headers, nozzles, reactors | Regulatory (ASME, EN 13480) weld traceability |
| **Railways & Rolling Stock** | GMAW, FSW | Long-reach articulated + rotary table | Bogie frames, carriage sidewalls | Long seams, fatigue-critical joints |
| **Nuclear & Defence** | GTAW, PAW, EBW | Clean-room articulated with traceable logs | Reactor shields, missile casings | Zero-defect, full digital weld records |
| **Construction & Structural Steel** | SAW, GMAW | Gantry / Cartesian | I-beams, girders, trusses | Repetitive fillet welds on standard sections |
| **Consumer Goods (Bicycles, Furniture)** | Laser, MIG | Compact articulated or cobot | Bicycle frames, tube furniture | Small batch, cosmetic weld beads |
